quote: "I knew if I looked back in 10 years and didn't do something related to it, I would probably regret that." — Eric Wu, Founder, NavigateAI

title: Uber’s product chief on hotels, robotaxis, and why the company doesn’t want to be ‘everything for everyone’
summary: Uber Chief Product Officer Sachin Kansal outlined the company's expanding ambitions in an interview with TechCrunch, revealing that AI is increasingly central to its product strategy — from an earner assistant that guides drivers to higher-demand areas, to a grocery cart assistant and voice-based ride booking. Kansal also disclosed that Uber has commercial relationships with generative AI companies, selling labeled data and audio transcription work performed by its driver network when they are off-trip. The company's new AV Labs unit is deploying hundreds of sensor-equipped vehicles to gather large-scale driving data, which Kansal framed as both a service to autonomous vehicle partners and a source of operational leverage. Kansal said a fully agentic Uber — where users delegate entire trip planning to an AI — is a direction the company is heading, though he declined to set a timeline.
quote: "In terms of our general product strategy, we're not trying to be everything to everyone." — Sachin Kansal, Chief Product Officer, Uber

title: Video-generation startup PixVerse raises $439M, valuation soars past $2B
summary: Singapore-based video-generation startup PixVerse has closed a Series C extension totaling $439 million, pushing its valuation past $2 billion. The company, founded in 2023, offers consumer and professional video-generation models as well as world models for game development, and claims over 150 million registered users. Investors in the extension round include Alibaba, Mirae Asset, and BlueFocus, among others. PixVerse plans to use the funding to expand enterprise sales globally, hire researchers, and launch new versions of its video and world models later this year.
quote: "We think the key difference is not in data, but how you label it, because data is available everywhere." — Jaden Xie, Co-founder, PixVerse

title: Siri AI is already changing how I use my iPhone
summary: Apple's revamped Siri AI, launched as an opt-in beta within iOS 27's first public beta in July 2026, represents a meaningful shift in how users interact with their iPhones — moving from app-first to intent-first interactions. The new Siri can pull context from emails, webpages, and on-screen content to complete tasks, and early testing shows it handling complex requests like parsing emails to populate calendar events. However, full capability depends heavily on third-party developer adoption, which cannot happen until iOS 27's full release in fall 2026, leaving the beta limited largely to Apple's own apps. The competitive stakes are significant: whether major players like Google integrate Siri AI support into their apps — potentially at the cost of their own engagement and ad revenue — will determine how broadly useful the feature becomes.
quote: "The conceptual challenge for developers is creating comprehensive support for every screen and function within an app." — Matthew Cassinelli, former Workflow employee

title: Apple sues OpenAI after ex-engineer allegedly used bug to steal trade secrets
summary: Apple has filed a lawsuit against OpenAI alleging a broad conspiracy to steal trade secrets, triggered by the discovery that a former Apple engineer, Chang Liu, exploited an authentication bug to access Apple's confidential servers for weeks after joining OpenAI in January 2026. Liu allegedly downloaded dozens of files covering unreleased products, circuit board designs, and proprietary technical specifications. Apple further alleges that OpenAI's chief hardware officer, Tang Yew Tan — a 24-year Apple veteran — orchestrated a systematic poaching and intelligence-gathering operation involving more than 400 former Apple employees, including coaching departing staff on evading security measures. The lawsuit seeks injunctions blocking OpenAI from using the stolen information, and signals a major legal and competitive confrontation between two of the most powerful players in consumer technology and AI hardware.
quote: "I am not afraid of Apple, but I have tremendous respect for them." — Sam Altman, CEO, OpenAI

title: The 6 wildest claims in Apple’s lawsuit against OpenAI
summary: Apple has filed a 41-page lawsuit against OpenAI, accusing the AI company of orchestrating a systematic campaign to steal trade secrets as it develops its first AI hardware device. The complaint alleges that former Apple employees, allegedly coordinating with OpenAI's hardware head, downloaded confidential files, exploited an authentication vulnerability to access Apple's cloud storage after leaving the company, and brought hardware components to job interviews for "show and tell" sessions. OpenAI is also accused of coaching incoming hires on how to evade Apple's security offboarding procedures, and of deceiving an Apple manufacturing partner into performing a proprietary metal-finishing technique without authorization. OpenAI denied the allegations, stating it has "no interest in other companies' trade secrets."

title: Anthropic starts localizing Claude pricing for India, its biggest market after the US
summary: Anthropic has begun rolling out Indian rupee pricing for Claude subscriptions in India, its second-largest market accounting for 5.8% of global Claude usage. Claude Pro is listed at ₹2,000 per month (about $21) annually, with higher tiers priced proportionally above U.S. rates, reflecting included local taxes. The move follows Anthropic's broader India expansion, including opening a Bengaluru office in February 2026 and appointing a country head. However, Anthropic has yet to support UPI payments, putting it behind OpenAI, which launched rupee pricing with UPI support in August 2025.

title: Waze adds new AI-powered features and customization updates
summary: Waze is rolling out a series of AI-driven updates to its navigation app, including Gemini-powered conversational search, personalized route suggestions based on user history and city traffic patterns, and the ability to report map updates through natural speech. A new Motorcycle mode uses AI to tailor routes and hazard alerts for two-wheelers, launching initially in seven countries. The updates also include a "less chatty" mode that reduces voice prompt frequency for drivers who prefer fewer interruptions. The moves reflect Google's broader strategy to integrate its Gemini AI assistant across its product portfolio as Waze competes with rivals like Apple Maps.

title: What Canadian tech startups should know about AI in the workplace
summary: With Canada's proposed Artificial Intelligence and Data Act having died last year, Canadian tech startups are operating in a legal vacuum on workplace AI, according to Toronto-based employment lawyer Monty Verlint of Littler. Existing human rights and privacy laws still apply — and vary by province — meaning companies face real legal exposure even without AI-specific legislation. Verlint advises startups to implement clear policies governing AI use, test systems for bias, train employees, and be transparent with workers and candidates, all to avoid becoming an early test case in what he describes as a "Wild West" environment. His U.S.-based colleague Niloy Ray adds that workers retain the same rights against discrimination and improper data collection that they have always had.

title: Silicon Valley hits Calgary Stampede
summary: Meta announced it will build Canada's largest data centre in Alberta, while Anthropic highlighted Alberta's AI adoption as a "case study" for other governments, marking tangible results from years of Canadian province courting major US tech firms. The announcements came during Calgary Stampede week, where executives from Microsoft, Google, and Amazon gathered with local founders and government officials. Calgary has added more than 900 tech companies since 2021, bringing its total to more than 1,200, aided by Amazon Web Services' 2021 commitment to build a cloud computing hub in the area. However, Alberta faces domestic uncertainty, with a Calgary Chamber of Commerce report warning that the province's separatist movement could trigger a GDP hit and business exodus if it succeeds.

title: Waze is getting a bunch of new AI-powered features
summary: Waze is integrating Google's Gemini AI assistant into its driving app with four new updates, two of which are explicitly AI-powered. The Gemini-backed features include an enhanced conversational reporting tool for flagging traffic incidents and map changes, and a new Destination Search that lets drivers find nearby locations — such as open coffee shops or low-price gas stations — using natural voice commands. Non-AI additions include a "less chatty" voice prompt mode, a Motorcycle Mode with two-wheeled routing, and personalized route suggestions based on past trips. The updates signal a gradual AI integration into Waze, even as Google has moved more aggressively to add AI capabilities to its flagship Google Maps product.

title: Lorde says Ray-Ban Meta AI glasses are ‘not sexy’
summary: Lorde publicly criticized AI smartglasses during her performance at the Real Cool Festival in Madrid on July 10, 2026, calling them "fucked up" and urging fans not to buy them. While she did not name a specific brand, her remarks were widely seen as directed at Ray-Ban Meta AI glasses, a festival sponsor. The comments arrive as Meta faces growing public backlash over its smart glasses and is reportedly planning a new "super sensing" version that records continuously. Adding notable contrast, Blackpink's Jennie — a Ray-Ban Meta AI ambassador — performed immediately after Lorde at the same festival.
quote: "For the record, fuck the glasses. Don't get the glasses. Not sexy." — Lorde, Performer

title: Apple’s failed self-driving car program left a legacy of powerful AI chips
summary: Apple's abandoned self-driving car project inadvertently laid the groundwork for the Neural Engine, now the backbone of the company's on-device AI processing, according to Bloomberg's Mark Gurman. The Neural Engine debuted with the iPhone X's A11 Bionic chip and has since become central to Apple's AI hardware strategy across its M-series chips for desktops and servers. Apple is now skipping the Pro, Max, and Ultra variants of its upcoming M6 chip and accelerating development of the M7, expected in the first half of 2027, which will feature significant Neural Engine upgrades. The M7 Ultra is anticipated to power a new Apple server product supporting up to 1.5TB of RAM, signaling Apple's push to compete in AI infrastructure.

title: The fight against AI data centers is just beginning
summary: Community opposition to AI data center construction is intensifying across the United States, with protesters blocking or delaying at least 75 projects valued at $130 billion in just the first quarter of 2026, according to Data Center Watch. The number of active opposition groups more than doubled — from 396 at end of 2025 to 833 by end of Q1 2026 — as residents raise concerns about rising energy costs, water quality, noise, and greenhouse gas emissions. The pushback has already forced tangible retreats: QTS dropped a $12 billion campus in Wisconsin, a Delaware City project was blocked under the state's Coastal Zone Act, and Kevin O'Leary was pressured to downsize his proposed 40,000-acre Utah campus. At the federal level, lawmakers from both parties are introducing legislation to protect ratepayers, while President Trump's executive order to fast-track data center construction faces growing political resistance ahead of midterm elections.
quote: "The number of active opposition groups more than doubled from 396 at the end of 2025 to 833 by the end of Q1 2026, now spanning 49 states." — Data Center Watch, Research Project

title: OpenAI bets on families as ChatGPT goes deeper into households
summary: OpenAI is hiring a dedicated product manager to build experiences for families, caregivers, and older adults, signaling a strategic shift from treating ChatGPT as an individual productivity tool to designing it for entire households. The move is backed by demographic data: the share of ChatGPT users aged 35 and older rose to 31% globally in Q2 2026 from 26% a year earlier, and nearly one in four U.S. smartphone users who are parents used the app during the quarter, up from 16% a year prior. The shift raises significant trust and safety challenges, particularly for children and teenagers, as new research from the Family Online Safety Institute found parents consistently underestimate how often their kids use generative AI. Analysts expect the household focus to drive features such as family plans, child profiles, parental controls, and shared household memory across the AI industry.
quote: "I see this as safety by redesign." — Stephen Balkam, Chief Executive, Family Online Safety Institute

title: Meta removes controversial AI feature on Instagram after backlash
summary: Meta has removed a feature from its new Muse Image AI generator that allowed users to generate images by referencing public Instagram accounts, after swift backlash from users and talent agencies including CAA. The feature, launched earlier this week as part of Meta Superintelligence Labs' new AI image tools, did not notify users when their photos were used as references. Meta acknowledged in a blog post that the feature "missed the mark" and confirmed it is no longer available. The episode highlights the persistent risk of AI image tools being misused to generate non-consensual imagery, particularly of public figures.
quote: "We've heard the feedback that this feature missed the mark, so it's no longer available." — Meta, Company Statement

title: Meta turns off the Instagram feature that let users make AI deepfakes of public accounts
summary: Meta has disabled a newly announced feature that allowed users to generate AI images by tagging any public Instagram account, using that account's content without the owner's permission. The feature, part of Meta's new Muse Image AI model rollout, drew immediate backlash from safety advocates and industry groups who warned it could facilitate sextortion and other scams. Meta had offered an opt-out through settings, but critics argued placing the burden on individuals to protect themselves was unacceptable. The Screen Actors Guild urged members to opt out before Meta ultimately reversed course and removed the feature entirely.
quote: "Pursuing high-risk design & then putting the onus on individuals to jump through hoops to opt out is unacceptable." — Haley McNamara, Executive Director and Chief Strategy Officer, National Center on Sexual Exploitation

title: Apple sues OpenAI for allegedly stealing hardware secrets
summary: Apple has filed a lawsuit against OpenAI, IO Products (Jony Ive's hardware startup acquired by OpenAI in 2025), and two named former Apple employees, alleging a systematic pattern of trade secret theft to advance OpenAI's hardware ambitions. The suit claims former employees accessed Apple systems after leaving, downloaded confidential files on unreleased products and supply chain processes, and coached colleagues on evading Apple's security team. Apple also alleges OpenAI actively solicited confidential materials during job interviews and leveraged Apple's supplier network to perform proprietary manufacturing processes for OpenAI's benefit. With OpenAI's first hardware product expected in 2026 and more than 400 former Apple staffers now at OpenAI, the lawsuit represents a major legal escalation between two of the most prominent players in consumer AI hardware.
quote: "This is a systematic effort to acquire, retain, and use Apple's trade secrets to help OpenAI try to replicate the secret technologies, business processes, and supply chain innovations that took Apple decades to build in its consumer hardware business." — Apple, Lawsuit Filing

title: Apple sues OpenAI over alleged trade secret theft
summary: Apple has filed a lawsuit against OpenAI in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California, alleging trade secret theft and breach of contract tied to a pattern of misconduct directed by OpenAI's senior leadership. The complaint centers on Chief Hardware Officer Tang Tan, a 24-year Apple veteran, who is accused of using Apple's confidential project code names during recruiting, coaching departing Apple employees on evading security procedures, and soliciting details about unannounced products. The lawsuit also names former Apple engineer Chang Liu, who allegedly failed to return an Apple laptop and used it to download confidential technical documents after joining OpenAI. The case carries significant strategic weight as OpenAI is developing its first hardware product — widely rumored to be an AI-driven smartphone — that would directly compete with Apple's core iPhone business.
quote: "At Apple, our teams are constantly developing breakthrough technologies to create the best products and services in the world, and protecting their work and intellectual property is something we take very seriously." — Apple, Company Statement

title: OpenAI introduces ChatGPT Work, a cloud-based AI agent that manages tasks across email, Slack and calendars
summary: OpenAI has launched ChatGPT Work, an autonomous AI agent embedded in ChatGPT that can execute complex, multi-step tasks across email, calendars, Slack, GitHub, and other workplace tools via MCP-based plugins. Powered by GPT-5.6 and running on a persistent cloud-based virtual machine, the product is designed to produce finished documents, spreadsheets, presentations, and websites — not just conversational replies. The launch repositions ChatGPT from a chatbot into a workplace execution platform and places OpenAI in direct competition with Anthropic's Claude Cowork and Microsoft's Copilot Cowork. ChatGPT Work is rolling out immediately to Pro, Enterprise, and Edu users, with Plus and Business access to follow within days, and OpenAI is emphasizing broad accessibility as a strategic differentiator given its 900 million weekly active users and 50 million paying subscribers.
quote: "Things that we would have spent three months doing, we can now spend a week doing — and do much more, and make a much better product." — Ty Geri, Product Manager, OpenAI

title: Wall Street is debating the AI buildout. Enterprises just answered: 86% say their GPUs run at half capacity or less
summary: A VentureBeat Research survey of 573 technical leaders finds that 86% of enterprises running their own GPUs report utilization of 50% or less, offering direct market evidence in the Wall Street debate over whether AI infrastructure is overbuilt. Only 44% of these companies rigorously track what their AI compute actually costs and returns, yet 45% are evaluating AI-specialized clouds and roughly a third are considering non-Nvidia accelerators as a hedge. The survey also finds that 71% of deployed "agents" are effectively single-prompt chatbots rather than true multi-step autonomous systems, suggesting industry adoption claims significantly outpace reality. Additional findings show 69% of companies allow credential sharing across their agent fleets — and those companies experienced security incidents at a rate of 63.5%, versus 40.9% for companies with properly scoped agent identities.

title: Open source AI matters more than ever, according to Hugging Face’s Clem Delangue
summary: Hugging Face CEO Clem Delangue argues that open source AI is experiencing a boom, with the platform now used by roughly half the Fortune 500 as companies increasingly migrate away from costly proprietary frontier APIs as they scale. Delangue identifies a recurring pattern: organizations begin with closed-source models but shift to open alternatives once usage costs become prohibitive. Speaking on TechCrunch's Equity podcast, he raised concerns that without strong open source alternatives, a small number of large technology companies could end up controlling the AI landscape. The conversation was framed in part around Anthropic's halted Fable release, which Delangue used to illustrate the stakes in the open versus closed source debate.

title: Dubai’s Ahoy to hire 200 in Montréal as it relocates R&D hub
summary: Dubai-based physical AI startup Ahoy is relocating its primary research and development hub to Montréal, Canada, and plans to directly hire 200 people in research, engineering, and go-to-market roles there. The company is deepening its partnership with Canadian AI research institute Mila and expects the expansion to generate an additional 300 jobs across the broader ecosystem through third-party implementation and consulting firms. Ahoy, which deploys AI in airports, utilities, and hospitals across the Middle East and North America, claims over $100 million USD in annual recurring revenue. The move accompanies a previously announced acquisition of Montréal-based business process automation startup Wrk in a nine-figure deal that has not yet closed.

title: Enterprise AI is entering an evaluation gap: Agents are gaining autonomy faster than companies can verify them
summary: A June 2026 VB Pulse survey of 157 enterprise respondents finds that half of companies have deployed an AI agent or LLM feature that passed internal evaluations but still caused a customer-facing failure, with one in four experiencing this more than once. Despite this, 66% of respondents already permit some production deployment without human review or plan to within 12 months, yet only 5% fully trust the automated evaluations underpinning those release decisions. The survey identifies the core problem as poor alignment between evaluation scores and real-world outcomes, meaning passing a test does not reliably predict how an agent will behave in production. Larger enterprises are moving toward zero-human deployment fastest while also reporting higher rates of customer-facing failures, underscoring that removing human oversight without stronger assurance mechanisms converts uncertainty into automated risk.

title: SK Hynix raises $26.5B in the biggest foreign IPO in US history, is urged to build new US fabs
summary: SK Hynix raised $26.5 billion in its U.S. market debut on Nasdaq, making it the largest-ever U.S. IPO by a non-American company, surpassing Alibaba's $25 billion listing in 2014. The South Korean memory chipmaker, a primary supplier of high-bandwidth memory (HBM) to Nvidia for AI GPUs, saw its shares open 14% above the IPO price amid demand reportedly more than seven times the available shares. Proceeds will fund a new fab and packaging facility in South Korea along with EUV scanners to address memory shortages driven by AI demand. U.S. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick is pressing both SK Hynix and Samsung to build new factories on American soil, while Micron announced a $250 billion U.S. manufacturing investment commitment.

title: Disable auto-play and infinite scroll or risk massive fines, EU tells Meta
summary: The European Commission has issued preliminary findings that Meta's Facebook and Instagram features — including auto-play, infinite scroll, and personalized recommendations — are addictive and pose risks to users' physical and mental wellbeing, including minors. Meta faces fines of up to 6 percent of its global annual turnover if it fails to comply with the EU's Digital Services Act, and separately faces a U.S. trial beginning in August where 29 states seek up to $1.4 trillion in penalties over platform addiction claims. The EU recommended Meta disable addictive features by default and implement effective screen-time breaks, while dismissing Meta's existing teen protections as insufficient. The pressure compounds Meta's financial strain as it simultaneously spends between $125 billion and $145 billion on AI data center infrastructure and faces backlash over its new Muse AI model, which automatically opted most users into sharing their content for AI training.
quote: "The next step is either that Meta changes its design or a non-compliance decision will follow." — Henna Virkkunen, EU's tech chief

title: Hugging Face’s CEO on why companies are done renting their AI
summary: Hugging Face CEO Clem Delangue says open source AI is booming, with the platform — now used by roughly half the Fortune 500 — seeing a consistent pattern: companies start on frontier APIs but migrate to open source models as costs scale. Delangue argues the open versus closed source debate carries significant stakes, particularly in the wake of Anthropic's halted Fable release. He also expressed concern that a small number of large companies could end up controlling the broader AI landscape. The comments came in an interview on TechCrunch's Equity podcast.

title: Instagram’s Adam Mosseri: If you don’t like AI, ‘then you shouldn’t have it in your feed’
summary: Instagram head Adam Mosseri said the platform should not filter out AI-generated content entirely, but should allow users to curate their feeds to avoid it if they choose. Speaking on Lenny Rachitsky's podcast, Mosseri acknowledged that detecting AI content is increasingly difficult as models improve, and suggested it may become more practical to label verified "camera-captured" real media instead. He noted that Instagram needs to address spammy AI content while still embracing the technology, including Meta's new AI image generator, Muse Spark, which allows users to place other people into AI-generated images by tagging them. Critics, including the National Center on Sexual Exploitation, have warned the feature creates serious risks of harassment, identity fraud, and sexual abuse.
quote: "I don't think we should filter out AI content. I think we should let you know if content is AI content or not." — Adam Mosseri, Head of Instagram

title: Sunrun plans to pay homeowners to host AI compute nodes in their homes
summary: Solar and home battery company Sunrun is launching a pilot program to place small AI compute nodes inside customers' homes, creating what it calls a "nationwide compute network" that would sell distributed computing power to enterprise AI buyers. The program targets Sunrun's 1.1 million existing solar and battery storage customers, who would be compensated for hosting the hardware. The approach is positioned as an alternative to traditional large data centers, which face growing public opposition — a May survey found over 70 percent of Americans oppose new data center construction in their area. Sunrun says it completed a successful proof of concept and expects to assess pilot results over the coming months before any broader rollout.

title: OpenAI says GPT 5.6 is the ‘preferred model’ for Microsoft Copilot 365 amid breakup chatter
summary: OpenAI announced that GPT-5.6 will be the preferred model powering Microsoft's 365 Copilot, supporting users across Word, Excel, PowerPoint, and Cowork. The announcement came amid reports from Bloomberg that Microsoft had been substituting some OpenAI software with its own in-house MAI models to reduce costs, raising questions about the durability of the two companies' partnership. OpenAI's move appears designed to counter narratives of a growing rift, though the "preferred model" designation does not clearly negate the earlier reporting that Microsoft is increasingly relying on its own models. The strategic ambiguity leaves open whether the two companies are genuinely deepening their collaboration or managing a gradual decoupling.
quote: "Our partnership with Microsoft has always been about bringing the benefits of advanced AI to more individuals and organizations, and we're excited to continue building on that shared commitment." — OpenAI, Company Statement

title: Microsoft’s carbon emissions went up 25 percent last year
summary: Microsoft's carbon emissions rose 25 percent in 2025 to 34 million metric tons, according to the company's 2026 sustainability report, driven primarily by datacenter expansion to support AI infrastructure. The report acknowledges that "sustainability solutions are not scaling fast enough to meet demand" from AI infrastructure growth. The increase puts Microsoft further from its 2030 carbon-negative goal, a target it has struggled to meet for several years. Google reported a similar 25 percent spike in supply chain emissions, while Amazon reported a 16 percent increase, reflecting an industry-wide challenge tied to AI data center growth.
quote: "While AI infrastructure is driving demand for energy, water, land, and materials, sustainability solutions are not scaling fast enough to meet demand." — Microsoft, 2026 Sustainability Report

title: Fidji Simo steps down from OpenAI’s no. 2 role
summary: Fidji Simo, OpenAI's CEO of Applications and the company's second-ranking executive, is stepping down from her full-time role due to an ongoing medical condition, transitioning instead to a part-time advisory capacity. Simo joined OpenAI in May 2025 to consolidate business and product operations under a newly created role, with COO Brad Lightcap, CFO Sarah Friar, and CPO Kevin Weil all reporting to her — though Weil has since departed as well. Her exit creates a significant leadership vacuum for Sam Altman as OpenAI eyes a potential IPO, particularly given that Simo had been seen as a likely candidate for expanded responsibilities once the company went public. The departure comes as OpenAI faces competitive pressure from Anthropic in coding tools and a cooldown in ChatGPT's growth, while the company simultaneously launched its new GPT-5.6 model family and a new agent product, ChatGPT Work.

title: Fidji Simo steps down from leading OpenAI’s AGI work due to illness
summary: Fidji Simo is departing her full-time role as OpenAI's AGI chief, transitioning to a part-time advisory position after a severe exacerbation of a chronic neuroimmune illness made a longer-than-expected recovery necessary. Simo had only recently taken on the AGI chief title before going on medical leave in April 2026, and her departure adds to a series of C-suite changes at OpenAI, including COO Brad Lightcap stepping back and CMO Kate Rouch also stepping down for health reasons. In her absence, President Greg Brockman assumed leadership of product strategy and "scaling," overseeing a May reorganization that merged ChatGPT and Codex into a unified agentic platform. The leadership churn at one of the world's most prominent AI companies signals ongoing organizational instability as OpenAI pursues its AGI ambitions.
quote: "It has been a jarring experience to spend my days helping build the future while simultaneously navigating a disabling disease that still has no cure." — Fidji Simo, AGI Chief (departing), OpenAI

title: OpenAI launches its new family of models with GPT-5.6
summary: OpenAI has launched GPT-5.6, a new family of AI models available in three tiers — Sol (flagship), Terra (mid-range), and Luna (budget) — with pricing ranging from $1 to $5 per million input tokens. The company is positioning Sol as its best coding model, claiming it scores 80 on the Artificial Analysis Coding Agent Index, 2.8 points above Anthropic's recently released Fable 5, while costing roughly one-third less. OpenAI also launched ChatGPT Work, an enterprise workplace tool for drafting documents, spreadsheets, and presentations. The release is a direct competitive strike at Anthropic, which has built a strong enterprise following, and arrives alongside similar model releases from SpaceXAI and Meta in the same week.
quote: "Sol is 54% more token efficient when it comes to AI coding tasks." — Sam Altman, CEO

title: An AI agent startup just let its agent run its $100M fundraise
summary: Lyzr, a Jersey City-based enterprise AI agent startup, used its own AI agent system — called SivaClaw — to manage its $100 million Series B fundraise at a roughly $500 million valuation. The agent fielded questions from over 130 investors, drafted investment memos, and tracked investor engagement with pitch materials, generating $400 million in total interest from Silicon Valley, Middle Eastern, and financial-sector investors. Notably, no founder traveled to meet investors in person, bypassing the traditional Sand Hill Road circuit entirely. The raise serves as both a product demonstration and a signal of how much capital is currently chasing AI bets, with founders reporting minimal effort required to secure nine-figure rounds.

title: OpenAI is shutting down Atlas, but its AI browser ambitions are still growing
summary: OpenAI is discontinuing Atlas, its AI-powered browser launched in October 2025, and redistributing its agentic browsing features into ChatGPT's desktop app and a new Google Chrome extension. The Chrome extension gives ChatGPT access to page context, enabling users to ask questions, summarize content, and initiate tasks directly from the browser — a direct competitor to Google's Gemini Side Panel. The desktop app is also being enhanced with a built-in browser and a separate cloud browser running on OpenAI's servers for completing tasks autonomously. The move signals that OpenAI views the browser as a feature layer rather than a standalone destination, consolidating its agent capabilities into platforms where users already spend time.

title: Elon Musk praises Mythos/Fable, promises not to ‘cut off’ Anthropic
summary: Elon Musk publicly praised Anthropic in July 2026, calling it "obviously currently the leader in AI" and pledging not to cut off its infrastructure access, after users raised concerns about SpaceX's leverage over a rival. The comments come as Anthropic is one of SpaceX's largest customers, having signed a $40 billion deal to rent the full output of xAI's Colossus 1 data center through May 2029 at $1.25 billion per month. Google also signed a separate deal for SpaceX infrastructure worth $920 million per month through June 2029. The arrangement gives SpaceX substantial revenue and potential operational visibility into how Anthropic runs its rapidly growing AI business, raising questions about competitive risk even as both sides publicly emphasize the partnership's benefits.
quote: "They are obviously currently the leader in AI. No company has released a model as good as Mythos/Fable and they will undoubtedly have Mythos 2 ready soon." — Elon Musk, CEO, SpaceX/xAI

title: Can AI answer the $3 trillion question?
summary: Sequoia partner David Cahn calculates that the AI industry must generate $3 trillion in revenue to justify cumulative infrastructure spending of $1.5 trillion in 2026 alone — a figure he notes is likely an underestimate given rising memory and chip costs. Against that backdrop, Apollo chief economist Torsten Slok warns that hyperscalers — Google, Meta, Microsoft, and Amazon — are banking on massive free-cash-flow acceleration by 2028, but face real risks from the shift toward cheaper open-weight models and falling token prices. OpenAI's latest model is reportedly 54% more token-efficient on coding tasks, which benefits users but threatens revenue for companies monetizing token volume. Slok warns that if hyperscalers miss their cash-flow targets, the fallout could extend beyond the tech sector, potentially tipping the broader economy into recession and pushing the S&P 500 into a correction.

title: OpenAI launches ChatGPT Work, a long-running agentic tool for workplace tasks
summary: OpenAI has launched ChatGPT Work, a rebranded and expanded version of its Codex tool designed to autonomously handle complex workplace tasks for hours at a time, including automating entire workflows from research to finished deliverables. The product integrates with major workplace platforms such as Slack, Microsoft Teams, Google Drive, and SharePoint, and includes a scheduled-tasks feature for recurring or event-triggered automation. The existing ChatGPT desktop app is being rebranded as "ChatGPT Classic," while the dedicated Atlas web browser is being sunset less than nine months after launch. The rollout coincides with the release of GPT-5.6, a new model priced at $5 per million input tokens and $30 per million output tokens, positioned as offering stronger performance per dollar for demanding tasks.

title: Shared API keys expose AI agents at 69% of enterprises, new VentureBeat research finds
summary: VentureBeat's June 2026 Pulse Research of 107 enterprises finds that 69% run AI agents with credential sharing in at least one part of their deployments, meaning a single compromised agent can inherit the permissions of every workflow tied to a shared API key. More than half of respondents (54%) have already experienced an agent security incident or near-miss, yet only 30% sandbox their highest-risk agents — the control that limits damage when other defenses fail. The gap between incident rates and containment widens sharply at larger organizations, where companies above 5,000 employees show a 60-point spread between incident frequency and sandbox adoption. The findings help explain more than $22 billion in acquisitions by Palo Alto Networks, CrowdStrike, and Cisco over the past year, all targeting the identity and runtime authorization layer that most enterprises have not yet secured.
quote: "Enterprises believe they've 'approved' AI vendors, but what they've actually approved is an interface, not the underlying system." — Merritt Baer, Chief Security Officer at Enkrypt AI

title: The ChatGPT browser is already dead
summary: OpenAI is shutting down ChatGPT Atlas, its agentic browser launched in October 2025, with deprecation targeted for August 9, 2026 — less than a year after launch. The move is part of OpenAI's broader effort to consolidate products and reduce "side quests," with Atlas's capabilities folded into the new ChatGPT Work desktop superapp, which includes an updated browser and cloud browsing for work mode. The shutdown follows OpenAI's March plans to combine ChatGPT, Codex, and Atlas into a unified desktop application. OpenAI has also recently shut down the Sora video generation app and paused plans for a ChatGPT "adult mode," signaling a strategic tightening of its product portfolio.
quote: "All these capabilities were built on what we learned from Atlas users who took a leap of faith on a new browser." — James Sun, OpenAI

title: Google will now tell you if an ad was made with AI
summary: Google is adding a "created or edited with AI" label to ads appearing on Google Search, Google Discover, and YouTube, visible through the "My Ad Center" panel accessed via the info button on any ad. The label will be applied automatically to ads produced with Google's own generative AI advertising tools, while ads created using third-party AI tools will require manual disclosure by advertisers. In some regions, the label may appear directly on the ad itself. The move follows similar steps by Meta, which has its own "AI info" label on ads, and builds on Google's earlier AI disclosure efforts including SynthID and C2PA content labels for deepfake detection.

title: Meta enters the crowded AI coding battle with Muse Spark 1.1
summary: Meta launched Muse Spark 1.1 on July 9, 2026, a multimodal AI model targeting agentic coding tasks such as bug fixing, large code migrations, and enterprise workflow automation — entering a market where Anthropic and OpenAI already have established products. The model is priced at $1.25 per million input tokens and $4.25 per million output tokens, positioning it competitively against Anthropic's Claude Haiku 4.5 and OpenAI's GPT-5.6 Luna. The release was significant enough to prompt CEO Mark Zuckerberg to post on X for the first time in three years, signaling Meta's seriousness about competing in the agentic AI space. The launch coincided with a broader wave of AI model releases this week, including a new Grok version from SpaceXAI and OpenAI's GPT-5.6 family, underscoring the intensifying competition across the industry.

title: Alberta to invest $50 million into Amii over five years
summary: Alberta's provincial government is investing $50 million in the Alberta Machine Intelligence Institute (Amii) over five years, drawing contributions from five ministries. The funding, announced by Premier Danielle Smith at Platform Calgary, will focus on applying AI research to improve public services, healthcare, and workforce preparation. This investment is roughly equal to all provincial funding Amii has received over the previous 24 years combined, signaling a significant escalation in Alberta's commitment to AI. Amii is one of Canada's three leading AI research institutes and counts Turing Award-winner Richard Sutton among its more than 500 researchers.
quote: "Alberta began investing in AI research more than two decades ago. That early bet paid off." — Nate Glubish, Minister of Technology and Innovation, Alberta

title: New York Times says OpenAI hid evidence in ChatGPT copyright trial
summary: The New York Times and The Daily News have accused OpenAI of concealing evidence in their ongoing copyright lawsuit, alleging the company lied about its ability to search training data and ChatGPT chat logs for their copyrighted content. A court-ordered deposition in April allegedly revealed that OpenAI had already conducted internal searches of its training corpus and maintained a database of roughly 78 million de-identified ChatGPT conversations to assess its own infringement exposure — even as it told courts such searches were technically burdensome. The plaintiffs further allege OpenAI deleted billions of ChatGPT outputs after the suit was filed, in violation of a court preservation order, and submitted a chat log sample so heavily redacted the court deemed it "unusable." The outlets are now asking the judge to sanction OpenAI, bar it from using the contested chat log sample as evidence, and compel it to pay the plaintiffs' legal fees.
quote: "If OpenAI genuinely believed that copying our clients' journalism was fair and legal, it wouldn't have hid the truth about having done it." — Ian B. Crosby, Lead Counsel for the Plaintiffs

title: OpenAI may have made a fatal misstep in copyright fight with news orgs
summary: News organizations led by The New York Times have filed a sanctions motion accusing OpenAI of deliberately concealing evidence for over two years in their ongoing copyright lawsuit, including hiding the existence of large pre-searched ChatGPT log samples of 10 million and 78 million conversations. A re-deposed OpenAI privacy engineer reportedly revealed that OpenAI had already conducted searches of its own output logs — including for NYT content — while simultaneously telling the court such searches were technically infeasible and prohibitively burdensome. OpenAI is also accused of deleting billions of logs subject to a court preservation order and making 19 billion redactions to a smaller sample it did provide, rendering it "unusable." If sanctions are granted, OpenAI could be barred from using that sample as evidence and face jury instructions highlighting its deletion of logs — a potentially decisive blow to its fair use defense, which hinges on disproving market substitution.
quote: "For over two years, OpenAI lied to The Times, The Daily News Plaintiffs, the public, and the court." — Ian Crosby, NYT Lead Counsel

title: Google will now disclose which ads are made with AI
summary: Google is rolling out a new "How this ad was made" feature within its My Ad Center panel, allowing users globally to see when an ad was created or edited using AI. The disclosure will be automatically applied when advertisers use Google's own generative AI advertising tools, but advertisers using external AI tools will need to self-report that fact — Google will not independently verify compliance. The feature will be accessible via the three-dot menu or info icon on ads appearing across Google Search, YouTube, and Google Discover. The move extends AI disclosure requirements beyond election ads, where they were previously mandatory, and reflects growing pressure on platforms to inform consumers when AI-generated imagery is used in commercial contexts.

title: Paris-based AI voice startup Gradium raises $100M seed, backed by Nvidia
summary: Gradium, a Paris-based voice AI startup spun out of French AI lab Kyutai, has raised a total of $100 million in seed funding after reopening its round to new investors including Nvidia. The company specializes in ultra-low-latency audio models that enable near-instant AI voice responses, targeting a competitive market that includes ElevenLabs (valued at $11 billion) and Google's Gemini. Gradium plans to use the funds to open a Bay Area office to compete for AI talent and proximity to leading AI companies. Since launching out of stealth in December 2025 with an initial $70 million, the startup has already landed notable customers including French automaker Renault.

title: How did the government decide OpenAI’s frontier model was safe to release?
summary: OpenAI has released Sol, its latest advanced large language model, under a government approval process that remains opaque even to industry insiders. The Trump administration has yet to establish a formal regulatory framework for frontier AI models 18 months in, with an executive order published last month setting a roadmap but leaving critical specifics unfilled. Approvals appear to have been granted through informal conversations between OpenAI CEO Sam Altman and senior officials including Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick and Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, with no clear criteria or designated expert evaluators. Critics warn the process is driven by personal connections and financial ties to the administration rather than technical expertise, raising concerns about who truly holds gatekeeping power over potentially dangerous AI systems.
quote: "It's existentially a problem, it's about who has the power to make decisions — who gatekeeps and decides on permissions?" — Andy Konwinski, Co-founder, Databricks/Perplexity/Laude Institute

title: Meta launches AI image feature that can use public Instagram photos without user consent
summary: Meta launched Muse Image on July 9, 2026, an AI image-generation tool embedded in its apps that allows users to create and edit images using photos from public Instagram accounts, simply by tagging those accounts. Users whose public photos are used receive no notification, raising immediate concerns about consent, harassment, impersonation, and nonconsensual image manipulation. Only private accounts and those belonging to users under 18 are automatically excluded. The launch is drawing heightened scrutiny given Meta's prior privacy controversies, including a $5 billion FTC fine in 2019 over user data misuse.

title: Meta’s new AI chips will begin production in September
summary: Meta's custom AI chips, developed under its Meta Training and Inference Accelerator (MTIA) program in partnership with Broadcom and manufactured by TSMC, are on track to begin production in September 2026, according to an internal memo cited by Reuters. At least one chip completed its testing phase in about six weeks. The chips are designed to reduce Meta's dependence on Nvidia and AMD GPUs for training ranking and recommendation algorithms, broader AI workloads, and inference across its applications. This development comes as Meta projects capital expenditures of $125–$145 billion in 2026, largely directed at AI infrastructure, with plans to deploy 7 gigawatts of compute this year and double that amount next year.

title: Nvidia is a victim of the compute marketplace it created
summary: Nvidia's stock has fallen 15% since its May 2026 peak even as revenue projections continue to grow, with the company now trading below the S&P average on a price-to-earnings basis. The shift reflects easing GPU supply constraints and a new bottleneck emerging in AI infrastructure: memory. Spot prices for DRAM have surged roughly tenfold over the past year while the hourly spot price for an H100 GPU has dropped from a peak of around $3.20, as hyperscalers including Google, Amazon, Microsoft, and OpenAI deploy custom processors that, while inferior to Nvidia's latest, are sufficient to drive down compute prices. Memory chipmaker Micron has nearly tripled in value over the same period, illustrating how Nvidia's own success in proving compute's value has attracted competitors that erode its pricing power.
quote: "Everyone wants to make their own silicon, but no one is making their own DRAM." — Wayne Nelms, Co-founder and CTO, Ornn

title: Microsoft’s patch Tuesdays are about to get bigger
summary: Microsoft is expanding its use of AI in its security update process, saying customers will see a higher volume of security fixes included in each monthly Patch Tuesday release. The company is updating its Secure Development Lifecycle to account for AI-enabled attack techniques, as hackers increasingly use AI to exploit vulnerabilities faster. Microsoft is also investing in Windows-specific tools and agentic systems to help generate and validate security fixes, while keeping human developers in the loop for code review and risk-based decisions. The move comes as AI is accelerating vulnerability discovery on both the offensive and defensive sides, with models like Anthropic's Claude Mythos reportedly already finding high-severity flaws in every major operating system.

title: OpenAI rolls out GPT-5.6 after government greenlight — and announces ‘ChatGPT Work’
summary: OpenAI has launched GPT-5.6 to the general public following Trump administration approval, after a brief period restricted to government-authorized organizations. Alongside the model, OpenAI unveiled ChatGPT Work, an AI agent combining ChatGPT and Codex capabilities for non-technical users, enabling tasks like document creation, spreadsheet building, and integration with tools such as Slack, Gmail, and Google Drive. The product directly competes with Anthropic's Claude Cowork and is powered by a three-model suite — Sol, Terra, and Luna — with Sol positioned as a high-capability, lower-cost alternative to rivals' top models. Access begins immediately for Mac and Windows desktop users globally, with broader mobile and web rollout following over the next 24 hours.

title: Anthropic’s new Claude feature is quietly selling you on AI
summary: Anthropic has launched Claude Reflect, a built-in analytics dashboard that lets users visualize their Claude usage patterns, topics discussed, and the types of tasks they rely on AI for. Available in beta for Free, Pro, and Max users with memory enabled, the feature frames Claude as a central productivity tool while also nudging users toward more intentional AI habits through prompts like asking what tasks they want to keep doing themselves. The feature also serves a clear retention purpose, suggesting workflow integrations such as Claude's Projects feature to deepen user dependence and reduce switching to competitors. Anthropic says data shown in Reflect is not used for other purposes, and health-related conversation data is excluded entirely.

title: Anthropic, OpenAI, and SpaceX are bigger than the last 25 years of tech exits
summary: The combined value of the SpaceX, Anthropic, and OpenAI public offerings is projected to exceed all U.S. venture-backed exits since 2000, according to the NCVA-Pitchbook Venture Monitor report released in July 2026. SpaceX went public at a $1.77 trillion valuation, with Anthropic and OpenAI both pushing into the trillions, putting the trio's combined value likely north of $4 trillion. For context, the SEC counted just $70 billion in U.S.-based IPO proceeds in 2025, and Uber's $84 billion IPO in 2019 — once considered enormous — represents less than 5% of SpaceX's offering alone. The report attributes the unprecedented scale to companies staying private longer and the capital-intensive nature of AI training, which has inflated valuations and is already straining financial infrastructure.

title: Meta says its new AI model is ready to compete on coding
summary: Meta has launched Muse Spark 1.1, an upgraded in-house AI model available through a new Meta Model API now in public preview for US developers. The company describes it as a "step-change" improvement over the first generation, with enhanced coding capabilities including complex bug detection and fixing, stronger support for agentic workflows and multi-agent systems, and native multimodal perception across images, videos, and documents. The launch follows Meta's release of an image generation model earlier this week and is part of the company's broader effort to compete with OpenAI, Google, and Anthropic after significant investment, high-profile hires, and a corporate restructuring. New API accounts will receive $20 in free credits, and the model is accessible now in Thinking mode through the Meta AI app and website.

title: Say hello to Claude Wrapped
summary: Anthropic has launched a "reflect" feature for Claude, giving users a dashboard to review their AI usage patterns over periods ranging from one month to a full year. The tool surfaces key topics, task types, and peak usage times, and will allow users to set quiet hours or break reminders — functioning similarly to Spotify Wrapped but framed around self-awareness and intentional AI use. The feature is available in beta for free, Pro, and Max subscribers who have memory enabled, accessible via Settings on the web or desktop app. Anthropic positions it as a tool to help users evaluate whether their Claude usage aligns with their personal goals and to preserve independent thinking.

title: Character.AI wants a piece of the microdrama pie
summary: Character.AI has launched c.ai Series, a short-form episodic video format featuring animated, AI-generated microdramas that users can interact with by chatting with characters after watching episodes. The company is debuting with three titles — Last Summer, The Nighttime Game, and Eden Fall — each consisting of 10 episodes under two minutes long, with the first eight free and the last two behind a paywall. The shows were developed by an in-house team using Hollywood screenwriters alongside Character.AI's proprietary generative AI pipeline, keeping production in-house to maintain visual consistency across characters and scenes. The move targets the microdrama market projected to reach $26 billion in coming years, and positions Character.AI to expand from chatbot interactions into broader AI-powered entertainment.
quote: "This isn't just a one-off thing we're doing because microdramas are the next cool thing; it's a very natural extension of what our users are already doing and it opens up another massive gateway into our community." — Karandeep Anand, CEO, Character.AI

title: Popular open source AI developer tool Ollama raises $65M, grows to nearly 9M users
summary: Ollama, the open source tool that lets developers run AI models locally on their PCs, has raised a $65 million Series B led by Theory Ventures, bringing total funding to $88 million. The company, founded in 2023 by the team behind Docker Desktop, now serves nearly 9 million monthly active developers and is deployed across 85% of the Fortune 500, all with just 14 employees. Ollama has expanded beyond its free local-model tool into a cloud subscription business where developers can access larger open-weight models by GPU time rather than token limits, a move that has attracted some criticism but also reflects growing enterprise demand for cost-effective alternatives to closed AI models. The funding round signals broader VC appetite for open source AI infrastructure companies as enterprises increasingly turn to open-weight models to manage inference costs.
quote: "Every company with high inference expenses has a vital existential project pushing them to move to open-weight models." — Peter Fenton, Board Member, Benchmark

title: Character.AI enters the microdrama arena with its own productions, but there’s a twist
summary: Character.AI is launching its own AI-produced microdrama series, entering a crowded market that now includes TikTok, Instagram, Amazon Prime, and Peacock. The company is debuting three shows — a romance, a horror series, and a survival drama — with a distinctive feature allowing users over 18 to chat with characters, ask questions, and roleplay alternative storylines. The move is part of a broader entertainment pivot, with Character.AI also testing audio series (c.ai FM) and a fiction creation tool (c.ai Reads), positioning itself as a platform for interactive, AI-native storytelling. The company says the studio-led model is a stepping stone toward creator tools that will let users build and share their own series, while engagement data from Sensor Tower shows users already spend more than 950 minutes per month on the platform.

title: Q&A: Minister Evan Solomon on why Ottawa wants Canadians to adopt AI
summary: Canada's Minister of Artificial Intelligence Evan Solomon outlined the federal government's "AI for All" strategy in a wide-ranging interview, emphasizing sovereign AI infrastructure, workforce development, and equal access to the technology. The strategy includes a $500 million Technology Growth Fund with equity investment optionality, reforms to the Compute Access Fund to prioritize Canadian providers, and a goal of creating 250,000 AI-related job placements. Solomon highlighted Canada's strategic advantage in large language models through Cohere — one of only four countries with such a model — and pointed to the Cohere-Aleph Alpha merger as a move to provide a global alternative to Silicon Valley. The minister framed the core national choice as whether Canada builds AI infrastructure, innovation, and rules domestically or cedes those decisions to others.
quote: "Either we build the infrastructure here or we rent it somewhere else. Either we build the innovation here with our SMEs and our entrepreneurs, or we buy it from someone else, and either we make the rules here or we follow someone else's." — Evan Solomon, Minister of Artificial Intelligence

title: Lovable reportedly in talks to double its valuation to $13.2B
summary: Lovable, a Swedish vibe-coding startup, is in talks to raise $300 million at a $13.2 billion valuation — double its December 2025 valuation — with Menlo Ventures expected to lead the round, according to Sifted. The company, which lets users build software by describing it in plain language, hit $500 million in annualized revenue run rate in June and counts Workday, Asana, and Nvidia among its enterprise customers. The raise underscores vibe coding's emergence as the most commercially significant AI use case, with rivals Replit valued at $9 billion and Cursor acquired by SpaceX for $60 billion last month. The deal would cement Lovable, at under three years old, as one of the most valuable AI startups in Europe.

title: SpaceX's Grok 4.5 launches at half the price of rivals — here's why that could rattle Anthropic and OpenAI
summary: SpaceX launched Grok 4.5 on Wednesday, its first AI model trained specifically for coding and autonomous agents, priced at $2 per million input tokens and $6 per million output tokens — less than half the cost of comparable frontier models from Anthropic and OpenAI. The model is the first tangible product of SpaceX's $60 billion acquisition of coding tool Cursor, whose interaction data and compute access helped shape its training. Independent benchmarking firm Artificial Analysis ranked Grok 4.5 fourth on its agentic performance index but measured it at $0.49 per completed task, nearly 90% cheaper than the models ranked above it, placing it on the Pareto frontier for performance versus cost. The launch is a pivotal test of Musk's vertically integrated AI strategy — combining the Colossus supercomputer, the Grok model, and Cursor's developer distribution — and a direct challenge to Anthropic, which currently controls roughly half the AI coding market.
quote: "Our internal assessment is that Grok 4.5 is roughly comparable to Opus 4.7, but much faster." — Elon Musk, CEO, SpaceX

title: Suspecting AI cheating, Ivy League prof ordered an in-person final; scores fell 50%
summary: A Brown University economics professor switched his spring 2026 final exam to in-person after suspecting widespread AI cheating following a midterm where 40 of 86 students scored a perfect 100, far above the historical average of 65–80%. When Roberto Serrano announced the change, 18 students dropped the course and 9 others skipped the final entirely — 22 of those 27 had scored perfect 100s on the midterm. Among students who sat the in-person final, the average score dropped from 96 to 48. The case adds concrete data to growing concerns about AI-enabled academic dishonesty, with a Princeton survey separately finding that nearly 30% of students admitted to AI cheating on at least one exam or assignment.
quote: "We cannot afford to have a society in which a significant fraction of our best young minds think that cheating is okay." — Roberto Serrano, Economics Professor, Brown University

title: Does Anthropic want to eat your business?
summary: Anthropic's announcement that it will begin developing its own drugs — after touting pharma clients like Sanofi and Novo Nordisk — has sparked sharp debate about whether enterprises are inadvertently training their future competitors. Palantir's Alex Karp and VC David Sacks both warned that businesses feeding proprietary data into Anthropic's models may be paying twice: once in tokens, and once in competitive intelligence. The concern is amplified by the reported case of Figma, which was allegedly blindsided when Anthropic launched Claude Design after the two had partnered on AI-assisted tools. The episode is accelerating interest in open-source, locally hosted models among companies protective of their intellectual property, particularly in health, legal, and finance.

title: Google’s deepfake detector system used to debunk McConnell hoax pic
summary: Google's SynthID watermarking system successfully identified a viral AI-generated hoax image purporting to show Senator Mitch McConnell hospitalized in distress, with fact-checking site Snopes using the tool to debunk the image by mid-week. SynthID, launched at Google's I/O conference in 2025, embeds an invisible signature into AI-generated images that survives screenshotting and cross-platform sharing. The system's reach has expanded significantly: Gemini models have carried the watermark since launch, and OpenAI joined the program in May 2026, though Anthropic has not participated. The incident represents a notable real-world validation of anti-deepfake watermarking technology, as well as a reminder of its key limitation — it only works when image-generation tools actively opt into the program.

title: Lawsuit: Man used Grok to make 7K sex images of stepdaughter, then shot himself
summary: A proposed class action lawsuit expanded Tuesday alleges that xAI's Grok chatbot enabled a stepfather to generate approximately 7,000 sexually explicit AI images of his stepdaughter from a single photo taken when she was 11, and that xAI repeatedly obstructed law enforcement investigations by refusing to share user IP addresses and other identifying information required under mandatory reporting rules. The lawsuit also adds Stability AI as a defendant, alleging its open-weight models underpin third-party "nudify" apps used to further manipulate Grok outputs, and cites a June 2026 report finding that the Stable Diffusion family drives 42.7 percent of online image-based nudification. NCMEC found that 90 percent of xAI's CyberTipline reports lacked actionable information for law enforcement, a pattern the complaint says reflects deliberate prioritization of profit over child safety. The case signals escalating legal and regulatory risk for AI companies whose content-generation tools are used to produce child sexual abuse material, with lawyers estimating thousands of minors may be eligible to join the classes.
quote: "This technology is a free, easily accessible weapon put into the hands of the worst people in the world." — Jane Doe 4, Plaintiff

title: Meta to build Canada’s largest data centre north of Edmonton
summary: Meta has announced a $13-billion CAD investment to build its first Canadian data centre in Alberta's Industrial Heartland, northeast of Edmonton, making it the largest data centre in Canada. The one-gigawatt, AI-optimized facility will be built in Sturgeon County and is expected to support 3,000 construction jobs and 300 permanent positions, with Meta also committing $60 million CAD to local infrastructure upgrades. The data centre will draw power partly from Alberta's electricity grid and partly from an on-site natural gas facility, with Meta pledging to match total energy use with 100 percent renewables. The project signals Alberta's aggressive push to attract hyperscale AI infrastructure, though it raises environmental questions, particularly around water use and emissions, amid broader North American backlash against large-scale data centre development.
quote: "Data centres need reliable energy and speed to market. Meta's investment proves that Alberta delivers." — Nate Glubish, Alberta's innovation minister

title: SpaceXAI releases Grok 4.5, which Elon describes as an ‘Opus-class model’
summary: SpaceXAI has released Grok 4.5, a new large language model the company positions as competitive with Anthropic's Opus-class models while offering significantly lower pricing — $2 per million input tokens and $6 per million output tokens, compared to Opus 4.7's $5 and $25 respectively. Elon Musk described the model as faster and more token-efficient than Anthropic's equivalent, claiming it is roughly comparable to Opus 4.7 in capability. The release comes as SpaceXAI also touts twice the token efficiency of competing models, a meaningful cost advantage as AI spending scrutiny grows among enterprise customers. The launch lands in a competitive week, with OpenAI also preparing to release GPT 5.6, described as its strongest model yet, on Thursday.
quote: "Our internal assessment is that Grok 4.5 is roughly comparable to Opus 4.7, but much faster." — Elon Musk, Founder, SpaceXAI

title: This startup thinks robotics is about to have its ChatGPT moment
summary: General Intuition, a startup that trained a foundation model on millions of hours of video game data, raised $320 million at a $2.3 billion valuation last month on the thesis that robotics is approaching its own foundation-model inflection point. CEO Pim de Witte argues that just as GPT-style models displaced task-specific NLP models, a single general-purpose physical AI model can replace the massive real-world data collection efforts currently underway across the robotics industry. The company demonstrated its model powering a quadrupedal robot after fine-tuning on just eight minutes of real-world data, suggesting dramatic reductions in the data requirements that have historically constrained robotics development. Rather than building robots itself, General Intuition aims to become the base model layer for other robotics companies, positioning itself as an infrastructure play analogous to what OpenAI's models became for software applications.
quote: "The generalization of the model itself is the product." — Pim de Witte, CEO, General Intuition

title: OpenAI launches GPT-Live, a full-duplex voice upgrade that lets ChatGPT talk more like a person
summary: OpenAI has launched GPT-Live, replacing its Advanced Voice Mode with two new models — GPT-Live-1 and GPT-Live-1 mini — that use a full-duplex architecture allowing ChatGPT to listen and speak simultaneously, eliminating the awkward turn-based pauses of previous voice AI. The system also decouples voice interaction from reasoning, delegating complex queries to GPT-5.5 running in the background while the voice model maintains conversational flow — a modular design that lets OpenAI upgrade intelligence without retraining the voice layer. GPT-Live rolls out globally today on iOS, Android, and ChatGPT.com, with GPT-Live-1 as the default for paid users and GPT-Live-1 mini for free-tier users, and an API offering planned. The launch is significant for enterprise use cases, as the architecture enables voice agents to hold natural conversations while simultaneously querying databases or performing multi-step reasoning — capabilities that previously introduced disruptive dead air.

title: Google Photos adds a new AI ‘Video Remix’ tool
summary: Google Photos is launching a Video Remix feature powered by Gemini Omni that allows users to transform videos with AI-driven edits — such as cinematic relighting, background swaps, and artistic style effects like watercolor or oil painting — in a matter of taps. The tool is accessible through the Create tab in Google Photos and is rolling out to eligible Google AI Plus, Pro, and Ultra subscribers across the U.S. and 13 other countries. The launch is part of Google's broader push to embed generative AI tools directly into consumer apps, competing with Apple, OpenAI, and Adobe. By integrating AI video editing into Google Photos, Google aims to reduce reliance on dedicated editing software and deepen user retention within its ecosystem.
quote: "Creating beautiful video clips shouldn't require professional skills or hours of editing." — Google, Blog Post

title: Why this CEO thinks video games make better training data than the internet
summary: General Intuition, a New York-based AI startup that spun out of gaming platform Medal TV, has closed a $320 million funding round at a $2.3 billion valuation, with investors including Coatue, Eric Schmidt, and researchers from MIT and Google DeepMind, along with backing from Jeff Bezos. The company is building world models trained on gaming data, arguing that video games — which simulate how objects move through space and time — provide richer training data than text-heavy internet content for developing physical AI. CEO Pim de Witte contends that large language models like ChatGPT and Claude lack the spatial and temporal reasoning needed for genuine artificial general intelligence. The funding signals growing investor conviction that gaming-derived data could be a meaningful differentiator in the race toward more generalizable AI systems.

title: Meta wants its AI glasses to seem less creepy. Its AI strategy says otherwise.
summary: Meta is adding a safeguard to its AI-enabled Ray-Ban glasses that will disable the camera if the LED recording indicator is tampered with, acknowledging that some users had been deliberately covering or destroying the light to record others covertly. However, the move comes alongside a string of privacy-eroding features: Meta AI can now use anyone's public Instagram photos to generate AI images unless users opt out, and the company is reportedly testing AI glasses prototypes that continuously collect audio and take photos every few seconds. The juxtaposition highlights a pattern in Meta's broader AI strategy — incremental privacy concessions paired with aggressive data collection and AI training practices. The company faces multiple ongoing investigations and lawsuits related to its AI glasses, including one stemming from allegations that outsourced Kenyan workers were forced to review graphic content while training Meta's AI on glasses footage.

title: ChatGPT’s upgraded voice mode is better at shutting up
summary: OpenAI has launched GPT-Live-1, a new full-duplex voice model for ChatGPT that can speak and listen simultaneously, enabling more natural conversations by interrupting users less and waiting through pauses. The model automatically routes complex queries to OpenAI's best text models, such as GPT-5.5, for reasoning and web search, and adds features like real-time translation and AI-generated visuals for weather, stocks, and sports. OpenAI has built in safeguards against harmful responses, crisis helpline support for self-harm conversations, and age-appropriate responses for teens — moves that come as the company faces lawsuits alleging ChatGPT harmed users' mental health. GPT-Live-1 is rolling out on iOS, Android, and web for paid subscribers, while a smaller GPT-Live-1 mini will serve free users.
quote: "This is a full duplex model. What it really means is that it can speak and listen at the same time." — Atty Eleti, OpenAI product lead

title: OpenAI releases new voice models for more natural live conversations
summary: OpenAI has launched GPT-Live-1 and GPT-Live-1 mini, full-duplex conversational voice models that can speak and listen simultaneously, enabling natural interruptions and real-time translation. The new models replace ChatGPT's current Advanced Voice Mode by default, with paid-tier users gaining access to the larger GPT-Live-1. Unlike the previous pipeline approach combining separate speech-to-text, language model, and text-to-speech components, the new models connect to frontier text models like GPT-5.5 for search, reasoning, and agentic tasks. OpenAI sees voice as a potential primary interface for complex work, with over 150 million people already using ChatGPT's voice and dictation features.
quote: "Over time, we think this will also unlock the ability to use voice as a kind of primary interface to computing, and to manage increasingly complex long-running agentic work." — Atty Eleti, ChatGPT Voice Product Lead

title: Prime Intellect raises $130M Series A to help enterprises build their own AI agents
summary: Prime Intellect, a startup offering a full-stack platform for building AI agents using reinforcement learning, has raised a $130 million Series A at a $1 billion valuation, led by Radical Ventures with participation from Nvidia Ventures, Intel Capital, and others. The company, founded in 2024, provides compute access, RL training frameworks, and evaluation tools so enterprises can train their own models without relying on frontier labs like OpenAI or Anthropic. It has reached $100 million in annualized revenue, with customers including Ramp and Zapier citing accuracy and cost advantages over frontier models. Growing enterprise wariness about data privacy risks and dependency on closed-source labs is accelerating demand for the kind of ownership Prime Intellect enables.
quote: "It shouldn't just be a few nerds in a glass tower in San Francisco that have the capability to train AI models." — Vincent Weisser, Co-founder and CEO, Prime Intellect

title: These AI startups are growing revenue at faster and faster rates
summary: A cohort of AI startups — including Anthropic, Mercor, Glean, Sierra, and Clio — are reporting accelerating revenue growth, with each successive milestone arriving in shorter timeframes. Anthropic crossed a $47 billion annualized revenue run rate in late May 2026, less than two months after surpassing $30 billion, while Mercor reached $2 billion in gross annualized revenue just four months after hitting $1 billion. The trend extends beyond AI-native firms: HR tech company Gusto surpassed $1 billion in trailing 12-month revenue with accelerating growth across five consecutive quarters, and legal software provider Clio hit $500 million ARR after embedding AI in 2023. The article notes that the underlying revenue metrics vary across companies — ranging from committed ARR to annualized run-rate revenue — making direct comparisons difficult, but the shared pattern is one of compressing timelines to each successive milestone.

title: AI4Eyes sets its sights on faster dry eye diagnoses in Montréal clinics
summary: Canadian healthtech startup AI4Eyes is rolling out its AI-powered dry eye disease diagnosis and treatment recommendation platform for real-world testing in select Québec clinics, backed by $700,000 from the Québec government's Medteq+ Support Program. The Montréal-based company, which combines hardware and software to deliver a multi-test exam in minutes, is one of seven medtech firms selected for the program. A subdivision of Québec's regional health agency will evaluate the platform's image quality, diagnostic accuracy, and treatment recommendation algorithms. The testing adds to $6.5 million CAD in financing AI4Eyes announced in January 2026, bringing fresh momentum to a company targeting a condition affecting up to 30 percent of Canadians.

title: Gaming data could be the secret to AGI, according to this Bezos-backed startup
summary: General Intuition, a New York-based AI startup valued at $2.3 billion, has closed a $320 million funding round backed by Jeff Bezos, Coatue, Eric Schmidt, and researchers from MIT and Google DeepMind. The company argues that large language models fall short of AGI because they lack the ability to understand how objects move through space and time — a gap it aims to fill by training world models on gaming data. General Intuition spun out of gaming platform Medal TV, betting that the rich spatial and temporal information in gameplay footage can produce more generalizable AI. The raise positions the company as a significant new player in physical AI, though it also faces questions about the ethical boundaries of its technology, including potential defense applications.

title: Slack’s Slackbot can now pull your CRM data, generate charts, and send DocuSigns — all from a chat message.
summary: Slack has launched an integration connecting Slackbot — the AI agent built into every workspace — to the full Salesforce platform, including CRM data, Tableau analytics, Data 360 customer profiles, and third-party apps like DocuSign, Atlassian, and Canva, all accessible through a single conversational prompt. The integration uses Salesforce's Model Context Protocol servers linked to its Headless 360 infrastructure, allowing users to query customer data, generate visualizations, update records, and trigger approvals without leaving Slack. Slack CMO Ryan Gavin frames the move around "multiplayer AI" — the idea that AI must operate in shared, visible team channels rather than private one-on-one sessions — as the company faces intensifying competition from Microsoft Teams with Copilot and Google Workspace with Gemini. The announcement also navigates a delicate tension with Anthropic, whose Claude Tag now operates inside Slack channels even as Salesforce spends an estimated $300 million annually on Anthropic tokens and holds a stake in the company.
quote: "It's going to benefit almost no one if every enterprise application out there spawns hundreds of agent babies, and employees end up in a worse world than they were before." — Ryan Gavin, CMO, Slack

title: AI chip maker SambaNova raises $1B at $11B valuation, 5 months after last mega round
summary: SambaNova Systems, an AI chip company, has raised $1 billion at an $11 billion valuation in a Series F round led by General Atlantic, just five months after closing a $350 million Series E in February 2026. The Palo Alto-based startup simultaneously announced that JPMorganChase has selected it as an "inference-infrastructure partner," with SambaNova's SN40L and SN50 systems set to power on-premises AI inference at the bank — a signal, CEO Rodrigo Liang says, that major financial institutions are moving to build private AI infrastructure rather than relying on cloud services. SambaNova's next-generation SN50 chip, designed to fit multi-trillion-parameter models onto a single rack, is set to begin shipping in the second half of 2026, with SoftBank as its first deployment partner. The company will use the funds to scale operations and secure its supply chain amid what Liang describes as an "incredible wave of demand."
