quote: "Banks of the caliber of JP Morgan are now building their own private, secure infrastructure to run inference on their most sensitive models." — Rodrigo Liang, CEO and Co-founder, SambaNova

title: Hackers can use 9 of the most popular AI tools to assemble massive botnets
summary: Researchers from Tel Aviv University, Technion, and Intuit have identified a new attack technique called HalluSquatting that exploits AI coding assistants' tendency to hallucinate incorrect resource locations, enabling attackers to build large-scale botnets and deliver ransomware without targeting individual victims. The attack works against nine widely used AI coding tools including Cursor, GitHub Copilot, Windsurf, and Gemini CLI, and is rooted in a foundational flaw across six major LLMs — including GPT-5, Gemini 2.5, and Claude — which hallucinate repository locations up to 92.4% of the time for resources published in 2025. Attackers can predict the most commonly hallucinated identifiers, register those locations, and seed them with instructions to install reverse shells, infecting large numbers of developer machines with minimal effort. Unlike prior prompt injection attacks that required targeting individuals, HalluSquatting scales automatically as AI agents pull resources in normal operation, marking a significant escalation in the AI security threat landscape.
quote: "Like typosquatting, it's a problem that's not going away. At the end of the day, it's about the level of agency we allow our agents." — Michael Bargury, CTO of Zenity

title: Meta just launched a new AI generator, Muse Image, and users are already pushing back over use of their photos
summary: Meta has launched Muse Image, an AI image generator built by its Meta Superintelligence Labs unit, now available free through the Meta AI app, Instagram Stories, and WhatsApp. The tool's most controversial feature allows users to tag any public Instagram account and use that person's photos to generate new AI images — without notifying the account holder. The feature is opt-out by default, meaning users must actively change their settings to prevent their images from being used, a pattern critics say echoes Meta's history of broad data use unless users intervene. The launch comes amid longstanding scrutiny of Meta's privacy practices, including a $5 billion FTC fine in 2019 over the Cambridge Analytica data harvesting scandal.

title: Data centers’ energy demand threatens Trump’s “Made in America” plan
summary: AI data center energy demand is driving up electricity costs across the 13-state PJM Interconnection grid, squeezing US manufacturers in the Rust Belt at the very moment the Trump administration is championing domestic production. PJM's capacity prices have surged from $28.92 per megawatt-day in 2024 to $329.17 in 2026, with steelmaker Metallus reporting a 70 percent jump in electricity costs — an extra $15 million annually. The grid operator forecasts a 6.6-gigawatt supply shortfall by 2027, while 266 gigawatts of power projects — 93 percent of them clean energy — were cancelled in 2025 alone, worsening the supply crunch. The conflict between AI infrastructure growth and manufacturing competitiveness poses a direct challenge to Trump's industrial revival agenda.

title: Meta’s new Muse Image model can pull other Instagram users into AI photos
summary: Meta has launched Muse Image, its first AI image generation model from its Superintelligence Labs division, now powering image-making tools across the Meta AI app, Instagram, and WhatsApp, with Facebook and Messenger support coming soon. The model is notable for allowing users to @ mention other Instagram accounts in prompts, enabling the AI to incorporate those users' likenesses using their public photos. Muse Image is described as "agentic," working alongside Meta's Muse Spark large language model to reason through prompts, search the web, and plan before generating images. The launch also includes 30 new AI effects coming to Instagram Stories in the US, along with capabilities such as room redesign, direct photo editing by drawing on images, and design creation for invitations and postcards.
quote: "Muse Image is 'agentic,' meaning it works with its Muse Spark large language model 'to reason through your prompt, search the web, and plan before it generates.'" — Alexandr Wang, Head of Meta Superintelligence Labs

title: Why the rise of open source AI isn’t hurting Anthropic … yet
summary: Data from Vercel's AI gateway and OpenRouter shows that while open-source models like DeepSeek are surging in token volume, frontier providers like Anthropic continue to capture the majority of AI spending. On Vercel's platform, Anthropic accounts for more than half of overall AI spend despite DeepSeek handling roughly a third of token volume. Decagon CEO Jesse Zhang's theory offers one explanation: frontier and open-source models occupy different phases of the same lifecycle, with expensive frontier models proving out new use cases that later migrate to cheaper alternatives as they mature, while new use cases continuously replenish demand at the top. This dynamic suggests a stable two-tiered AI economy where frontier labs maintain premium pricing by dominating early-stage deployments even as open-source models increasingly own production workloads.
quote: "The frontier labs will keep owning discovery. Open source will increasingly own production." — Jesse Zhang, CEO of Decagon

title: Microsoft joins AI cost-cutting trend by relying more on its own models
summary: Microsoft has begun routing a portion of user prompts in Excel and Word through its own in-house MAI models rather than relying exclusively on OpenAI and Anthropic, according to Bloomberg. The move is part of a broader cost-reduction strategy as AI service expenses have become a significant burden across the tech industry. Microsoft expanded its MAI model lineup last month at its Build conference, launching seven new models including an agentic coder and a text-to-image generator. The trend extends beyond Microsoft, with Amazon, Uber, Meta, and Accenture also reportedly cutting AI spending, and some companies even turning to Chinese models for cheaper agentic solutions.

title: Discord admits AI moderation bug wrongfully banned users over harmless images
summary: Discord acknowledged that a bug in its AI moderation system led to the wrongful permanent banning of more than 8,000 users over two months, after harmless images such as spreadsheets, chessboards, and game textures were incorrectly flagged as harmful content. The system works by matching uploaded images against databases of known illegal material, but a bug bypassed the intended human review step, resulting in immediate account bans. All affected accounts are being restored, and Discord says it is working on better safeguards. The incident underscores broader risks as platforms scale AI-assisted moderation, with similar unexplained mass suspensions having also affected Instagram, Facebook Groups, and Tumblr users in recent years.

title: Canada’s telco and banking incumbents form AI consortium
summary: Scotiabank, Sun Life, Telus, and Toronto-based consultancy Lightworks have launched The AI Consortium, a Canadian industry group aimed at pooling engineering and research efforts to address shared AI infrastructure and governance challenges among large regulated institutions. Members will jointly develop intellectual property that all participants can use, avoiding duplicated effort across competitors — a model compared to Symcor, the 1990s cheque-processing consortium formed by Canadian banks. Lightworks, the managing founding member, secured venture financing from Round13 earlier this year to build the initiative, which has been in development for 18 months. The Consortium's first project, the Agentic Control Plane, will help members track and manage how AI agents are deployed across their organizations, with future projects targeting AI cost management and access to sovereign AI compute.
quote: "That consortium was an interesting precedent in Canada since competitors came together to solve a common problem." — Laura Money, Sun Life

title: ElevenLabs to open Toronto office and double Canadian headcount by end of 2026
summary: ElevenLabs, the AI voice and conversational agent startup valued at $11 billion, is formalizing its Canadian presence by opening a Toronto office and doubling its 13-person Canadian workforce by end of 2026. The company has appointed Max Lemmens as general manager for Canada to lead the expansion, with existing clients including Telus Digital, The Globe and Mail, and Boosted.ai. ElevenLabs, which recently surpassed $500 million in annual recurring revenue and counts Ontario Teachers' Pension Plan among its investors, sees Canada's bilingual status as a particular opportunity for voice and audio AI adoption. The move aligns with the Canadian government's goal of boosting domestic AI adoption from 12 percent to 60 percent by 2034.

title: Anthropic is launching Claude Cowork on mobile and web
summary: Anthropic is expanding Claude Cowork, its AI agent platform, to mobile (iOS and Android) and web for the first time, having previously been limited to the macOS and Windows desktop apps. The rollout begins with Max subscribers, with broader availability across other plans coming in the following weeks. A key addition is cloud-based processing, allowing Cowork sessions to continue across devices and run scheduled tasks even when no user device is online, with notifications sent to mobile when review or approval is needed. Anthropic is also extending doubled Cowork usage limits through August 5th, 2026.

title: Claude Cowork expands to mobile and web
summary: Anthropic is expanding Claude Cowork — its agentic AI tool for general knowledge work — from a desktop-only app to web and mobile platforms for Max subscribers, starting July 7, 2026. The move allows users to assign tasks, monitor progress, and retrieve outputs across devices without needing their laptop active, positioning Cowork as a persistent background assistant rather than a desktop-bound tool. Anthropic also released usage data from 1.2 million anonymized sessions across more than 600,000 organizations, finding that business process tasks (33.4%) and content creation (16.4%) far outpace software development (8.7%) as the leading use cases. The expansion signals Anthropic's broader ambition to compete for the space where everyday office work happens, a race it is running alongside OpenAI, which is similarly pushing its Codex tool beyond developers into general business use.
quote: "While coding is still — understandably — one of the uses of AI that gets the most attention, the use of AI for everyday business work is on the rise, and the kinds of tasks people are finding it most helpful for are coming into focus." — Anthropic, Company Statement

title: Facing US export controls, China's DeepSeek plans to make its own chips
summary: DeepSeek, the Chinese AI startup known for building large language models competitive with US rivals, is planning to enter the chip business, according to Reuters. The company has spent about a year exploring a move into silicon, meeting with potential hardware partners and hiring engineers, with a focus on data center inference chips. The goal is to reduce reliance on both Nvidia — largely blocked from China by US export controls — and Huawei, which currently holds roughly half of China's data center chip market. The move mirrors similar strategies by US AI leaders: OpenAI recently announced its first inference chip, Jalapeño, developed with Broadcom, while Anthropic is also exploring custom chip design.

title: Anthropic brings Claude Cowork to mobile and web as usage data shows most users aren’t coding
summary: Anthropic has expanded Claude Cowork to mobile and web platforms, enabling tasks to run autonomously in the background and sync across devices, with the rollout beginning in beta for Max subscribers. Alongside the launch, Anthropic released usage data from 1.2 million anonymized Cowork sessions showing that software development accounts for only 8.7% of usage, while business process and operations tasks (33.4%) and content creation (16.4%) dominate — together comprising roughly half of all sessions. The data directly challenges the prevailing enterprise AI narrative that coding assistants are the primary driver of AI adoption, and positions Anthropic's strategy around capturing the far larger market of knowledge workers handling what the company calls "the work around the work." The mobile expansion also arrives amid unresolved security questions after a firm published research describing a sandbox escape in Cowork's Windows desktop version, though the attack surface differs from the server-side architecture used by the new web and mobile versions.
quote: "Claude Code, Cowork, and chat are very single-player, whereas Claude Tag is built to be interactive and multiplayer." — Cat Wu, Head of Product for Claude Code and Cowork, Anthropic

title: Alberta is open-sourcing its AI playbook for government
summary: The Government of Alberta has released "The Velocity Papers," an open-source collection of technical white papers detailing how the Canadian province used AI tools from Anthropic and Google to modernize its digital infrastructure over the past 18 months. A team of AI agents built on Anthropic's Claude reviewed more than 466 million lines of government code in 20 hours, a task Alberta estimates would have taken six years and cost over $2 billion using a human-led approach. The documents are being made available free of charge with step-by-step instructions, positioned as a replicable roadmap for other governments. The release comes shortly after the US temporarily froze foreign access to Anthropic's latest models, making Alberta a timely showcase for the company's capabilities abroad.
quote: "The Velocity Papers are the first defensible in-production blueprint for an agentic public service." — Cole Cioran, Managing Partner, Info-Tech Research Group

title: Savi’s app aims to protect consumers from realistic AI scams like kidnappers demanding ransom
summary: Savi Security, founded by brothers Patrick and Ryan Coughlin, has launched an iOS and Android app that uses AI in real time to detect and flag scams delivered via text, voicemail, and live phone calls, including AI-generated voice cloning attacks. The startup raised $7 million in seed funding led by Acrew Capital and charges $8/month to cover an unlimited number of family members. The launch follows a harrowing incident in which the founders' mother received a convincing AI-generated kidnapping call using a cloned voice of her daughter. The FTC reported that imposter scams cost Americans $3.5 billion in 2025, triple the 2020 figure, as cheap generative AI tools have dramatically lowered the cost of executing sophisticated fraud.
quote: "You can clone a voice off three seconds of audio, off a publicly available social media post." — Patrick Coughlin, Co-founder, Savi Security

title: How AI could enable autonomous robot workers in workplaces—and maybe homes
summary: AI advances — particularly reinforcement learning and large foundation models — are enabling a new generation of autonomous robots capable of handling diverse tasks in unstructured environments, moving well beyond the repetitive, narrowly programmed industrial robots of past decades. Researchers and founders, including those at Boston Dynamics and Physical Intelligence, describe a shift from robots that navigate point-to-point to systems that can understand sequences of tasks and generalize across novel situations. The key bottleneck remains data: collecting enough high-quality training data for physical tasks is costly, and current methods produce robots that are either highly reliable at one specific task or broadly capable but not yet robust enough for real-world deployment. Investment in humanoid robots is substantial, but experts expect the near-term outcome to be specialized AI models powering many different robot form factors suited to specific jobs, rather than a single universal humanoid.
quote: "You can either have something that is kind of OK but not amazing at everything or something that's extremely good at one thing — we really want something that's extremely good at all things, and that's still at the frontier of research." — Sergey Levine, Computer Scientist, UC Berkeley and Cofounder, Physical Intelligence

title: The first American autonomous ground vehicles are fighting in Ukraine
summary: Forterra, a US autonomous vehicle company, has deployed more than 100 self-driving ATVs in Ukraine over the past nine months in what it describes as the largest combat deployment of autonomous ground vehicles by any US defense tech company. The gas-powered Lancer vehicles, based on Polaris ATVs and capable of carrying 750 kilograms, have completed over 1,100 missions, driven 2,500 miles, and performed 52 casualty evacuations. However, Ukrainian soldiers have largely been teleoperating the vehicles rather than running them fully autonomously, as the AI is not yet capable of identifying and reacting to unexpected enemy threats in real time. The deployment has provided Forterra with critical battlefield data to refine its autonomy stack — combining classical robotics with generative AI — and positions the company to compete for larger US military contracts.
quote: "Ground autonomy is achievable now and we've seen it." — Sergeant Major Corey Wilkens, US Army autonomous vehicles program lead

title: US investors will soon get access to SK Hynix, another memory maker riding the AI boom
summary: South Korean memory chipmaker SK Hynix is planning a U.S. IPO, offering nearly 17.8 million American depositary receipts that could raise approximately $28 billion based on its recent Seoul share price. The company is riding a massive AI-driven memory boom, with first-quarter revenues up nearly 200% year-over-year and its stock up roughly 260% in 2026. Demand for high-bandwidth memory, DRAM, and NAND chips used in AI systems has created a widely noted shortage — dubbed "RAMageddon" — as hyperscalers race to build out AI infrastructure. SK Hynix and Samsung have pledged over $550 billion in new manufacturing capacity, though analysts note that oversupply risks loom if AI memory requirements shift before those facilities come online.

title: Paraito raises $2.65 million to automate grunt work for Québec notaries
summary: Canadian proptech startup Paraito has raised $2.65 million CAD in pre-seed equity funding, led by Inovia Capital with participation from Boreal Ventures, to deploy generative AI for automating real estate title searches in Québec. The company's platform uses AI to analyze public property records—including old handwritten documents—replacing a labor-intensive manual process that notaries currently perform for every transaction. Paraito claims its tool can help firms handle three times the volume of files without additional hiring, a significant proposition given that Québec faces a documented shortage of notaries and a growing backlog of real estate work. The startup sells its French-language platform on a per-search basis and plans to eventually expand across Canada.
quote: "When we launched, I think we sent 35 emails to notaries and got 29 positive responses." — François Arbour, Co-founder, Paraito

title: You can now customize Siri’s pace and expressivity in the latest iOS 27 beta
summary: Apple's iOS 27 beta 3 has enabled voice customization controls for Siri, allowing users to adjust the assistant's speaking pace and expressivity through sliders, following their introduction at WWDC 26 in June 2026. The update is part of Apple's broader effort to rebuild Siri around generative AI and make it feel more natural and personal. Users can also choose from voices with different accents, and Siri will demonstrate adjustments in real time with sample phrases. The feature still lags behind ChatGPT's voice customization, which since December 2025 has offered controls for warmth, enthusiasm, and overall tone and style.

title: Every major tech layoff in 2026 that has name-checked AI
summary: Roughly 120,000 tech roles have been cut in 2026, according to Layoffs.fyi, with AI cited as a primary driver across major companies including Microsoft, Meta, Oracle, Snap, Intuit, Cisco, and dozens more. The pattern is consistent: companies reporting record revenues are simultaneously reducing headcount, pointing to AI as both the engine of growth and the rationale for eliminating roles. Companies are restructuring management layers, flattening hierarchies, and redirecting savings toward AI infrastructure investment. The wave represents what many in the industry describe as a structural transformation — not a cyclical downturn — with Block's Jack Dorsey predicting most companies will make similar cuts within a year.
quote: "Our approach is not AI replaces people. But it would be disingenuous to pretend AI doesn't change the mix of skills we need or the number of roles required in certain areas. It does." — Mike Cannon-Brookes, CEO, Atlassian

title: Katalyze AI thinks the future of pharma operations is teams of AI agents
summary: Canadian-founded startup Katalyze AI has raised $10.5 million USD in seed funding to deploy its agentic operating system across major pharmaceutical companies, with the round led by Bonfire Ventures and participation from Canadian investors Inovia Capital and Ripple Ventures. The platform allows pharma workers to build teams of AI agents to handle engineering, scientific, and manufacturing tasks, and is already in use at five of the world's 20 largest pharma firms, including Sanofi. The Canadian startup is dual-headquartered in Toronto and San Francisco, with co-founders Reza Farahani, a University of Waterloo graduate, and COO Shreyas Becker, who previously led AI and data products at Sanofi in Toronto. Katalyze aims to use the funding to expand its agent catalogue, grow its 40-person team, and scale deployments, with an ambitious goal of halving the time and cost to bring a drug to market by 2030.
quote: "We want to cut that in half by 2030, and we're starting where the industry loses the most time: the operational and manufacturing arc." — Reza Farahani, Co-founder and CEO

title: OpenAI proposes giving the US government a 5% equity stake to share AI wealth with Americans
summary: Sam Altman is reportedly in talks with President Trump about giving the US government a 5% stake in OpenAI, a concept Altman has advocated in various forms since 2021. At OpenAI's current $852 billion valuation, a 5% stake would be worth roughly $42.6 billion — about $320 per American household if distributed directly. The proposal has political parallels, including a far more aggressive push from Senator Bernie Sanders for a 50% public stake in top AI companies. MIT Technology Review argues the plan currently functions more as a political narrative than a concrete policy, with its real purpose being to persuade the public that the AI boom will be large enough to share broadly.

title: Google opts users into AI training on uploaded media, including images, audio, and video
summary: A recent update to Google's Search services privacy settings, announced via customer email in June 2026, has quietly opted users into expanded AI training that includes saved media such as images, audio recordings, and video. The change applies across Google Search, Maps, Shopping, Translate, Lens, and other search services, meaning that photos snapped via Google Lens or voice recordings from Google Translate or Search Live may now be retained to train AI models. Google states directly that saved media is "used to develop and improve Google services and technologies, including AI models and safety measures." Users can opt out by visiting the Search Services History settings page and unchecking the Save Media box, though the update also separated Search data into a new standalone setting, meaning prior opt-outs through Web App Activity settings no longer cover Google Search services.
quote: "Like your Search Services History, your saved media is also used to develop and improve Google services and technologies, including AI models and safety measures." — Google, Customer Email

title: Secret Claude tracker shocks users after Anthropic’s anti-surveillance stance
summary: Anthropic secretly embedded code in Claude Code that tracked Chinese users' timezone, proxy settings, and potential connections to Chinese AI labs, before removing it after a security researcher exposed the hidden tracking in July 2026. The engineer who added it described it as an "experiment" meant to prevent account abuse and protect against distillation attacks — large-scale use of Claude to train competing Chinese models. The revelation drew sharp criticism from privacy advocates, particularly given Anthropic's public stance against government surveillance of US users and its ongoing lawsuit against the White House over that issue. Alibaba subsequently banned employees from using Claude Code, citing "back-door risks," while the incident highlights the escalating tension between US AI firms and Chinese competitors accused of copying frontier models.
quote: "Hiding the signal in the system prompt makes every other privacy claim harder to believe." — Thereallo, Security Researcher

title: Microsoft lays off nearly 5,000 employees across Xbox, commercial sales
summary: Microsoft cut approximately 4,800 employees — 2.1% of its global workforce — on Monday, with Xbox absorbing 1,600 of those cuts in what CEO Asha Sharma called the most significant restructure in Xbox history. Xbox is slashing management layers from 14 to no more than five, spinning out studios including Compulsion Games and Double Fine Productions, and narrowing its focus to high-performing franchises like Minecraft and Candy Crush. The layoffs come as Microsoft simultaneously invests $2.5 billion in its new Frontier Company AI business unit, reinforcing a broader 2026 pattern in which tech job cuts correlate with surging AI spending — with close to 154,000 tech workers losing jobs in the first half of the year. Microsoft maintains the eliminated roles are not being directly replaced by AI, but acknowledges AI is fundamentally changing how work gets done.
quote: "Our business today is not healthy. We are operating at margins that are 3–10x lower than comparable platform and publishing businesses." — Asha Sharma, CEO of Xbox

title: Reddit is using LLMs to solve a problem LLMs largely created
summary: Reddit says it has deployed large language model-based tools to detect and block spam, much of which is itself generated by LLMs. The platform reports blocking 23 million spam views per day and catching roughly 25,000 new spam posts and comments daily, claiming a 20% reduction in user exposure to spam from January to March 2026 compared with the prior three months. The development reflects a broader dynamic in which social platforms must adopt AI-driven moderation to counter AI-driven abuse. Experts continue to caution, however, that AI moderation must be paired with human oversight to achieve the most effective results.
quote: "We leverage LLMs to catch the highly subtle, coordinated patterns of fake behavior and artificial hype that older systems once missed." — Reddit, Blog Post

title: UK regulator warns of "arms race" to keep up with AI use in financial services
summary: A senior UK Financial Conduct Authority official has warned that regulators are in an "arms race" to keep pace with AI adoption in financial services, calling for greater powers for the watchdog. Sheldon Mills, an FCA executive director, published a report recommending a three-to-six month review of whether AI models like ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini should fall under financial regulation, given that a fifth of UK adults are already open to using such tools for personal finance decisions without any regulatory protection or compensation recourse. The report highlights both risks — including hyper-personalized manipulation, fraud amplification via deepfakes, and opaque pricing — and potential benefits, such as democratizing access to sophisticated financial advice for lower-income individuals. Mills also recommends expanding the FCA's oversight powers over critical Big Tech providers including Anthropic, OpenAI, Amazon, Google, and Microsoft.
quote: "You need a human on the hook for what they're doing." — Sheldon Mills, Executive Director, Financial Conduct Authority

title: Microsoft is laying off 4,800 employees
summary: Microsoft is laying off approximately 4,800 employees — about 2.1 percent of its workforce — as it begins its new financial year, with cuts concentrated in its commercial sales business and Xbox division. Around 1,600 Xbox employees are affected immediately, with plans to eliminate roughly 20 percent of Xbox jobs by year-end; Microsoft is also selling off four Xbox studios. The company framed the cuts as a response to a shifting technology landscape driven by AI, though its chief people officer stressed the eliminated roles are not being directly replaced by AI. Microsoft had attempted to soften the impact through a voluntary retirement program, with over 30 percent of eligible employees participating.
quote: "I also want to be direct that the roles eliminated today are not being replaced by AI." — Amy Coleman, Executive Vice President and Chief People Officer, Microsoft

title: Station F ramps up as a launchpad for Europe’s hottest AI startups
summary: Station F, the Paris-based startup hub founded by Xavier Niel, is launching its second batch of the F/ai accelerator program in September 2026, targeting a handful of AI-focused startups with a goal of reaching €1 million in revenue within six months. The program's first cohort of 20 AI startups collectively raised $34 million in pre-seed funding, with two teams winning major international competitions. The second cohort adds notable new corporate backers including ElevenLabs, Nebius, Rippling, OpenRouter, HubSpot, and GitHub. The initiative is explicitly designed to counter the perception that European AI founders must relocate to the U.S. to access top-tier networks and resources.
quote: "Today, if the founders here want to speak to people at this level, they all seem to think they need to go to the U.S. and join a program there. We actually want to show that you can stay here and do it from here." — Roxanne Varza, Director, Station F

title: Some of the nation’s rich are letting AI teach their kids
summary: Wealthy American families are paying tens of thousands of dollars annually to enroll their children in private schools that use AI tutors as the primary educational delivery method, with companies like Alpha School charging up to $75,000 per year for kindergarten. Silicon Valley venture capitalists are among the early adopters, framing traditional education as "broken" and viewing AI-led schooling as the fix. However, none of these companies share performance metrics, meaning there is no evidence the approach improves educational outcomes. Critics also raise concerns about AI's well-documented sycophancy and Alpha School's stated policy of keeping "hot-button social issues" out of the curriculum, which could exclude topics like slavery and women's rights.
quote: "We recognize that education is likely broken the way it is and there's going to be entrepreneurs that try to fix it… You want someone to be able to think on their feet and navigate the world, not necessarily a recitation of facts in a particular discipline." — Shaun Johnson, San Francisco-based venture capitalist

title: Amazon will stop accepting new customers for Mechanical Turk
summary: Amazon will close its Mechanical Turk crowdsourcing platform to new customers on July 30, 2026, effectively placing the 21-year-old service on life support. AWS stated the decision came after "careful consideration," with existing customers able to continue using the service but no new features planned. The platform, which once powered human-annotated data for AI training through SageMaker, had become increasingly compromised — a 2023 analysis found that 33–46% of workers were using large language models to complete tasks, undermining the very data quality that made it valuable for AI development. The shutdown signals the end of an era for a platform that was central to debates about crowdsourced labor and the human infrastructure behind AI products.

title: Midjourney wants Hollywood studios to reveal the details of their AI usage
summary: Midjourney is seeking to compel Disney, Universal, and Warner Bros. to disclose the full scope of their internal generative AI usage as part of discovery in ongoing copyright infringement litigation. The studios sued Midjourney for generating images of their copyrighted characters, but Midjourney argues that if the studios are themselves training AI on unlicensed content — even for internal purposes like storyboarding — that would demonstrate an industry-wide practice supporting its fair use defense. A judge had previously limited the studios' disclosure obligation to AI usage that produced consumer-facing content, and Midjourney's latest filing seeks to overturn that restriction. The outcome could have significant implications for how copyright law applies to AI training practices across the entertainment industry.

title: Alibaba reportedly bans employees from using Claude Code
summary: Alibaba has instructed employees to stop using Anthropic's Claude Code tool starting July 10, classifying it as high-risk software and directing staff toward its own Qoder tool instead. The ban follows reports that Anthropic had embedded functionality in a version of Claude Code capable of secretly identifying Chinese users, part of an effort to close loopholes allowing Chinese users to access Claude despite Anthropic's existing prohibition on use by Chinese companies. Anthropic's Thariq Shihipar acknowledged the feature was an experiment launched in March to combat unauthorized resellers and model distillation, adding that stronger mitigations have since been implemented. The episode highlights the growing tension between U.S. AI companies enforcing export-style restrictions and Chinese firms that have come to rely on their tools.
quote: "The team has landed stronger mitigations since then and we've actually been meaning to take this down for a while." — Thariq Shihipar, Anthropic

title: What is Mistral AI? Everything to know about the OpenAI competitor
summary: Mistral AI, the French AI company valued at roughly $13.8 billion after its September 2025 Series C, is positioning itself less as a ChatGPT rival and more as an enterprise and government AI partner following the Palantir playbook — deploying models and agent platforms directly within customer infrastructure. The company reported annual recurring revenue above $400 million in early 2026, up from $20 million a year prior, and claims it is on track to surpass $1 billion ARR. Mistral is also building out significant compute infrastructure, including a €4 billion data center investment strategy across France and Sweden and the planned launch of Mistral Compute, a European AI platform powered by Nvidia processors. CEO Arthur Mensch has signaled an IPO as the long-term goal and confirmed a new open-weight model is coming in summer 2026, with early access opening in July.
quote: "We exist to make sure that everyone gets access to the best AI systems, outside of centralized control exercised by states or corporations that feel the need to control in-fine deployment of AI." — Arthur Mensch, CEO, Mistral AI

title: The fanfiction community is at war with AI — and itself
summary: Fanfiction communities are mobilizing to detect and publicly shame writers using generative AI, centering on a new browser skin for Archive of Our Own (AO3) that identifies a coding artifact left when text is pasted directly from Anthropic's Claude into the platform. The tool reliably flags direct Claude-to-AO3 pastes but carries significant limitations: it misses works edited through Word or Google Docs first, cannot distinguish full AI generation from minor AI-assisted edits like spell-checking, and is easily evaded by future writers. The backlash has already ensnared at least one innocent writer whose editor used Claude without their knowledge, highlighting the risk of false accusations. The Verge notes that no universally reliable AI-text detection method currently exists, and AO3's own voluntary tagging system remains the most honest disclosure mechanism — though it depends on authors' transparency in an environment where honesty carries heavy social costs.
quote: "Fandom is a uniquely connective, collaborative space. It thrives on the human element and the creative spark which drives it and feeds off it." — @heatedrivalryai, Anonymous Account Creator

title: Anthropic wants to develop its own drugs
summary: Anthropic announced Claude Science, an AI workbench for scientists, and revealed plans to develop its own drugs, focusing on treatments for neglected diseases — making it one of the first major frontier AI companies to move beyond selling tools to pharma and actually enter drug development itself. The company has been building wet labs and actively hiring biologists and life sciences talent, including poaching candidates from Big Pharma and top academic institutions. Experts caution that the field remains far from delivering AI-designed drugs to patients, with regulatory approval likely at least a decade away for any new candidate, and significant experimental work — and spending — still required regardless of AI's role. Anthropic's move puts it in the unusual position of competing with the same drugmakers it is simultaneously selling software to.
quote: "It takes time to show experimentally that something's safe." — Matthew Todd, Professor of Drug Discovery, University College London

title: A behind-the-scenes look at Midjourney’s medical scanner leaves many questions unanswered
summary: Midjourney has released a behind-the-scenes video of its dunk-tank ultrasound scanner, a device the AI image-generation company plans to deploy in spas as a wellness product focused on body composition. The video, hosted by a Midjourney engineer, shows the hardware — described as ultrasound probes connected to off-the-shelf computers and Raspberry Pis — but fails to address the physics and imaging limitations that experts flagged when the project was first announced. The company is explicitly positioning the scanner as a wellness device rather than a diagnostic medical tool, allowing it to avoid FDA clearance and clinical trials, though the video continues to use medical language and frames the product around its potential clinical value. CEO David Holz cited the company's lack of outside investors as the reason it can pursue the project without constraint.
quote: "No one can tell me not to do it." — David Holz, CEO

title: Enterprises lost Claude Fable 5 for a few weeks. New data shows two-thirds had already built their hedge
summary: A VentureBeat Pulse survey of 145 enterprises conducted during the June 2026 Claude Fable 5 blackout — triggered by a U.S. export-control order that pulled the model offline for all users with no warning — found that two-thirds had already hedged their AI model strategy before the disruption hit. Fifty-one percent blend closed frontier models with open-weight models on their own infrastructure, and 16% are actively moving core workflows off closed APIs entirely. The survey also revealed a sharp governance gap: only 1 in 10 enterprises has automated monitoring to detect a failing production AI model, while 79% have already taken a financial or operational hit from unauthorized agentic AI activity by their own employees. Microsoft was named the most likely vendor to be downsized in the next 12 months, cited by 30% of respondents, ahead of OpenAI at 21% and Anthropic at 15%.
quote: "You can't lock in right now in one vendor and even one framework." — Brian Craig, Senior Director of Architecture, Liberty IT

title: Mark Zuckerberg tells staff that AI agents haven’t progressed as quickly as he’d hoped
summary: Mark Zuckerberg told Meta employees at an internal town hall that AI agent development has not accelerated as quickly as the company had hoped, acknowledging the expected benefits of its new AI-focused structure have not yet materialized. Earlier this year, Meta laid off roughly 8,000 employees — about 10% of its corporate workforce — and moved another 7,000 into AI-focused teams, including a unit called Agent Transformation. Zuckerberg admitted the cuts "were not as clean as they should have been" but expressed confidence that the company would begin seeing returns on its AI investments within the next three to six months. Meta is projected to spend up to $145 billion on AI infrastructure in 2026, making the slower-than-expected progress a significant strategic concern.

title: Canadians can once again access Anthropic’s Fable after US lifts ban
summary: The US government has lifted its export restrictions on Anthropic's Mythos and Fable AI models, roughly three weeks after the June 12 ban was imposed following reports that Amazon researchers found a way to bypass Fable 5's safeguards to identify software vulnerabilities. Anthropic confirmed that the jailbreak technique did not expose unique Mythos-level capabilities and that the same technique worked on other, less capable models as well. As part of its redeployment, Anthropic has introduced a new safety classifier for Fable 5 that blocks requests resembling the reported jailbreak and redirects them to its Opus 4.8 model. Canadian access is now in question, as the Canadian government had been granted access to Mythos through the Canadian Centre for Cyber Security just over a week before the ban, and BetaKit has sought clarification on whether that access has been restored.

title: Vancouver Police sergeant becomes first Canadian ALS patient to receive Neuralink brain implant
summary: Vancouver Police sergeant Lee Marten became the first Canadian ALS patient to receive a Neuralink brain implant following surgery at Toronto Western Hospital on May 20, 2026, making him the 26th patient globally to receive the device. The implant now allows Marten, whose ALS had left him with only limited hand mobility to operate his wheelchair, to control a phone or laptop using his thoughts alone. His surgery was also notable for pioneering a new technique in which the device's electrode strands are inserted through the brain's outer protective layer, the dura, without cutting it open — a refinement Neuralink says makes the procedure safer and more repeatable. The development signals continued expansion of Neuralink's clinical trial footprint into Canada, with Toronto Western Hospital positioning itself as a leading center for the procedure's most advanced iterations.
quote: "Getting a terminal diagnosis, you don't have much to look forward to. This is going to maybe improve my time that I have left, and allow me to be a kind of trailblazer for anyone else going through this." — Lee Marten, Vancouver Police Sergeant

title: Meta quietly launches vibe-coded gaming app Pocket
summary: Meta has launched a new app called Pocket, a creative platform that lets users generate small interactive apps and games using AI prompts. The app is a direct result of Meta's earlier acquisition of Gizmo, a vibe-coded gaming platform, and shares many similarities with that original product, including a discovery feed of user-created experiences. Pocket appeared on the App Store and Google Play on June 29, 2026, though Meta has not officially announced it, suggesting the product is still in an early experimentation phase. The launch extends Meta's broader push to mainstream AI creation tools, alongside its existing AI image, video, and social platform offerings.

title: Anthropic is discussing a new custom chip with Samsung
summary: Anthropic is in discussions with Samsung to explore a collaboration on a custom AI chip, according to a report from The Information. The company has not yet determined the chip's intended use, server integration, or performance specifications. The move follows competitor OpenAI's recent announcement of a custom inference chip called Jalapeño, developed with Broadcom, and reflects a broader industry push to reduce dependence on Nvidia. Anthropic noted that its compute strategy will continue to rely on a diversified hardware stack including chips from Google, Amazon, and Nvidia.

title: OpenAI proposed donating 5% of its equity to a US sovereign wealth fund
summary: OpenAI CEO Sam Altman has proposed donating 5% of the company's equity to a U.S. sovereign wealth fund, with the expectation that other AI companies would make similar contributions, according to the Financial Times. The move is framed as a way to secure goodwill with the Trump administration and reduce political friction, following earlier public comments by President Trump about AI companies sharing stakes with the American public. OpenAI has been developing the idea further, with a recent policy paper proposing a public wealth fund that would invest in AI labs and distribute returns directly to citizens. The talks remain preliminary and would likely require congressional approval; separately, Sen. Bernie Sanders has proposed a more aggressive version — a one-time 50% tax on AI company stock deposited into a similar public fund.
quote: "Returns from the Fund could be distributed directly to citizens, allowing more people to participate directly in the upside of AI-driven growth, regardless of their starting wealth or access to capital."  — OpenAI, Industrial Policy for the Intelligence Age policy paper

title: Trump gets OpenAI to offer US 5% stake, far lower than Sanders’ target
summary: OpenAI CEO Sam Altman is in active talks with the Trump administration about the US acquiring a 5 percent stake in the company, with Google and Meta also approached about similar arrangements. The proposal draws on OpenAI's earlier suggestion of creating a sovereign AI wealth fund modeled on Alaska's Permanent Fund, giving the public a financial stake in AI-driven growth. Senator Bernie Sanders has rejected the 5 percent figure as far too small, pushing instead for a 50 percent one-time tax on leading AI firms' stock that he estimates would yield $7 trillion for public benefit programs. The negotiations reflect broader pressure on AI companies to address growing public hostility toward AI, with polls showing 70 percent of Americans opposed to AI data centers in their communities.
quote: "The public has got to have a significant seat at the table to make sure that terrible things do not happen to ordinary people, and that in fact, AI benefits ordinary people, not hurts them." — Bernie Sanders, Senator (I-Vt.)

title: Musk’s X poses “serious risk to Americans’ privacy,” advocates warn FTC
summary: A coalition of 15 privacy and consumer protection advocates, including the EFF and EPIC, filed comments with the FTC on July 2, 2026, urging the agency to reject X's petition to terminate a 2022 consent order requiring independent audits of its data practices. X argued the order is burdensome and unnecessary given its rebranding and restructuring under Musk, but advocates countered that the platform's conduct — including a 2.8 billion record data leak, Grok generating CSAM, and training AI on hundreds of millions of user posts without meaningful consent — demonstrates heightened risk, not reduced oversight. Advocates also dismissed X's claim that GDPR compliance substitutes for FTC monitoring, noting X is currently under GDPR investigation for unauthorized data collection to train Grok. The coalition warned that terminating the order would strip the FTC of its most effective deterrence tools against what they call a "known repeat offender."
quote: "X Corp.'s foray into artificial intelligence development should prompt greater FTC oversight of the company's privacy practices, not less," advocates said.

title: Microsoft launches its own AI deployment company with $2.5 billion commitment
summary: Microsoft is launching Microsoft Frontier Company, a new operating business backed by $2.5 billion and 6,000 engineers, focused on delivering successful enterprise AI deployments using Microsoft's existing tools. The venture closely resembles the Forward Deployed Engineer model being adopted across the industry, though Microsoft's Commercial Business CEO Judson Althoff distanced the effort from that label. The announcement comes just days after Amazon Web Services committed $1 billion to a similar AI deployment venture, and follows comparable moves by OpenAI and Anthropic. Microsoft's existing Fortune 500 relationships give it a significant head start, with early partners including the London Stock Exchange Group, Unilever, and Land O'Lakes.
quote: "This goes beyond what has been labeled as Forward-Deployed Engineering, and will be the largest, most capable, outcome-driven engineering organization in the industry." — Judson Althoff, Commercial Business CEO, Microsoft

title: Google’s AI buildout drove 37% increase in electricity use in 2025
summary: Google's electricity consumption rose 37% in 2025 — the largest annual increase in the company's history — driven by its AI data center buildout, with total usage up more than 250% since 2019. Data centers alone consumed over 42 million megawatt-hours, rivaling the electricity use of entire countries such as New Zealand and Denmark. Despite the surge, Google reported a 2% reduction in operational carbon emissions by purchasing record amounts of clean energy, though total "ambition-based emissions" still grew 18% due to a carbon-heavy Asia-Pacific supply chain. The company acknowledged its AI infrastructure is "accelerating faster than the grid is decarbonizing," and faces scrutiny over a potential $40 billion Texas data center campus that may draw power from a 933-megawatt natural gas plant without carbon capture.
quote: "While the path to achieving our climate ambitions will not be linear—given our AI infrastructure buildout is currently accelerating faster than the grid is decarbonizing—we remain focused on scaling abundant and affordable clean power globally and progressing technological innovations that drive down emissions across our operations and the broader industry." — Google, Sustainability Report

title: OpenAI floats giving Trump administration 5 percent cut of AI boom
summary: OpenAI CEO Sam Altman has proposed giving the US government a 5 percent ownership stake in the company as a way to ease tensions with the Trump administration and reduce public backlash against AI, according to the Financial Times. Based on OpenAI's latest valuation of $852 billion, that stake would be worth roughly $42.6 billion. Altman reportedly first pitched the idea to Trump early last year and has suggested the proposal would extend to other US AI companies as well. The move comes as the Trump administration has taken an unusually interventionist stance toward AI, including designating competitor Anthropic a Pentagon supply chain risk and placing unexpected export controls on its latest models.

title: Z.ai launches ZCode to challenge Cursor, Claude Code and GitHub Copilot in AI coding
summary: Z.ai, the Beijing-based lab formerly known as Zhipu AI, has launched ZCode, a free agentic coding environment built around its GLM-5.2 model, entering direct competition with Cursor, Claude Code, and GitHub Copilot. GLM-5.2 is a 744-billion-parameter model trained entirely on Huawei chips, priced at up to 82 percent less than Anthropic's comparable Claude Opus 4.8, and released as open-source under the MIT license. The launch is strategically timed: it coincided with the U.S. government's June 12 directive blocking foreign-national access to Anthropic's most advanced models, a move that was only reversed on June 30 and that exposed what analysts are calling "sovereign access risk" in enterprise AI procurement. Z.ai's market capitalization crossed HK$1 trillion following the launch, and JPMorgan projects over 534 percent revenue growth for the company in 2026.

title: Indian tech tycoon bets $30M of his own money to build AI alternative to Microsoft Office
summary: Serial entrepreneur Bhavin Turakhia is personally investing $30 million to build Neo, an enterprise AI work platform designed from scratch to compete with incumbents like Microsoft and Google. Launched internally in April 2026 and built in just three months with heavy AI assistance, Neo combines project management, documents, file storage, and AI into a single product, targeting mid-sized businesses in technology, consulting, and professional services. Turakhia argues that legacy workplace software cannot be meaningfully upgraded with AI bolt-ons — it must be rebuilt entirely — and that Neo's model-agnostic architecture gives enterprises flexibility incumbents cannot match. The Bengaluru-based startup, currently at 45 employees, plans to reach 100 by year-end as it begins its external rollout.
quote: "Even if we end up with 2% to 5% market share, that's larger than anything I've built so far." — Bhavin Turakhia, Founder, Neo

title: Checking in on Canada’s digital sovereignty
summary: BetaKit is launching a second weekly newsletter edition focused on global tech from a Canadian perspective, coinciding with Canada Day 2026. The publication frames the expansion around its sustained reporting on Canadian digital sovereignty, covering topics including AI strategy, defence tech, and homegrown alternatives to US platforms. Canada's new AI strategy directs support for open-source research built with allied nations, and Cohere co-founder Nick Frosst has argued that sovereign AI requires genuine autonomy and agency. Minister of AI and Digital Innovation Evan Solomon is featured discussing Canada's refreshed AI strategy and social media regulations.
quote: "Either we build the innovation here with our SMEs and our entrepreneurs … or we buy it from somewhere else. And either we make the rules here, or we follow someone else's. Those are the choices we've got as a country." — Evan Solomon, Minister of AI and Digital Innovation

title: SpaceX has an AI device prototype, and it sure sounds phone-ish
summary: SpaceX has shown investors a prototype of a "handset-like" AI device described as sleeker and slimmer than an iPhone, according to The Wall Street Journal, though Elon Musk has denied the report. The device is said to run a proprietary operating system and integrate technology from xAI, which SpaceX acquired earlier this year, positioning it as a native AI interface independent of Google's Android or Apple's platforms. The move would put SpaceX in direct competition with OpenAI, which is developing an AI device with former Apple design chief Jony Ive. The competitive landscape for standalone AI hardware remains uncertain, given the high-profile failures of Humane and Rabbit in the space.

title: Cloudflare’s new policy pushes AI companies to pay for publishers’ content
summary: Cloudflare announced that starting September 15, 2026, its default settings will block "mixed-use" web crawlers — those blending search, AI agent use, and model training — from pages that host ads, unless site owners opt out. The policy affects new customers, new sites, and all existing free-tier customers, and is designed to pressure AI companies into separating their crawlers by purpose. Cloudflare also expanded its Pay Per Crawl marketplace into a Pay Per Use model, allowing publishers to earn revenue when their content generates value in AI outputs, not just when it is fetched — with initial partners Ceramic.ai and You.com. The move reflects Cloudflare's broader push to establish a sustainable commercial ecosystem as bot traffic has already surpassed human traffic online, ahead of earlier projections.
quote: "Now that the majority of traffic on the Internet is non-human, we must go further and act faster so that a sustainable ecosystem can emerge." — Matthew Prince, Co-founder and CEO, Cloudflare

title: After spooking Trump into safety testing, Anthropic AI models get global release
summary: The US has lifted export restrictions on Anthropic's Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models, roughly three weeks after the Trump administration designated them national security risks over fears that adversaries could exploit their cybersecurity capabilities to attack US infrastructure. Fable 5 is now available globally, while Mythos 5 — described as able to find and exploit software vulnerabilities more effectively than all but the most skilled human experts — is being made available to vetted domestic and international partners through a program called Glasswing. In exchange, Anthropic agreed to expand its government partnership, launch a HackerOne red-teaming program, and deploy a 24/7 internal team to monitor jailbreak threats. The episode is pushing both Anthropic and the Trump administration toward a more formal public-private framework for AI safety testing, with Anthropic now calling on Congress to legislate standards for all frontier model developers.
quote: "Over the past two weeks, we have worked closely with Anthropic to analyze and approve Fable 5 to ensure alignment across the US Government and strengthen America's leadership in AI." — Howard Lutnick, Commerce Secretary

title: LLMs are stuck in a groupthink groove. This startup is trying to get them out.
summary: Australian startup Springboards has developed an LLM called Flint, built on Alibaba's open-source Qwen 3 model, that is trained to produce more varied responses to open-ended questions than mainstream models like ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini. Research presented at NeurIPS found that 25 different LLMs converged on nearly identical answers when asked the same open-ended questions, suggesting the homogeneity stems from similar training data and methods across the industry. Springboards' approach targets specific decision points in a model's output — such as the moment it selects a destination or brand name — injecting controlled randomness rather than raising temperature settings globally, which tends to make models incoherent. The company is currently targeting advertisers and marketers, but argues the limitation affects any use case involving brainstorming or creative exploration.
quote: "You can't really create something boundary-breaking with tools that pull you back to the average." — Maximilian Weigl, Cofounder and Chief Strategy Officer, Uncommon

title: Venice AI becomes a unicorn with $65M Series A as its privacy-first AI platform takes off
summary: Venice AI, a privacy-focused platform offering access to more than 200 AI models, has raised a $65 million Series A at a $1 billion valuation, its first external funding round. The round was led by crypto-focused venture firm Dragonfly, with participation from Coinbase Ventures and North Island Ventures. The company, founded by bitcoin advocate Erik Voorhees, already claims profitability with annualized run-rate revenues exceeding $70 million, over 3 million active users, and 1.7 million API calls per day. Venice differentiates itself by hosting uncensored open-source models on its own infrastructure, encrypting user data client-side, and routing queries through external proxies so no user data is stored on its systems, positioning itself as a privacy-respecting alternative to mainstream AI chatbots.
quote: "We're optimizing for freedom and actually respecting users as adults, which is, I think, rare these days." — Erik Voorhees, CEO, Venice AI

title: Gemini Spark, Google’s agentic assistant, is now available on Mac
summary: Google has launched Gemini Spark, its agentic AI assistant, for macOS as a beta available to Google AI Ultra subscribers in the U.S., adding it to the existing Gemini desktop app. The Mac launch enables Spark to work with local files, compete more directly with desktop AI agents like Claude Desktop and Microsoft Copilot, and sets the stage for future cross-device task delegation between phones and desktops. Google has also expanded Spark's integrations to include Google Keep, Google Tasks, and third-party apps such as Canva, Dropbox, Instacart, OpenTable, and Zillow Rentals, enabling real-world tasks like reserving tables, ordering groceries, and booking apartment tours. Additional updates include real-time topic tracking for events like sports scores and breaking news, and support for custom Model Context Protocol (MCP) to let users connect their own apps directly into Spark.

title: Meta, like SpaceX, looks to turn excess AI compute into cash
summary: Meta is developing plans to launch a cloud infrastructure business called Meta Compute, selling access to AI compute power and hosted AI models — including its recently launched closed-weight model, Muse Spark — putting it in direct competition with AWS, Google Cloud, and Microsoft Azure. The move mirrors SpaceX/xAI's recent strategy of leasing out data center capacity to companies like Anthropic, Google, and Reflection AI. Meta has committed to spending $182.9 billion on AI infrastructure in coming years, including a data center in Ohio described by Zuckerberg as the size of Manhattan, but has not demonstrated material standalone revenue from its AI models or services. The parallel strategies of Meta and SpaceX suggest the dominant winners of the AI era may be those who own large-scale compute infrastructure rather than those who build the best models.

title: AI minister says Ottawa considering taking the lead on AI investment rounds
summary: Canada's federal AI minister Evan Solomon indicated the government is exploring taking a lead investor role in Canadian AI company funding rounds through its $500 million Canadian Tech Growth Fund. Speaking on the BetaKit Podcast, Solomon said the fund could offer debt and equity optionality, giving Ottawa a stake in AI startups while providing safeguards for taxpayer money. The move marks a significant shift from Canada's historical preference for loans and grants over equity investments in startups. Proponents argue a government anchor investor could spur private capital confidence, though critics including the Council of Canadian Innovators have flagged a lack of detail on how the investment strategy would actually work.
quote: "There's often a lack of a lead investor, we could play that role." — Evan Solomon, AI Minister

title: Minister Evan Solomon on Canada’s AI strategy, sovereignty, and social media
summary: Canada's AI Minister Evan Solomon sat down with BetaKit for an hour-long interview covering Canada's new AI strategy, a ban on Anthropic models, and new federal legislation on social media, privacy, and tech policy. The conversation touches on digital sovereignty, with Solomon framing Canada's choices as either building its own AI innovation or buying from others, and either setting its own rules or following someone else's. The interview follows BetaKit's May town hall with Solomon and comes after a wave of significant Canadian policy developments that fall under his ministerial purview. The episode underscores Canada's ambition to assert greater control over its AI future amid growing global uncertainty.
quote: "Either we build the innovation here with our entrepreneurs, or we buy it from someone else. And either we make the rules here, or we follow someone else's. Those are the choices we've got as a country." — Evan Solomon, Minister of AI, Canada

title: Google built a great smart speaker, but Gemini isn’t ready for it
summary: Google's new $99.99 Home Speaker — its first new smart speaker in six years — earns praise for its compact hardware design, solid sound, and smart home integration as a Matter controller and Thread border router. But Gemini for Home, the AI assistant powering it, proves slow, unreliable, and occasionally confidently wrong, undermining the device's core value proposition. Reviewer Jennifer Pattison Tuohy at The Verge found that complex multi-room commands took up to 10 seconds, compared to under three seconds for Amazon's revamped Alexa Plus. Several advanced Gemini features, including Gemini Live, are locked behind a $10-per-month Google Home Premium subscription.

title: Trump drops restrictions on Anthropic’s Mythos and Fable models
summary: The U.S. has lifted export restrictions on Anthropic's Mythos and Fable AI models, which had effectively cut off public access to the models since June 12, 2026, when they were added to the government's list of export-restricted technologies. In exchange, Anthropic agreed to proactively detect and address security risks, work with the government on protocols for future model releases, and report malicious activity — commitments the company had largely already made voluntarily. Cybersecurity experts had been skeptical of the original restrictions, viewing them as leverage against Anthropic whose executives had publicly criticized the Trump administration's approach to AI. The lifting of the ban comes as Asian competitors including Fugu and Tulongfeng began releasing models approaching Mythos-level capabilities, pressuring the U.S. to allow American AI to compete globally.

title: Wayve launches $85M employee tender offer at $8.5B valuation
summary: Wayve, a U.K.-based autonomous driving AI startup, has launched an $85 million employee tender offer at its existing $8.5 billion valuation, allowing staff to sell portions of their vested equity. The offer is led by existing and new investors and follows the company's $1.2 billion Series D raised in February 2026, backed by Eclipse, Balderton, SoftBank Vision Fund 2, Microsoft, Nvidia, and Uber. The move reflects a broader trend of AI startups using tender offers as a retention tool rather than waiting for a traditional exit event. Wayve, which has grown to 1,200 employees, is targeting robotaxi pilot launches with Uber later in 2026 and plans to integrate its AI software into Nissan's driver-assist systems starting in 2027.

title: Anthropic’s long-sidelined Fable 5 is greenlit to return
summary: Anthropic announced it will restore global access to Claude Fable 5 after the U.S. Department of Commerce lifted export controls that had been in place for weeks following a Trump administration ultimatum over jailbreak vulnerabilities. The company addressed the core security concern by training a new safety classifier that blocks the specific jailbreak technique flagged by Amazon researchers in over 99% of cases. To prevent future conflicts, Anthropic committed to pre-release government access for national security-relevant models, rapid jailbreak information sharing, and collaboration with Amazon, Microsoft, Google, and other partners on a voluntary industry-wide framework for assessing AI jailbreak severity. The episode comes as Anthropic prepares for an IPO and continues navigating a contentious relationship with the Trump administration over a supply chain risk designation.
quote: "We've received notice that the Department of Commerce has lifted export controls on Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5. We'll begin restoring access tomorrow, and will share an update soon." — Anthropic, Company Statement

title: Morgan Stanley cut its riskiest reconciliation job in half — by making its agents less autonomous
summary: Morgan Stanley has deployed an agentic AI system called FIXR in its profit and loss reconciliation workflow, cutting the process from up to six hours down to two to three hours per book and saving roughly 1,500 controller hours per week across approximately 100 staff globally. The system works by keeping humans tightly in the loop — controllers review and approve every recommendation, and their decisions are iteratively converted into fixed, repeatable rules the system can apply autonomously over time. Counterintuitively, Morgan Stanley found that deliberately limiting model autonomy and replacing learned patterns with deterministic rules made the system more reliable, cheaper to run, and easier to govern. The deployment offers a replicable enterprise template: establish process intelligence first, prove the use case, then extend it — rather than chasing bespoke model fine-tuning.
quote: "One of our strong principles in our AI governance generally is that there always has to be human accountability, even if there's a degree of automation." — Todd Johnson, Managing Director, Morgan Stanley

title: OpenClaw is finally available on Android and iOS
summary: OpenClaw, the free open-source AI agent platform that gained widespread attention earlier this year, has launched mobile apps on both iOS and Android. Users can connect their phones to the OpenClaw Gateway, a routing layer that links requests to AI agents and their associated tools, enabling on-the-go use of automated agents for tasks ranging from coding to meal planning. The platform rose to prominence partly through the MoltBook spectacle — a social media site purportedly run entirely by AI agents, later revealed to involve humans impersonating agents — and creator Peter Steinberger subsequently joined OpenAI in February 2026. The mobile launch signals continued mainstream expansion of agentic AI into everyday consumer devices.

title: Claude Science is Anthropic’s newest flagship product
summary: Anthropic has launched Claude Science, a standalone AI product designed to support scientific research the way Claude Code supports software engineering, now available to all paid Claude subscribers. The tool can autonomously execute research tasks, interface with scientific software and databases in computational biology and drug development, manage compute clusters, and prioritize reproducibility. Anthropic is also using Claude Science to conduct its own drug discovery research into neglected diseases, both to advance science and to better understand the product's real-world performance. The launch positions Anthropic as a serious contender in AI for science, a space long dominated by Google DeepMind — and is bolstered by the recent departure of Nobel-linked researcher John Jumper from DeepMind to Anthropic.
quote: "Our mission is to develop AI that serves humanity's long-term well-being, and we believe that by far the greatest opportunity to do that is in the life sciences." — Eric Kauderer-Abrams, Anthropic's head of life sciences

title: The DeepMind trio who built a poker AI are now making money for quant hedge funds
summary: EquiLibre Technologies, a Prague-based AI lab founded by three former DeepMind researchers who built the first AI to beat professionals at poker, has raised a Series A that values the company at $500 million. The round was led by Creandum in what the firm described as the largest single investment it has ever made. EquiLibre applies reinforcement learning — the same technique used in its poker AI — to stock trading, and in partnership with quant firm Tower Research Capital its algorithms now trade billions in daily volume across the S&P 500 and Nasdaq. The startup claims a perfect record of zero negative months since inception and is now planning to build one of the largest compute clusters in Central and Eastern Europe.
quote: "The nice thing about trading and markets is that the scoring is super simple: how much money did the agent make?" — Martin Schmid, CEO, EquiLibre Technologies

title: New attack provides one more reason why AI browsers are a bad idea
summary: Security researchers at LayerX have demonstrated a novel attack called "BioShocking" that tricks AI browsers into abandoning their safety guardrails by immersing them in a false reality where normal rules no longer apply. The technique uses a puzzle that rewards incorrect answers — such as 2 + 2 = 5 — to disorient the embedded LLM, after which it can be prompted to extract sensitive data like code from private repositories or credentials from password managers. The attack worked across a wide range of AI browsers including ChatGPT Atlas, Comet, Fellou, Genspark, Sigma, and the Claude Chrome plugin. The findings underscore a structural risk in AI browsers: by merging web content display with autonomous action-taking on behalf of users, they create a much larger attack surface than traditional browsers.
quote: "But if we can trick the AI into changing its context into fantasy—where the rules are made up and anything goes—then it can behave as though its actions don't have real world consequences." — Roy Paz, Researcher, LayerX

title: Google introduces a faster, cheaper image generator with Nano Banana 2 Lite
summary: Google has launched Nano Banana 2 Lite, a new AI image generator that produces images in four seconds at $0.034 per 1,000 images, making it significantly faster and cheaper than its predecessor. The model is optimized for high-volume workflows and replaces the original Nano Banana, which Google now calls its legacy model. Google also announced a wider release of Gemini Omni Flash, priced at $0.10 per second of video output, and debuted Omni Product Studio, a demo app that converts static images into cinematic e-commerce videos. The releases signal continued competitive pressure on AI image and video generation pricing, with Google positioning its lineup across speed, cost, and capability tiers.

title: Google's new Nano Banana 2 Lite image model is its fastest and cheapest yet
summary: Google DeepMind has launched Nano Banana 2 Lite (officially Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite Image), a new AI image generation model that produces images in roughly 4 seconds compared to 20 seconds for the standard Nano Banana 2. The model trades some quality for speed, with known weaknesses in text rendering, infographics accuracy, and character consistency across iterations. Pricing is set at $0.034 per 1,000 images via API, with output token costs at $1.50 per million — half the rate of Nano Banana 2 and one-eighth that of Nano Banana Pro. The launch represents a significant cost and speed signal in the AI image generation market, with Google positioning the model for rapid prototyping and high-volume use cases, though critics note it may accelerate the spread of low-quality AI-generated content.

title: Nvidia competitor Etched hits $5B valuation, $1B in sales for AI chip
summary: AI chip startup Etched announced $1 billion in contract orders for its inference-focused chip systems, along with a previously undisclosed $500 million funding round closed in December 2025 at a $5 billion post-money valuation, bringing total funding to $800 million. The company, founded in 2022 by Harvard dropouts Gavin Uberti and Robert Wachen, designs specialized chips and full systems — called frontier inference clusters — aimed at running AI inference faster and more cheaply than general-purpose GPUs. TSMC successfully manufactured Etched's chip earlier in 2026, and the company is now testing the product with customers. The announcement signals growing investor conviction that inference-optimized silicon is a major competitive battleground, with backers including Jane Street, Two Sigma, and angel investors such as Geoffrey Hinton and Andrej Karpathy.

title: Anthropic launches Claude Sonnet 5 as a cheaper way to run agents
summary: Anthropic has released Claude Sonnet 5, a mid-tier model priced at $2 per million input tokens and $10 per million output tokens through August 31, 2026, positioning it as a cheaper alternative to flagship models for agentic tasks. The model delivers performance close to Anthropic's top-tier Opus 4.8 — scoring 63.2% on agentic coding benchmarks versus Opus 4.8's 69.2% — while undercutting the price of OpenAI's GPT-5.5 and Google's Gemini 3.1 Pro. Sonnet 5 becomes the default model for free and Pro Claude plans immediately. The launch signals that agentic capability is now a baseline expectation across price tiers, with competition shifting toward cost and reliability rather than raw capability.
quote: "It can make plans, use tools like browsers and terminals, and run autonomously at a level that, just a few months ago, required larger and more expensive models." — Anthropic, Blog Post

title: Acti puts AI agents directly into your smartphone keyboard
summary: Singapore-based startup Acti has launched an agentic keyboard for iOS and Android that embeds AI directly into the interface users type from across all their apps, rather than requiring them to switch to a separate AI chatbot. Powered by Google's Gemini models, the keyboard can perform real-time tasks — such as surfacing local restaurant recommendations or live stock prices — directly within messaging, email, and social media apps. A key feature called Skills lets users program single keys to trigger multistep AI actions, described in plain language rather than code, with over 1,000 Skills built by early testers in under two weeks. The company, founded by a former Baidu executive who grew Facemoji Keyboard to 300 million daily active users, has raised $5.3 million in seed funding led by BITKRAFT Ventures.
quote: "Text was no longer just something people typed; it had become a carrier of intent." — Young Wang, Founder and CEO, Acti

title: Netflix is using an AI-generated Gene Wilder voice in its Willy Wonka reality show
summary: Netflix is using an AI-generated voice of Gene Wilder for its upcoming reality competition show "Wonka's The Golden Ticket," premiering September 23, 2026. The voice was created in partnership with ElevenLabs, with consent from Wilder's family, continuing a trend of Netflix using AI audio to recreate deceased celebrities — including Michael Caine and Stan Lee. The show follows 12 golden ticket winners competing over roughly a week, with a two-part finale on September 30. The project signals growing mainstream use of AI voice cloning for high-profile entertainment productions, raising broader questions about the commercialization of deceased performers' likenesses.

title: Anthropic’s Claude Science bets on workflow, not a new model, to win over scientists
summary: Anthropic launched Claude Science, an AI workbench designed to give researchers a single environment for computational work, connecting to more than 60 scientific databases with prebuilt toolkits for fields like genomics, protein structure, and chemistry. Rather than a new or specialized model, the product runs existing Claude models and competes on workflow integration — a deliberate contrast to OpenAI's GPT-Rosalind, a fine-tuned biology model gated behind enterprise qualification, and Google DeepMind's Gemini for Science, which bundles proprietary foundational models like AlphaFold. The launch reflects Anthropic's broader strategy of owning vertical workflow layers — as Claude Code has done for software development — rather than competing solely on raw model capability. Claude Science is available in beta to Pro, Max, Team, and Enterprise subscribers, with Anthropic also offering up to $30,000 in credits for up to 50 graduate and postdoctoral research projects through July 15, 2026.

title: Trump's plan to redesign every .gov website leads to AI-designed horrors
summary: A year after Trump created the National Design Studio (NDS) by executive order to overhaul 27,000 government websites in three years using AI, the initiative has produced little beyond a few dozen mostly single-page sites, several privacy law violations, and widespread accessibility failures.
