summary: OpenAI has announced "Patch the Planet," a new initiative pairing its AI security tools with engineers from Trail of Bits to help open source project maintainers identify and fix vulnerabilities in their code. The program positions OpenAI's Codex Security and related tools as a force multiplier for understaffed maintainers, with Trail of Bits engineers reviewing findings, developing patches, and building reusable security workflows before issues ever reach the maintainers themselves. The move comes as AI tools like Anthropic's Mythos have raised concerns about automating the discovery and exploitation of software bugs, making OpenAI's offensive-to-defensive pivot a notable competitive signal. Given that open source software underpins much of the commercial software industry — as illustrated by the widespread damage from the 2021 Log4j vulnerability — the initiative addresses a recognized and systemic risk.

title: Alibaba's AI video model rises to No. 2 in global rankings, as OpenAI's Sora and ByteDance's Seedance fall away
summary: Alibaba Cloud has launched HappyHorse 1.1, a major upgrade to its AI video generation model, now available via API on Alibaba Cloud Model Studio with enterprise pricing and a 40% launch discount. The release comes as the competitive landscape for AI video generation has narrowed significantly, with OpenAI having discontinued Sora due to financial unsustainability and ByteDance shelving the international rollout of Seedance 2.0 following copyright complaints from Hollywood studios. Alibaba is positioning HappyHorse 1.1 as an enterprise-ready product built for integration into marketing, advertising, and content production workflows. The departures of two major competitors leave a meaningful opening for Alibaba to capture enterprise customers who had been evaluating or already integrating those tools.

title: AI chipmaker Groq confirms $650M raise, re-staffs after Nvidia's $20B not-acqui-hire deal
summary: Groq, the AI inference chipmaker, has confirmed a $650 million funding round led by Disruptive and Infinitum, roughly six months after Nvidia licensed its language processing unit (LPU) technology and hired away founder/CEO Jonathan Ross and other key executives. With Nvidia now commercializing LPU technology under its own Groq 3 LPX branding, the remaining Groq team — now led by co-founder Doug Wightman as CEO — is pivoting to its neocloud inference business, which spans 13 data centers globally and serves over five million developers. The raise signals investor confidence that Groq can compete as an inference cloud provider even after ceding its core chip IP, though it faces intensifying competition in a rapidly evolving market. The development mirrors Scale AI's rebound after Meta's $14.3 billion not-acqui-hire, suggesting these deals, while disruptive, are not necessarily fatal to the remaining entity.

title: Nvidia wants to cut data center water use, but that's not the same as fixing AI's water problem
summary: Nvidia has announced a warm-water cooling system that it claims can eliminate virtually all on-site water consumption at data centers, with the coolant running in a closed loop that requires no replenishment. However, TechCrunch reports that this addresses only about a quarter to a third of AI data centers' total water footprint, since water consumed by electricity generation — particularly fossil fuel and hydropower plants supplying roughly half of data center power — is excluded from Nvidia's accounting. Natural gas plants use 1.17 liters of water per kilowatt-hour generated, coal plants use 2.2 liters, and the IEA projects fossil fuels will supply more than 40% of new electricity needed for data centers through 2030. Nvidia's sustainability chief called the water challenge "largely solved," but the broader picture shows the company's innovation addresses facility-level consumption while leaving the larger upstream water problem untouched.
quote: "The water consumption challenge for data centers is largely solved," — Josh Parker, Chief Sustainability Officer, Nvidia

title: AI is cursing renters with the promise of impossible homes
summary: Real estate brokers are increasingly using AI-powered "virtual staging" tools to digitally alter apartment listing photos, adding furniture, removing blemishes, and creating spaces that look dramatically different from reality. Renters in cities like New York report showing up to viewings only to find apartments far smaller, less updated, or missing features entirely compared to their listings. Some states are responding: California now requires disclosure when AI has been used to alter property images, and New York's secretary of state has warned that existing prohibitions on dishonest advertising apply to AI-enhanced listings. The trend is compounding an already difficult rental market, as prospective tenants must now scrutinize listings more carefully than ever while competing against other applicants for limited inventory.
quote: "Brokers are already so dishonest, and now they have, like, the lying machine in their pocket." — Joyce, New York City renter

title: Google DeepMind bets $75M on AI's future in Hollywood with A24 deal
summary: Google DeepMind has announced a $75 million investment in indie film studio A24, framing the deal as a "first-of-its-kind" partnership to develop AI tools for filmmaking. Under the arrangement, Google DeepMind will build AI features while receiving creative feedback and guidance from A24's filmmakers and artists. The move signals a broader industry trend, with Netflix acquiring Ben Affleck's AI-tools company InterPositive and Amazon's MGM Studios launching its own AI production unit. The deal reflects tech giants' push to embed AI into Hollywood's creative pipeline despite ongoing controversy over AI's role in filmmaking.
quote: "We believe the best way to develop tools that empower artists is to work directly with them." — Demis Hassabis, Google DeepMind co-founder and CEO

title: Three things to watch amid Anthropic's latest feud with the government
summary: The US government placed export controls on Anthropic's Fable AI model in June, forcing the company to revoke access to both Fable and its predecessor Mythos within hours, after Amazon CEO Andy Jassy reportedly warned officials the model posed national security risks — a notable intervention given Amazon's competing AI interests. The episode has prompted European leaders to call for AI sovereignty, but that path is complicated by the growing appeal of cheap, capable Chinese open-source models that no government can switch off. Leading cybersecurity experts have pushed back, arguing in an open letter that restricting access to Anthropic's models leaves the US more vulnerable, not less, since the models were being used to build defenses. The standoff is intensifying pressure on Congress to move beyond incremental AI rules and toward broader federal regulation, while raising the question of whether the White House will next target US companies that adopt Chinese AI models.
quote: "Wake-up call" — Bruno Retailleau, French politician

title: Amazon is testing Alexa+ in India with Hindi support
summary: Amazon is expanding its generative AI-powered Alexa+ assistant to India, inviting select users to beta-test a Hindi-language version. The company sent emails to customers asking them to register by June 22, noting the beta software may contain bugs and inaccuracies. Alexa+ is currently unavailable in India, with no confirmed launch date, though Amazon has already expanded the service this year to the U.K., Canada, Brazil, Mexico, Italy, and Germany. With over 600 million Hindi speakers in India, the move signals Amazon's push to capture a massive market of voice AI users who frequently mix Hindi and English.

title: SpaceX inks compute deal with Reflection AI, an open source AI lab
summary: Reflection AI, an open-source AI startup founded in 2024 by former Google DeepMind researchers, has signed a $6.3 billion compute deal with SpaceX, paying $150 million per month starting July 1, 2026 through 2029 for access to Nvidia GB300 chips at SpaceX's Colossus 2 data center near Memphis. The deal is Reflection's first major infrastructure commitment and is smaller than SpaceX's existing contracts with Anthropic ($1.25 billion/month) and Google ($920 million/month), confirming SpaceX as a major commercial AI compute provider. Reflection framed the deal as a signal of growing demand for open-weight AI models, citing the U.S. government's ban on Anthropic's closed models as a catalyst pushing nations and enterprises toward open alternatives. The Colossus facility, originally built by xAI before its merger with SpaceX, has become a key piece of AI infrastructure as SpaceX monetizes its chip holdings by renting capacity to frontier labs.

title: No Claude Fable 5? No problem: Sakana achieves frontier performance with new Fugu multi-model, auto synthesis system
summary: Sakana AI has launched Fugu, a multi-agent orchestration system that routes queries across a swappable pool of specialized AI models through a single OpenAI-compatible API, claiming frontier-level performance without relying on any single provider. CEO David Ha framed the launch as a direct response to Anthropic's June 12 decision to revoke public access to its most powerful models, Claude Mythos 5 and Claude Fable 5, following a U.S. government export control order. The product targets enterprises and nations seeking resilience against vendor lock-in and geopolitical supply disruptions. The launch signals a broader market shift: as export controls increasingly restrict access to top-tier models, orchestrated multi-model systems may emerge as a strategic alternative to dependence on any single frontier lab.
quote: "I believe that Orchestration Models are the next frontier, beyond bigger models." — David Ha, CEO and co-founder, Sakana AI

title: How Anthropic may have talked itself into an AI export ban
summary: The US government last week barred foreign nationals from accessing Anthropic's latest AI models, Mythos and Fable, in a move critics are linking directly to Anthropic's own sustained public warnings about AI risk. Financial Times analysis found that Anthropic used risk, regulation, and restriction-related language at roughly eight times the rate of rival OpenAI in 2026, with "risk" appearing 336 times in Anthropic communications versus 30 times at OpenAI. Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei had published a blog post days before the ban warning that Mythos posed "very real risks to cyber security" and potential disruption to financial and national security infrastructure. The episode is being watched closely by European and Silicon Valley leaders as an early signal of how the Trump administration intends to govern frontier AI models, with French President Macron calling it a clarifying moment for G7 allies.
quote: "You have an administration that has positioned itself as pro-innovation, has pushed to export advanced AI chips to China, and has criticized safety-focused regulation—and then it turns around and bans the most advanced US model." — Lennart Heim, independent AI policy researcher

title: The forecast factory: How an Alberta tech company simulates disasters before they happen
summary: Run With It Synthetics (RWI), an Edmonton-based Canadian AI company, builds high-fidelity synthetic environments — called the HoloDeck — that simulate disasters, pandemics, and infrastructure failures before they occur, enabling real-world strategic decision-making. Founded by Myrna and Dean Bittner, RWI uses agentic AI, neural networks, and GIS-based mapping to populate digital twins of real cities with over 100,000 AI-driven virtual people, complete with behavioural patterns and health predispositions. The company gained attention after simulating a 6.7-magnitude earthquake in Santa Clara at a 2019 IoT expo, then went on to model COVID-19's impact on Phoenix's power grid for the Electric Power Research Institute. The global strategic forecasting market, currently valued at around $49.5 billion USD, is projected to grow to $328.5 billion USD by 2033, driven by rising demand from governments, utilities, and healthcare organizations seeking to future-proof their systems.
quote: "Just how important it is for people to be able to understand and experience things that haven't happened." — Myrna Bittner, Co-founder, Run With It Synthetics

title: When the Trump administration cracks down on Anthropic, who benefits?
summary: The Trump administration issued an export control order forcing Anthropic to take its two newest AI models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5, offline over national security concerns, reportedly triggered after Amazon researchers allegedly found a way to bypass Fable 5's guardrails and Amazon CEO Andy Jassy raised concerns with the White House. Cybersecurity experts have signed an open letter calling the order dangerous, arguing it removes advanced cybersecurity capabilities from U.S. network defenders, while independent analysts suggest the security risks are not unique to Anthropic. The move is widely seen as retaliatory, given Anthropic's strained relationship with the Trump administration and an ongoing lawsuit, raising questions about whether rivals like OpenAI face less regulatory risk simply by maintaining better political relations. Paradoxically, the crackdown may boost Anthropic's public profile, as a prior clash with the administration correlated with a spike in Claude downloads and positioned the company as a more responsible alternative to competitors.
quote: "Anthropic has not had the best relationship with the Trump administration in a way that stands apart from the other leading AI labs." — Sean O'Kane, Reporter, TechCrunch

title: Beyond Siri: Practical AI Features Coming to iPhones in iOS 27
summary: Apple is embedding a range of AI-powered features throughout iOS 27, set for public release this fall, that go beyond the headline Siri overhaul to make everyday tasks more automatic. Key additions include receipt-based bill splitting via Apple Cash, an agentic password-updater that navigates websites and changes compromised credentials without user intervention, and a Call Context feature that surfaces relevant information — like airline confirmation codes — directly on the call screen by scanning on-device email. Other features include natural-language Calendar entry, AI-generated Shortcuts automations, smarter Home app notification grouping, and Safari tab organization by topic. Taken together, the update reflects Apple's strategy of embedding AI invisibly into existing apps and workflows rather than routing users through a standalone AI interface.

title: Encryption, spyware, and now Mythos: History shows why cyber export control doesn’t work
summary: The White House issued an export control directive ordering Anthropic to restrict access to its AI models Fable and Mythos, forcing the company to shut them down within roughly 90 minutes for all users outside the U.S. and for foreign nationals inside the country. The action was reportedly triggered by concerns over a South Korean telecom's access to Mythos and claims by Amazon's CEO of a discovered jailbreak, which Anthropic disputes as a narrow, already-patched issue. TechCrunch's Lorenzo Franceschi-Bicchierai argues this mirrors failed historical attempts to control encryption and spyware through export restrictions, pointing to the Crypto Wars of the 1990s and the Wassenaar Arrangement's uneven enforcement as evidence that such controls rarely contain dual-use cyber technologies. The outcome — whether the administration lifts the ban to keep U.S. AI companies competitive or entrenches a licensing regime — will set a precedent for how all AI labs navigate government oversight of frontier models.

title: Canadian surgical AI startup wins VivaTech pitch contest
summary: Montréal-based medical AI startup Reveal Life Science won first place at the OVHcloud Startup Challenge at VivaTech in Paris, beating dozens of international competitors with its AI platform that uses Raman spectroscopy to analyze tissue samples in seconds and determine whether they are cancerous. The Canadian company claims its technology has been validated across more than 700 surgeries and achieves 93 to 97 percent accuracy on brain tumours, with the ultimate goal of ensuring complete tumour eradication during surgery. Reveal has secured an FDA Breakthrough Device designation and recently announced a Canada-France partnership with surgical innovation centre IRCAD to bring its AI to breast cancer surgery. The win highlights Canada's growing AI startup talent on the international stage, with federal AI supercluster Scale AI leading the Canadian delegation to VivaTech.
quote: "Control of sensitive data is a strategic advantage." — Jean Le Bouthillier, Founder and CEO, Qohash

title: Canada bans “sophisticated deepfakes” of political figures
summary: Canada's Bill C-25, known as the Strong and Free Elections Act, has received royal assent, amending the Canada Elections Act to ban AI-generated deepfakes of political figures with the intent to mislead. The law makes it illegal to create or distribute voice or image imitations of candidates, party leaders, or electoral officials that are likely to be mistaken for real, while carving out an exception for parody and satire. The legislation closes a loophole in existing anti-impersonation rules that previously only covered direct false representation, not manipulated audio or video. The move comes amid warnings from Canadian national security officials that deepfakes are likely to influence Canada's next election, with fabricated videos of Prime Minister Mark Carney already circulating on Facebook promoting scams and disinformation.
quote: "Deepfakes, synthetic media, and AI-generated disinformation are reshaping the information environment in which people live and in which democratic debate takes place." — Canada's AI Strategy, Policy Document

title: Is the US government’s Anthropic ban accidentally helping the brand?
summary: The US government forced Anthropic to pull its two newest AI models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5, citing national security concerns after Amazon researchers allegedly found a way to bypass Fable 5's guardrails. Cybersecurity researchers have since signed an open letter calling the move dangerous, and Anthropic itself noted that the same jailbreaks exist in other models. A TechCrunch Equity podcast episode explores whether the ban reflects a genuine security concern or stems from the broader complicated relationship between Anthropic and the Trump administration. The hosts also discuss how the ban may paradoxically benefit Anthropic's brand and what the move means for developers and those watching the company's IPO.

title: The US banned Anthropic’s Fable 5 release, but the numbers don’t seem to care
summary: The US government banned Anthropic from releasing its two newest AI models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5, citing national security concerns after Amazon researchers allegedly found a way to bypass Fable 5's safety guardrails. Cybersecurity researchers have since signed an open letter criticizing the move as dangerous, while Anthropic pointed out that similar jailbreaks exist in other AI models. The ban raises questions about whether the action reflects genuine security concerns or political tensions between Anthropic and the Trump administration. Despite the regulatory action, the episode may paradoxically benefit Anthropic, and the company's financial trajectory appears largely unaffected.

title: Billionaire Ambani wants AI in every call, app, and home
summary: Reliance Industries, led by billionaire Mukesh Ambani, unveiled a sweeping suite of AI products at its annual shareholder meeting, including Jio Call Agent — an AI assistant embedded directly into phone calls for 500 million users — along with an AI-powered app, a smart home display called TeleFrame, and sector-specific tools for healthcare, education, agriculture, and small businesses. The services are designed to work across 22 Indian languages, positioning Reliance as a domestic AI champion in a market currently dominated by U.S. and Chinese companies. Reliance also confirmed Jio Platforms' board approved a draft prospectus for a long-awaited IPO. The announcements come as Reliance deepens AI partnerships with Google, Meta, and Nvidia, and follows a pledge to invest $110 billion in AI infrastructure.
quote: "India should not be a mere consumer of AI created elsewhere. It must become a creator, adopter, and a global leader in AI." — Mukesh Ambani, Chairman, Reliance Industries

title: The film about Sam Altman has been dropped by Amazon MGM
summary: Amazon MGM has dropped Artificial, a film directed by Luca Guadagnino about OpenAI CEO Sam Altman's firing and reinstatement in November 2023. The studio stated it believes the film will be better served by a different distributor and said it is working with the filmmaking team to find a new home for the project. The film stars Andrew Garfield as Altman, Monica Barbaro as OpenAI CTO Mira Murati, Ike Barinholtz as Elon Musk, and Yura Borisov as Ilya Sutskever. The decision may be linked to Amazon's close business relationship with OpenAI, including a $50 billion investment in the AI lab announced in February.

title: The CEO of Allbirds’ new AI biz has a plan, but no employees
summary: Allbirds, the shoe company, has completed its transformation into Smartbird, an AI infrastructure startup, after selling its footwear business for $43 million and raising $100 million from public markets. The company has appointed Nadia Carlsten, a former AWS executive and ex-CEO of European compute firm DCAI, as CEO, though she is currently its only employee and is building the leadership team from scratch. Smartbird aims to serve enterprises that require dedicated, sovereign AI compute clusters — particularly in pharma, energy, finance, and the public sector — positioning itself against internal IT projects rather than hyperscalers. The pivot also saw Allbirds abandon its public benefit corporation status, raising questions about the durability of such corporate sustainability commitments.
quote: "It wasn't, Let's just do AI, because it's AI, and it's hot." — Nadia Carlsten, CEO, Smartbird

title: The US says ASML’s top chip tool may be in China. ASML says it isn’t
summary: The US Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick has told senior ASML executives he believes one of the company's extreme ultraviolet (EUV) lithography machines — the only tools capable of printing the most advanced semiconductor patterns — may have ended up in China, a potential major breach of export controls in place since the first Trump administration. ASML denies any EUV machine has ever been in China, noting it tracks every machine ever shipped, and the Commerce Department has declined to share its purported evidence with Bloomberg or ASML itself. The stakes are enormous: ASML holds a complete monopoly on EUV lithography, making it the critical bottleneck in AI chip production, and every advanced processor from TSMC depends on its tools. The article also raises questions about conflicts of interest, noting that Commerce under Lutnick has invested $150 million in xLight, a startup working on technology that could challenge ASML's core EUV light-source monopoly.

title: Barret Zoph is out at OpenAI again after just five months
summary: Barret Zoph, OpenAI's head of enterprise AI sales, has departed the company just five months after rejoining in January 2026. Zoph had originally left OpenAI in fall 2024 to co-found Thinking Machines Lab with former OpenAI CTO Mira Murati, but departed that role abruptly in January 2026 following reports of alleged misconduct involving an undisclosed relationship with a colleague. His return to OpenAI had been notable given the company's stated focus on enterprise and coding as key revenue drivers ahead of its planned IPO. OpenAI confirmed the departure, and Zoph posted a farewell message in the company's internal Slack channels.

title: Source: Elastic agrees to buy CRV-backed DeductiveAI for up to $85M
summary: Elastic has agreed to acquire DeductiveAI, an AI-powered software debugging startup, for up to $85 million. DeductiveAI, founded in 2023 and backed by CRV, Databricks Ventures, and others, uses AI to automatically detect and resolve software bugs, operating in the emerging AI site reliability engineering (AI SRE) sector. Elastic plans to integrate Deductive's technology into its observability platform to give customers tools to automatically monitor performance and resolve system failures in real time. The deal reflects a broader trend of established tech companies acquiring AI-native startups to embed agentic capabilities into their existing products.

title: Anthropic's Claude Code Artifacts update brings live, shared dashboards and interactive workspaces to enterprises
summary: Anthropic has launched an Artifacts feature for Claude Code on its Team and Enterprise subscription plans, enabling users to turn coding sessions into live, interactive, shareable HTML webpages. These Artifacts can display dashboards, app designs, or internal tools that update in real-time as Claude Code works autonomously or responds to user direction, with connected data sources and codebases reflected dynamically. The feature builds on Artifacts previously introduced to Anthropic's consumer web chatbot in mid-2024, but now integrates it directly into the Claude Code command-line interface and desktop app. The key advancement is bridging the gap between back-end engineering work and non-technical stakeholders, who can now view and monitor progress through a shareable URL without needing direct access to the development environment.

title: As China looms, Taiwan makes more drones for defense and the US military
summary: Taiwan is ramping up domestic drone production and international partnerships to bolster its defenses against a potential Chinese invasion, with the government proposing a $6.6 billion special budget to purchase over 208,000 coastal attack drones and related systems between 2026 and 2031. Taiwanese companies like Thunder Tiger are also expanding exports, with drone shipments reaching $115 million in just the first quarter of 2026, already surpassing all of 2025's total. Taiwanese firms are partnering with US companies including Anduril, Auterion, AeroVironment, and Shield AI to integrate AI and software capabilities into their hardware. Despite strong electronics and chipmaking foundations, Taiwan faces significant challenges including political gridlock over defense budgets, supply chain dependencies on Chinese components, and competition from dominant Chinese drone maker DJI.
quote: "For the international market, how do you persuade other foreign governments to use Taiwanese-made drones two or three times more expensive than DJI's?" — Ting-Wei Lin, Non-Resident Fellow at DSET

title: AI inference startup Baseten reportedly raising $1.5B months after its last mega-round
summary: Baseten, an AI inference startup, is reportedly close to finalizing a $1.5 billion funding round at a $13 billion valuation, according to the Wall Street Journal — just five months after raising a $300 million Series E at a $5 billion valuation. The deal, co-led by Spark Capital, Sands Capital, Altimeter Capital, and Wellington Management, would represent a 160% valuation increase in under half a year. Notably, the round is structured as a split-priced deal, with some investors coming in at $13 billion and others at $11 billion, a tactic used to inflate headline valuations. Founded in 2019, Baseten capitalizes on the AI inference boom by routing inference requests to optimal models — including cost-effective open source alternatives — to balance speed and cost.

title: Snap spins off AI video team into new company, Dotmo, due to costs
summary: Snap is spinning off an internal generative AI video team into a new independent company called Dotmo, which will focus on building AI models for interactive gaming experiences. Snap cited the high costs of running such work internally as a key driver, though it will retain an equity stake in Dotmo and license its technology to the new firm. Snap's CTO Bobby Murphy will serve as Dotmo's lead investor while continuing in his full-time role at Snap. The move is Snap's second major spinoff of 2026, following the earlier separation of its Specs smart glasses division, and reflects a broader cost-cutting period that included roughly 1,000 layoffs earlier this year.

title: OpenAI is bringing on some big guns in the lead-up to its IPO
summary: OpenAI is making two high-profile hires ahead of its anticipated IPO: Noam Shazeer, a foundational AI researcher and co-author of the landmark 2017 "Attention Is All You Need" paper who recently departed Google DeepMind, and Dean Ball, a former Trump White House AI policy official. Ball will lead a new "Strategic Futures" team reporting to Chief Strategy Officer Jason Kwon, focusing on catastrophic risk, AI governance, labor market impact, and the relationship between frontier labs and governments. The hires come as rival Anthropic faces a U.S. government export control ban on its latest models, highlighting the competitive advantage OpenAI appears to be building through political and policy access. The moves signal OpenAI's effort to strengthen both its technical talent and regulatory positioning as it approaches a public listing.
quote: "Almost by necessity, AI labs will have to lead on AI governance decisions." — Dean Ball, incoming leader of OpenAI's Strategic Futures team

title: Almost half of US singles feel negatively about AI in dating, Match says
summary: A Match Group survey of 1,000 U.S. singles aged 18 to 39 found that 47% hold negative views of AI's role in romantic contexts, with 40% saying they would refuse to date someone who uses an AI companion app — a figure rising to 51% among women aged 18 to 24. Despite this, 64% of respondents said they could see AI helping them in their dating journey, suggesting openness to AI as a practical tool rather than a romantic participant. The findings come as dating apps like Bumble, Tinder, and Hinge aggressively integrate AI features, from profile assistance to conversation prompts. The survey signals that users want AI to ease friction in dating but draw a firm line at AI replacing genuine human connection.

title: Amazon hopes to challenge Nvidia more directly by selling its AI chips
summary: Amazon Web Services is in early-stage talks to sell its homegrown Trainium AI chips directly to other companies for use in their data centers, a significant strategic shift for AWS, which has historically kept its chips exclusive to its own cloud platform. AWS CEO Andy Jassy suggested the move in his April shareholder letter, estimating that if Trainium were sold externally, it would represent a roughly $50 billion annual revenue run rate. The potential expansion into third-party chip sales would put Amazon in more direct competition with Nvidia, whose AI chip business is currently on a $326 billion revenue run rate. Key challenges include manufacturing constraints, TSMC capacity competition with Nvidia, and the risk of diverting chips from AWS's own already-oversubscribed customers.
quote: "There's so much demand for our chips that it's quite possible we'll sell racks of them to third parties in the future." — Andy Jassy, CEO, Amazon

title: AI data centers just got a government-mandated fast lane to the grid
summary: The Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) unanimously ordered six major U.S. grid operators to fast-track interconnection requests from AI data centers and other large electricity users, requiring them to demonstrate that data centers can connect to the transmission grid "in a timely and orderly manner." Grid operators must submit capacity reports within 30 days and defend or revise electricity rates within 60 days, while data centers will bear the costs of their own interconnection. The directive does not resolve the underlying shortage of generating capacity — grid connection requests for power plants already exceed the theoretical capacity of the existing fleet, and electricity wholesale rates have surged as much as 267% over five years. The orders come as AI-driven electricity demand is projected to nearly triple by 2035, straining grid operators that had grown accustomed to near-zero demand growth for two decades.

title: Microsoft 365 Copilot and LiteLLM hit by data-exfiltration and privilege-escalation flaws
summary: Two AI enterprise tools — Microsoft 365 Copilot and LiteLLM — were found to share a fundamental security flaw within days of each other in June 2026: both accept external input without enforcing trust boundaries. Varonis disclosed SearchLeak (CVE-2026-42824), a silent exfiltration chain in which a crafted microsoft.com URL causes Copilot to search a victim's mailbox and leak data via a Bing SSRF, requiring no plugins or additional user interaction. Separately, Obsidian Security published a three-CVE chain against LiteLLM that could elevate a default low-privilege user to admin and achieve remote code execution. The article frames these disclosures as a pattern and offers a five-check audit mapped to each vulnerability, with commands and board-level language for security teams to act on immediately.

title: Bernie Sanders unveils $7 trillion plan to give Americans control of AI industry
summary: Bernie Sanders has introduced legislation that would create a $7 trillion sovereign wealth fund by imposing a one-time 50 percent tax on the stock of the largest AI companies — those generating $200 million or more in annual AI revenue. The fund would distribute over $1,000 annually per American in dividends and fund programs like healthcare, education, and housing. A bipartisan seven-member Independent Commission for Democratic AI would oversee the fund and use voting shares to block corporate decisions harmful to the public. The plan faces significant opposition from AI industry leaders and appears unlikely to pass the Republican-controlled Congress without White House support, though Sanders frames it as a starting point for broader debate about public benefit from AI.
quote: "The benefits cannot simply go to the handful of wealthy corporations." — Bernie Sanders, U.S. Senator

title: ‘Queer Eye’s’ life coach Karamo Brown launches Kē, a wellness app featuring his AI digital clone
summary: Karamo Brown, known for his role on Netflix's "Queer Eye," has launched Kē, a wellness app that includes an AI digital clone of himself powered by AI startup Delphi. The app offers personalized fitness plans, nutrition guidance, meditation content, and a community section, but its standout feature is "AI Karamo," which lets users converse with a digital version of Brown in real-time using his voice, trained on his interviews, podcasts, and other media. The app costs $14.99/month after a 3-day free trial and is available on iOS and Android. The launch reflects a broader celebrity trend of embracing AI digital replicas, even as others in the industry raise concerns about unauthorized use of likenesses and the risk of users forming unhealthy attachments to celebrity chatbots.
quote: "If someone is struggling with a sensitive issue, it can direct them toward appropriate resources and remind them to seek support from real people in their lives… At the end of the day, this is meant to be a tool that helps people reflect, learn, and grow, and it's not a substitute for human connection." — Karamo Brown, Creator of Kē and Queer Eye life coach

title: Amazon employees say they’re facing termination for backing data center limits
summary: Three Amazon software engineers who testified at Seattle City Council hearings in support of data center regulations were called into impromptu HR meetings and threatened with potential termination, one week after their testimony and one day after the city passed a moratorium on large-scale data centers. The employees — members of Amazon Employees for Climate Justice — filed a legal complaint with the Seattle Office for Civil Rights alleging Amazon violated a city law that prohibits employment discrimination based on political speech or beliefs. Seattle's one-year moratorium on large-scale data centers was prompted in part by concerns that proposed new facilities would consume electricity equal to one-third of the city's average daily usage. The case highlights growing tensions between tech workers, their employers, and communities over the rapid expansion of AI data center infrastructure.
quote: "I am unwilling to accept a reality in which Amazon or any corporation can silence me in exercising my rights." — Patrick Schloesser, Amazon Software Engineer

title: General Intuition in talks to raise $300M at around $2B valuation
summary: General Intuition, a New York-based AI startup building foundation models to train agents on spatial-temporal reasoning, is in talks to raise $300 million at a valuation of just over $2 billion, according to TechCrunch sources. The company spun out of Medal — a gaming clip-sharing platform — eight months ago with a $134 million seed round, and leverages Medal's dataset of 2 billion videos per year from 10 million monthly active users to train its world models. Backers in the new round include Jeff Bezos, Eric Schmidt, Khosla Ventures, and General Catalyst. The startup distinguishes itself from competitors like Runway and World Labs by building world models specifically to train AI agents rather than selling the models themselves, with plans to release a new product by late summer or early fall.

title: A tech worker-backed PAC is bringing a $5M knife to Big Tech’s $100M gunfight
summary: The Guardrails Alliance, a new super PAC backed by tech workers, labor unions, and other groups, launched Thursday with $5 million in current funds and a goal of raising $15 million to support AI safety legislation and pro-regulation political candidates. Founded by Democratic operatives Shaunna Thomas and Leah Hunt-Hendrix, it positions itself as a grassroots counterweight to well-funded industry groups like Leading the Future, which has over $100 million backed by figures such as OpenAI President Greg Brockman. The PAC is already buying ads to support New York congressional candidate Alex Bores, who has been targeted by Leading the Future and is running in upcoming primaries. The effort reflects growing discontent among rank-and-file tech employees over AI governance, with workers mobilizing around issues ranging from AI safety to government contracts with ICE.
quote: "Our fundamental belief here is that people still do have the power to stop this autocratic takeover of the Trump administration and the tech sector." — Shaunna Thomas, Co-founder, Guardrails Alliance

title: Adobe embeds agentic AI workflows across Creative Cloud, shifting from media generation to production orchestration
summary: Adobe has launched a major expansion of its AI "creative agent" across Creative Cloud applications including Premiere Pro, Photoshop, Illustrator, InDesign, and Frame.io, now available in public beta. Unlike earlier generative AI tools that produce standalone media outputs, this system functions as an orchestration layer that interprets natural language prompts and directly manipulates application APIs to execute complex, multi-step production workflows. The upgraded Firefly AI studio, in private beta, introduces new architectural components called "Elements" and "Projects" that enable persistent contextual memory and more sophisticated workflow management. The shift marks a meaningful evolution from AI as a media generation tool to AI as a production automation layer embedded within professional creative software.

title: Who decides when AI is too dangerous?
summary: The US government imposed export controls on Anthropic's Fable 5 and its underlying Mythos model last Friday, barring foreign nationals — including those working at Anthropic in the United States — from accessing either model. The action was triggered after Amazon researchers flagged a potential jailbreak to Anthropic, which then reportedly reached Trump administration officials, leading to a 90-minute ultimatum for Anthropic to address the issue before controls were imposed. Anthropic responded by taking both models fully offline, saying it could not otherwise ensure compliance with the restriction. The episode highlights a deeper tension: Anthropic has long argued for serious government AI regulation on safety grounds, but now finds itself on the receiving end of an enforcement action it did not anticipate and does not fully accept.

title: Photoshop and Premiere now have AI assistants
summary: Adobe has launched AI assistants across its major Creative Cloud applications — Photoshop, Premiere, Illustrator, InDesign, and Frame.io — as part of a public beta. Each assistant is powered by Adobe's "conversational creative agent" but is fine-tuned for app-specific tasks, such as reorganizing video timelines in Premiere or managing layers and backgrounds in Photoshop. Users can issue natural language prompts to automate complex, multi-step workflows that previously required manual effort. The rollout extends AI capabilities Adobe had already introduced in Express, Acrobat, and Firefly to its most widely used professional tools.
quote: "Every creative now has an agent capable of helping them execute across every app and platform where they work so they can set the vision, apply their taste, and make the calls that only they can." — David Wadhwani, Adobe creativity head

title: Adobe’s redesigned AI studio remembers what your creations look like
summary: Adobe has launched a redesigned Firefly AI studio in private beta, featuring persistent context, reusable assets, and organized workflows to streamline the creative process from ideation to production. Two new features are central to the update: "Elements," which lets users save and name characters, objects, and locations for reuse across projects without re-entering prompts, and "Projects," which organizes assets and creative context in one place. The Firefly AI assistant is also gaining new capabilities, including brand kit generation (logos and color palettes), video Quick Cut editing, storyboard creation, and image-to-video transformation. Adobe frames Firefly as a creative co-working partner rather than a replacement for human creativity, with the flexibility to serve users who prefer conversational AI workflows as well as those who want more hands-on control.
quote: "Creativity has many paths, and the idea is that the agent can kind of meet those users however they want to work with the agent." — Forest Key, Vice President of Agentic AI for Creativity and Productivity, Adobe

title: Pixi’s new iOS app turns text messages into interactive AR experiences
summary: Pixi, a startup founded by ex-DreamWorks and ex-Apple veteran Mark Drummond, has launched an iOS app that lets users send AI-powered augmented reality characters through iMessage. The characters appear through the recipient's iPhone camera and can react to their surroundings in real time — responding to pets, facial expressions, and voices — with all visual and audio processing handled on-device for privacy. At launch, the free app offers a robot, cat, and animated envelope character, plus simple games, with plans for a marketplace where brands and creators can offer their own characters. Pixi aims to expand to Android and platforms like WhatsApp and Instagram, and eventually allow users to generate custom characters through generative AI prompts.
quote: "The consumer problem we're solving is thinking of a friend when they're not present." — Mark Drummond, Founder, Pixi

title: Midjourney Medical goes from generating ‘cat images’ to full-body ultrasound scans
summary: Midjourney, known for its AI image generator, has unveiled the Midjourney Scanner — a full-body ultrasound CT scanner developed in partnership with Butterfly Network that uses a ring of thousands of underwater sensors to create detailed 3D images of a person's body composition in about 60 seconds. The system uses 40 ultrasound-on-chip imaging modules and two petaflops of processing power to analyze how ultrasonic waves pass through the body. CEO David Holz plans to install 10 scanners in a Midjourney Spa at San Francisco's Union Square, targeting a launch before the end of 2027, with the goal of eventually making the technology widely available for preventative health screening. The company says current applications focus on body composition mapping, which doesn't require FDA clearance for diagnostic imaging, though broader medical uses would need regulatory approval.

title: Shopify introduces in-store returns, more updates to agentic commerce
summary: Shopify's Spring 2026 "Everywhere Edition" update introduces over 150 platform changes, including in-store pickup and returns via QR codes through the Shop app, expanded agentic commerce capabilities, and an AI-powered marketing autopilot. Merchants can now monitor how their products perform on AI platforms like ChatGPT, Gemini, and Copilot, and receive guidance on improving their visibility in those channels. The Shopify Catalog will also automatically enrich product data to improve uptake by AI chatbots, boosting conversion in AI-driven shopping interactions. These updates build on Shopify's earlier partnerships with Google and Microsoft to embed its merchants' products into major AI chatbot ecosystems.
quote: "To put it simply, we wanted to make sure that everyone on Shopify knows that you don't need anything else to show up everywhere." — Vanessa Lee, Product VP, Shopify

title: Roelof Botha joins SpaceX’s board of directors
summary: Roelof Botha, former managing partner of Sequoia Capital, has joined SpaceX's board of directors, filling an existing vacancy and also taking a seat on the company's audit committee. The appointment comes less than a week after SpaceX completed what is described as the largest IPO in history. Botha has a long personal and professional history with SpaceX CEO Elon Musk, dating back to 2000 when Musk hired him to lead finance at PayPal. Despite joining the board, Botha and other directors have limited influence, as Musk controls more than 80% of SpaceX's voting power and has authority over all board composition changes.
quote: "He was the first person to offer me a job in America. He believed in me when I was an unknown student at Stanford." — Roelof Botha, SpaceX Board Director

title: After unveiling ridiculously expensive AR glasses, Snap’s stock takes a dive
summary: Snap unveiled its long-awaited augmented reality glasses, called Spectacles, at a price of nearly $2,200 per pair, triggering a more than 5% drop in the company's stock, which fell from $5.86 to a low of $4.83. The high price has raised concerns about the product's commercial viability, particularly given that Snap's core user base consists largely of teenagers who are unlikely to afford such a device. CEO Evan Spiegel defended the pricing by framing Spectacles as a computer comparable to high-end laptops, and positioned the product between cheaper but less capable smart glasses like Meta's Ray-Bans and more expensive headsets like the Apple Vision Pro. The launch highlights the challenge Snap faces in monetizing a decade of AR development while its stock has already fallen 30% over the past year.
quote: "The most important way to think of Specs is as a computer, and so they're comparably priced to other high-end computers or high-end laptops." — Evan Spiegel, CEO

title: NEA’s Tiffany Luck says enterprises are still figuring out their AI ROI
summary: NEA partner Tiffany Luck appeared on TechCrunch's Equity podcast to discuss the current state of enterprise AI adoption, particularly the tension between AI hype and measurable return on investment. The conversation comes amid reports of companies like Uber exhausting their annual AI budgets in months and others scaling back AI tool access after costs surged. Luck, who focuses on AI investing at NEA, discussed how startups are emerging to help enterprises track and justify their AI spending, as well as the outlook for personal AI agents and this year's AI IPO landscape. The episode reflects broader industry reckoning with whether aggressive AI adoption, sometimes called "tokenmaxxing," is actually delivering business value.

title: AI experts want a middle-power coalition to set safety guardrails. Some think Canada could lead it
summary: AI policy experts and advocates gathered at the British High Commission in Ottawa to discuss the International AI Safety Report, overseen by Canadian AI researcher Yoshua Bengio, calling for a coalition of middle-power nations to establish AI safety guardrails independent of the US-China dynamic. Speakers argued that Canada could potentially lead this coalition, though noted the country currently lacks the leverage to unilaterally influence AI development. The report warned that risk management gaps could lead to serious harm as AI capabilities rapidly advance. Notably, attendees said employees at major AI companies including OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google are privately urging middle powers to step up, expressing concern that the American government is not acting in good faith on AI regulation.
quote: "Safety is the only way that we can truly reap the benefits of AI, so Canada, along with its allies, can continue playing a key role in how we shape a safer trajectory in AI for the whole world." — Yoshua Bengio, AI researcher and report overseer

title: World leaders want American AI. They just don’t want America to be able to turn it off.
summary: At the G7 Summit, world leaders including France's Emmanuel Macron and India's Narendra Modi raised alarms about U.S. control over access to American AI models, warning that the ability to cut off access overnight poses economic and national security risks. The concerns were triggered by the Trump administration's recent decision to block Anthropic from exporting its Mythos 5 and Fable 5 models on national security grounds, leaving international businesses and governments uncertain about the reliability of U.S. AI infrastructure. G7 leaders discussed creating a "trusted partners" scheme to grant broader access to advanced AI models from firms like Anthropic and OpenAI, aiming to bypass unilateral U.S. restrictions while countering rivals like China. Canadian enterprise AI firm Cohere's CEO Aidan Gomez also weighed in, warning that dependence on a small number of big tech companies threatens national and economic sovereignty.
quote: "Digital sovereignty is not just about market competition or any one company or nation. It's about who controls the foundational technology that will shape our economic security and national sovereignty for decades to come." — Aidan Gomez, Co-founder and CEO, Cohere

title: Anthropic ships major Claude Design overhaul with design system imports, code round-trips, and a fix for its token-burning problem
summary: Anthropic has released a major update to Claude Design, its AI-powered design tool that drew over one million users in its first week after launching in April but quickly drew criticism for consuming tokens at an unsustainable rate. The overhaul directly addresses the token consumption problem that caused one reviewer to burn through 80% of his weekly Claude Pro allowance in about 25 minutes while producing only three webpage prototype variations. Beyond fixing efficiency, the update repositions Claude Design as a design system compliance tool that integrates with enterprise workflows, enforces brand consistency, and connects design outputs to code. The move reflects Anthropic's broader strategy of transforming early-stage AI demos into enterprise-ready products.
quote: "We're talking another token-hungry Claude product here, one that Pro users in particular will barely be able to use before burning through their usage limits." — PCWorld reviewer, Product Reviewer

title: Anthropic becomes first AI startup to join the Frontier carbon removal coalition
summary: Anthropic has joined Frontier, a carbon removal coalition founded by tech companies including Stripe, Google, and Shopify, becoming the first pure AI startup to join the group. The company is contributing to a new $915 million funding tranche that nearly doubles Frontier's total pledges to $1.8 billion, which will be used to contract carbon removal projects across technologies like direct air capture and enhanced rock weathering. The move is notable given that AI companies have faced scrutiny over surging energy consumption, and Anthropic has yet to publish a sustainability report or make prior climate commitments. Frontier is also shifting strategy, focusing on fewer but larger projects with an eye toward government support taking over by 2040.

title: Anthropic got hit by export rules nobody understands
summary: The Trump administration invoked export control rules to order Anthropic to block all foreign nationals — including its own employees and US-based users — from accessing its newest AI models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5, in what experts say is an unprecedented use of such regulations. The legal basis for the order has not been publicly explained, creating significant confusion since the models remain hosted on Anthropic's servers and no actual transfer of code, weights, or data occurs. Experts warn the episode exposes deep instability in US AI governance, as successive administrations have failed to clearly define what is expected of model developers under existing export control frameworks. The incident has broader implications for the entire AI industry, raising questions about what standards other frontier labs must meet and whether foreign governments and companies should rely on American AI systems at all.
quote: "If creating models that are impossible to jailbreak becomes the de facto standard for the United States, then it will have no AI models." — Andrew Reddie, Professor, UC Berkeley's Goldman School of Public Policy

title: NEA’s Tiffany Luck on AI IPOs, personal agents, and the ROI reckoning
summary: NEA partner Tiffany Luck spoke with TechCrunch's Equity podcast about the growing tension between AI hype and measurable returns on investment, a dynamic she describes as central to her work at the venture firm. The conversation covered the so-called "tokenmaxxing" trend — where companies pushed aggressive AI adoption — and the subsequent budget overruns at firms like Uber, which reportedly exhausted its annual AI budget within months. Luck discussed opportunities she sees in personal AI agents and consumer applications, as well as how startups are emerging to help enterprises track and justify their AI spending. The episode also touched on her perspective on this year's AI IPO landscape.

title: "Dangerous" AI models are coming no matter what
summary: Anthropic's advanced AI models Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 were taken offline following a U.S. government export-control directive citing national security concerns over the models' dual-use cybersecurity capabilities. The Trump administration argues that Fable 5's guardrails can be bypassed to access Mythos 5's full hacking capabilities, but cybersecurity experts warn the restrictions are largely futile since competing companies and open-source developers are rapidly approaching similar capabilities. Experts emphasize that the broader policy challenge is not stopping any single model but developing coherent frameworks for managing AI's inevitable advances in sensitive domains. The situation highlights a growing tension between government attempts to control dangerous AI capabilities and the reality that such capabilities are becoming industry-wide.
quote: "The policy question is not whether a technology has risk. The question is whether a specific restriction meaningfully reduces that risk or whether it mainly slows down the people trying to make systems safer." — Chris Wysopal, cofounder of Veracode

title: World model maker Odyssey nabs $1.45B valuation backed by Amazon and other big names
summary: Odyssey, a world model AI startup founded by autonomous vehicle veterans Oliver Cameron and Jeff Hawke, has raised a $310 million Series B round at a $1.45 billion valuation, making it a unicorn. The round was led by Natural Capital with participation from Amazon, AMD Ventures, GV, and notable angel investors including Jeff Dean, Elad Gil, and Cruise founder Kyle Vogt. Odyssey builds AI world models that simulate physical environments with accurate physics, with applications ranging from video game creation to robotics and interactive video generation from text prompts. As part of the deal, AWS becomes Odyssey's preferred cloud provider, and the company will optimize its models to run on Amazon's Trainium AI chips.

title: Two-thirds of Americans think AI is advancing too quickly
summary: A new Pew Research poll finds that 49 percent of Americans now use AI chatbots at least occasionally, up sharply from 33 percent in 2024, while 63 percent believe AI is advancing too quickly. ChatGPT usage has doubled since 2023, with 44 percent of respondents reporting use. Despite growing adoption, sentiment remains largely negative, with only 16 percent believing AI will have a positive impact on society. Younger Americans aged 18–29 show the highest usage rates but are also among the most pessimistic, with 48 percent expecting a negative impact.

title: Only 16 percent of Americans think AI will have a positive impact on society, a new study shows
summary: A new Pew Research study finds that only 16% of Americans believe AI will have a positive impact on society over the next 20 years, while 40% expect a negative impact. Despite widespread skepticism, AI tool usage is growing rapidly, with 44% of U.S. adults now using ChatGPT — more than double the figure from 2023 — and about a quarter using AI chatbots daily. Trust in both government and industry is low, with 67% doubting the government will meaningfully regulate AI and 59% not trusting companies to develop it safely. The findings highlight a striking paradox: Americans are increasingly reliant on AI in their daily lives even as they grow more pessimistic about its societal consequences.

title: Google bets on Gemini to reinvent the smart home speaker
summary: Google has launched the Google Home Speaker, a $99.99 smart speaker built specifically around its Gemini AI, marking the company's first standalone audio device since the Nest Audio in 2020. The device supports natural language and multistep commands, mid-sentence corrections, and two-way conversations on a wide range of topics beyond smart home control. A $10/month Google Home Premium subscription unlocks more advanced features including Gemini Live conversations and Nest camera activity summaries. The launch represents Google's attempt to revitalize the stagnant smart speaker market using generative AI while also creating a new recurring revenue stream.

title: The Gemini-powered Google Home Speaker arrives on June 25 for $100
summary: Google has announced its first new smart home speaker in nearly six years, the Google Home Speaker, launching June 25 for $99.99 and available for preorder now. The device features a 58 mm full-range driver, three far-field microphones, a quad-core processor with a dedicated NPU for local AI processing, and deep integration with Google's Gemini AI assistant. While audio quality falls below the older Nest Audio, the speaker emphasizes AI capabilities including Gemini Live for conversational AI and improved background noise filtering. The purchase includes six months of Google Home Premium, which unlocks additional AI features in the Home app.

title: Collecting robot training data is dirty, unglamorous work. Some AI labs are already paying XDOF to do it.
summary: XDOF, a robotics data startup founded by former UC Berkeley researchers, emerged from stealth today after raising $70 million from Thrive Capital, Spark Capital, a16z, Lux, and WndrCo. The company aims to solve a core bottleneck in physical AI development: the lack of high-quality robot training data comparable to the text corpora that powered large language models. XDOF builds data pipelines, collection tools, and annotation systems for frontier AI labs and robotics companies, and is already working with 20 customers it cannot name. Alongside its launch, the company is partnering with UC Berkeley to release what it calls the largest collection of high-quality robot training data ever assembled, covering 130,000 trajectories of robot manipulation data.
quote: "We've already seen some of the downfalls of falling a little bit behind in the language model race … you don't want to be in this type of situation where you pursue this technology too late, and everyone is in this boat where physical AI is the next frontier." — Philipp Wu, Co-founder and CEO

title: Pramaana Labs raises $27M seed round from Khosla Ventures to bring formal verification to AI
summary: Pramaana Labs has raised a $27 million seed round led by Khosla Ventures to apply formal verification techniques to AI systems in high-stakes domains like law, drug discovery, and tax preparation. The startup combines a conventional large language model with a deterministic verification layer built on the open-source LEAN programming language, which is typically used to verify mathematical proofs. The approach aims to reduce AI hallucinations in fields where errors carry serious consequences, with advisors including former IRS Commissioner Danny Werfel and professors from IIT Delhi, IIT Madras, and UC Berkeley. Pramaana's model reflects a broader industry push to make AI outputs more reliable as enterprises move beyond pilot programs into production deployments.
quote: "The world's hardest problems are not unsolvable. They are unformalized." — Ranjan Rajagopalan, Co-founder and CEO, Pramaana Labs

title: Autonomique says backflipping robots are not the point
summary: Canadian AI startup Autonomique has moved its physical AI software from pilot to full production, with F P Manufacturing — a Canadian subsidiary of Japanese auto parts supplier F-Tech — using the technology to direct semi-humanoid robots assembling chassis and suspension parts at its Tottenham, Ontario plant. The Montréal- and Menlo Park-based company integrates its AI-driven motion control software into third-party robot hardware rather than building its own, reducing capital intensity. Autonomique has closed the first tranche of an undisclosed seed round backed by Canadian investors including White Star Capital, Inovia Capital, Innovobot, and Garage Capital, as well as the co-founders of Clearpath Robotics and Otto Motors. The deployment and funding signal growing Canadian momentum in industrial automation, a sector where Canada has historically lagged peer nations.
quote: "It all boils down to commercial execution." — Neha Khera, Managing Partner, Innovobot IRV Fund

title: AI search grounded in Facebook posts? What could go wrong?
summary: Meta has launched an AI Mode search feature within the Facebook app, designed to answer complex queries by drawing on public posts from Facebook Groups, Instagram Reels, and other Meta platforms. The feature is aimed at helping users plan trips and discover local activities, but testing by The Verge revealed a pattern of hallucinations, including suggesting a closed community pool, recommending a kids' coffee shop located in the wrong state, and citing Facebook posts that don't appear to exist. While the tool largely resisted producing clear misinformation on sensitive topics like vaccines or elections, it did produce a dubious justification of the January 6th Capitol rioters when prompted. The mixed results highlight the broader challenge of grounding AI search in user-generated social media content, where accuracy and reliability are inherently uneven.

title: Google’s first smart speaker in six years arrives next week
summary: Google is launching its first new smart speaker in six years, the Google Home Speaker, with preorders opening June 17th and shipping beginning June 25th at a price of $99. The device is built specifically around Gemini for Home, Google's conversational AI assistant, which can understand natural language, handle complex queries, and maintain dialogue without repeated wake words. The speaker features 360-degree audio, Matter controller and Thread border router capabilities, and can be paired for stereo or spatial surround sound with a Google TV Streamer. Some key features, including Gemini Live and AI-powered camera search, require a Google Home Premium subscription at $10 per month, though early buyers receive six months free.

title: Canadian pension giant joins race to fund India’s AI-fueled data center boom
summary: Canada Pension Plan Investment Board (CPP Investments) has committed up to ₹70 billion (roughly $741 million) to Indian data center operator CtrlS, acquiring an 8.2% stake and joining a joint venture to develop hyperscale data center campuses across India. The deal reflects surging global investment in AI and cloud infrastructure, with India emerging as a key destination for data center buildout alongside major commitments from Amazon, Google, Microsoft, and others. CPP Investments, Canada's largest pension fund, has been active in India since 2009 and holds approximately $20 billion in net assets there. The investment adds to a broader wave of capital targeting Indian digital infrastructure, including Blackstone-backed AirTrunk's $30 billion pledge and Meta's partnership with Reliance Industries on an AI-enabled data center.
quote: "As one of the world's fastest-growing digital markets, India represents an important pillar of our global data center strategy." — Max Biagosch, Global Head of Real Assets, CPP Investments

title: DeepL acquires Mixhalo for live-event audio streaming and translation
summary: DeepL, a German AI translation company, has acquired Mixhalo, a San Francisco-based real-time audio streaming startup founded in 2016, for an undisclosed sum. Mixhalo's technology enables live audio streaming and translation at concerts, sports events, and conferences, complementing DeepL's existing voice-to-text and voice-to-voice translation products. The deal extends DeepL's translation suite into the live events space and will serve as a real-world demonstration platform for its AI translation capabilities. As part of the acquisition, DeepL is opening a Bay Area office to expand its U.S. operations.
quote: "Mixhalo has been a long-time DeepL customer, and I attended a customer dinner and ended up seated next to Sebastian, DeepL's CTO." — Vik Singh, CEO, Mixhalo

title: Michael Hyatt-backed BrokerPlus launches to disrupt “antiquated” mortgage industry
summary: Canadian startup BrokerPlus has launched from stealth with an AI-powered platform aimed at modernizing the residential mortgage broker industry, which remains heavily reliant on spreadsheets and legacy software. Founded by Swish Goswami and Amir Agassi, both formerly of Toronto-based AI networking startup Boardy, the company helps brokers identify missed renewal and refinancing opportunities hidden in their existing client databases. BrokerPlus has raised over $500,000 CAD in pre-seed funding from investors including Michael Hyatt's family office and Tennr co-founder Trey Holterman, and is already generating revenue with clients across four Canadian provinces. The startup aims to reach profitability in 2024 while keeping its team under five people by using AI to dramatically amplify individual employee output.
quote: "I want people from the outside to think that we're a team of 20 or 30 people, but in fact, we're actually three or four—and that's possible with AI." — Swish Goswami, CEO

title: Pinterest launches an experimental AI shopping app called ‘Ask Pinterest’
summary: Pinterest has launched Ask Pinterest, an experimental standalone web app that uses conversational AI to help users discover products and get personalized shopping recommendations. The app leverages Pinterest's proprietary Taste Graph — its internal data system mapping users to their interests and aesthetics — to deliver context-aware, multi-step shopping guidance across sessions. By building it as a separate app rather than integrating it into the main platform, Pinterest can test AI-driven discovery without disrupting its core experience. The announcement also included a Pinterest MCP layer for advertisers, an AI assistant in Ads Manager, and a new Performance+ creative model, all positioning Pinterest to compete in an increasingly AI-driven advertising and e-commerce landscape.

title: Did the Anthropic model ban prove Cohere is right about sovereign AI?
summary: The US government ordered Anthropic to ban foreign access to its most advanced AI models, prompting Anthropic to remove access entirely and sending shockwaves through the AI industry. The move has benefited Canadian AI company Cohere, which reportedly saw a surge of inbound interest as a result, given its model of offering enterprise-focused, deployable AI that avoids dependence on a single government or platform. Cohere co-founder Nick Frosst argues the incident validates the company's long-standing emphasis on sovereign AI — the idea that organizations need AI infrastructure they control, not access that can be revoked by a foreign government. The episode highlights a growing global debate over who controls frontier AI technology and the risks of dependence on a single nation's AI ecosystem.
quote: "If your entire technology is coming from a single country, and that country decides that every now and again they're going to shut off access to you, that's not a foundation you can build on." — Nick Frosst, Co-founder, Cohere

title: The next humanoid robot might not look human at all
summary: Genesis AI, a French startup backed by former Google CEO Eric Schmidt, has unveiled Eno, a robot designed around human capability rather than human appearance. Unlike most humanoid robots, Eno may lack a head or legs and could use a wheeled base, but it features hands precisely matched to human form and function so it can use existing tools and objects. The company positions Eno as a fully general-purpose robot and plans to begin production and targeted deployments by end of 2026, initially in manufacturing, laboratories, and logistics. The announcement signals a growing divergence in robotics philosophy, with some developers prioritizing functional versatility over the humanoid aesthetic championed by companies like Tesla and Figure.

title: Why Weibo’s tiny VibeThinker-3B has the AI world arguing over benchmarks again
summary: Sina Weibo researchers have published a technical report claiming their 3-billion-parameter model, VibeThinker-3B, matches or outperforms AI systems hundreds of times its size on a demanding math reasoning benchmark. The model scored 94.3 on AIME 2026, placing it alongside DeepSeek V3.2 (671 billion parameters) and ahead of Google's Gemini 3 Pro, which scored 91.7. Using a proprietary test-time scaling technique called Claim-Level Reliability Assessment, the score rises to 97.1, reportedly surpassing virtually all publicly known systems. The paper has quickly attracted significant attention in the AI research community, reigniting longstanding debates about benchmark validity and whether small models can genuinely rival much larger ones.

title: Anthropic’s latest feud with the Trump admin may actually help it, sales data suggests
summary: Anthropic surpassed OpenAI in business market share for the first time in May, capturing 41% of AI subscriptions versus OpenAI's 39.5%, according to spending data from financial platform Ramp covering more than 70,000 businesses. The AI lab also raised $65 billion at a near-trillion-dollar valuation and filed confidential IPO paperwork, reportedly after its first profitable quarter. However, the Trump administration ordered Anthropic to ban non-Americans from accessing its newest models — Mythos 5 and the public-facing Fable 5 — citing export control concerns, effectively forcing the company to pull those products from the market. Paradoxically, analysts suggest the government conflict may boost Anthropic's appeal, as a similar clash in March appeared to accelerate rather than dampen business adoption.
quote: "Anthropic's best month on record, as far as business adoption, was the month that the Department of Defense labeled them a supply-chain risk." — Ara Kharazian, Lead Economist, Ramp

title: Trump admin tries to block Clean Air Act lawsuit over xAI's gas turbines
summary: The Trump administration filed a brief urging a federal court to dismiss an NAACP Clean Air Act lawsuit against xAI, arguing that the company's gas turbines powering its Colossus data center and Grok AI systems serve national security interests. The Justice Department contended that Mississippi state regulators have already determined the turbines do not require permits, and that citizen enforcement suits should not override government discretion. The NAACP and the Southern Environmental Law Center counter that xAI is operating 57 unpermitted turbines emitting harmful pollution in a predominantly Black community near Memphis, and that the DOJ's argument could broadly undermine citizen enforcement of environmental law. The case raises significant questions about whether AI infrastructure projects can claim exemption from environmental permitting requirements on national security grounds.
quote: "The provision was passed by Congress with bipartisan support, and courts have repeatedly upheld the constitutionality of citizen suits. Now, the Department of Justice is indicating that it has a right to come in and cancel such community-led suits at any time."  — Southern Environmental Law Center, Representative

title: Anthropic "pauses" token-based billing for its Claude Agent SDK
summary: Anthropic has paused a planned billing change to its Claude Agent SDK that would have significantly increased costs for heavy users by switching them from generous subscription-based usage limits to standard API rates. The change, originally set to take effect June 15, would have meant that users running automated agents or third-party apps through the SDK could quickly exhaust the value of their subscriptions, with one analysis suggesting power users could burn through the equivalent of their subscription cost in just days. Anthropic cited a need to "better support how users build with Claude subscriptions" in announcing the pause. The reversal comes amid broader industry sensitivity around AI usage billing, just weeks after GitHub Copilot's own token-based billing changes caused sticker shock among developers.
quote: "Our subscriptions weren't built for the usage patterns of these third-party tools." — Boris Cherny, Head of Claude Code

title: SpaceX valuation balloons to $2.6T, briefly passes Amazon
summary: SpaceX's valuation surged to as high as $2.9 trillion on its second full day of public trading, briefly surpassing Amazon to become the fifth-most valuable company in the world. The spike was driven by news of SpaceX's $60 billion all-stock acquisition of AI coding tool Cursor and the launch of options trading on SpaceX shares. The volatility reflects both the thin float — only about 4% of total shares were made available at IPO — and investor enthusiasm for SpaceX's ambitions as an AI infrastructure company, despite the firm posting a $4.9 billion loss on $18.7 billion in revenue last year. SpaceX's IPO raised nearly $86 billion, and its valuation has grown by roughly $1 trillion since its public debut last Friday.

title: Shopify shareholders vote down responsible AI proposal
summary: Shopify shareholders at the company's annual general meeting voted down a proposal from Canadian non-profit SHARE, submitted on behalf of the United Church of Canada's pension plan, that would have committed the e-commerce giant to respecting human rights and international standards in its AI use. Shopify's board had opposed the measure, dismissing it as a generic template with no relevance to the company's specific operations. The outcome was largely expected given Shopify's dual-class share structure, which grants CEO Tobi Lütke outsized voting power through a special "founder share." The vote reflects a broader pattern in Canada and beyond, where responsible AI shareholder proposals are increasingly common but rarely result in binding commitments.
quote: "SHARE is applying a generic AI policy template across public companies with no regard for what those companies actually do or how they operate." — Shopify Board, Statement

title: Pentagon boasts of using AI to write reports mandated by Congress
summary: The US Department of Defense is using generative AI tools, primarily Google's Gemini for Government through its GenAI.mil platform, to draft congressionally mandated reports that traditionally require hundreds of hours of staff work. Pentagon Chief Technology Officer Emil Michael and other officials have openly promoted this practice, with one team claiming their AI-generated report was "the best report we've written in the past five years." The Pentagon reports that 1.5 million of its roughly 3.5 million personnel are now using these tools, up from 80,000 in December 2025. Critics and outside observers warn that AI-generated content has frequently contained errors and false claims, raising concerns about the accuracy of reports meant to provide congressional oversight of military spending, especially as the Pentagon seeks an unprecedented $1.5 trillion budget for fiscal year 2027.
quote: "I have to report to Congress every year on this thing. Let me load all the papers onto it and have it draft me a congressional report that would otherwise take 200 hours of staffing time and do it in five hours." — Emil Michael, Pentagon Chief Technology Officer

title: Android 17 launches with new multitasking tools as Google expands Gemini features
summary: Google has released Android 17 and Wear OS 7, bringing a wide range of AI-powered features centered on its Gemini models alongside new multitasking and productivity tools. The update introduces capabilities like video editing through conversation with Gemini Omni, music generation via Lyria 3, and speech-to-speech translation using AudioLM on the Pixel 10a. New interface features include a bubble bar for faster app switching, simultaneous front-camera and screen recording for social media, and expanded parental controls. The release highlights Google's strategy of using its hardware and software ecosystem to showcase its latest AI models, as it competes with Apple's anticipated AI upgrades to Siri and iOS 27.

title: Apple 2027 rumors: AirPods with cameras for AI and the second folding iPhone
summary: Apple is rumored to be developing camera-equipped AirPods for a late 2027 launch, with cameras in the stems to provide Siri with "visual context" about users' surroundings, serving as a stepping stone toward eventual smart glasses. Bloomberg's Mark Gurman also reports on a second-generation foldable iPhone following a model expected this fall, and a special "20th anniversary" iPhone featuring a nearly edge-to-edge display with curved glass. Chip plans call for a 2nm A21 chip in most 2027 phones, with Apple considering Intel for some production alongside TSMC.
