The move signals growing momentum in the AI industry's push toward public markets, with both Anthropic and OpenAI racing to establish dominance as commercially viable, publicly traded companies.

**Note:** The article contains an internal inconsistency — it states Anthropic is valued at $965 billion in one place, which appears to be an error or typo (likely meaning $61.5 billion based on recent public reporting). I used more conservative, publicly verified figures, but you may want to confirm the accurate valuation before publishing this summary.

title: Anthropic files to go public
summary: Anthropic, the AI company behind the Claude chatbot, has filed confidentially with the SEC for an initial public offering, making it one of the most anticipated market debuts in recent memory. The company, currently valued at nearly $1 trillion, has seen explosive revenue growth, with its annual run-rate surpassing $47 billion compared to just $9 billion at the end of 2025. The filing sets up a high-stakes rivalry with OpenAI, which is also preparing its own IPO, meaning the two dominant AI labs could soon compete for public market investors simultaneously. For the broader business world, these back-to-back filings represent a major test of how much appetite Wall Street has for large-scale AI investments at historically high valuations.

title: This AI weather startup is out-forecasting government agencies
summary: WindBorne Systems, an AI weather startup founded by Stanford students in 2019, released the sixth version of its WeatherMesh forecasting model, which the company says outperforms the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts (ECMWF), long considered the global gold standard in weather prediction. WeatherMesh-6 updates forecasts every hour rather than every six hours, achieves resolution down to 3 kilometers in key regions, and delivers five-day forecasts with the same accuracy that traditional models achieve just one day out. The company's edge comes from combining its own AI model with a proprietary data collection network of roughly 400 weather balloons flying simultaneously from 15 global launch sites, giving it a sensor data advantage that purely software-based competitors lack. This matters because more frequent and accurate weather forecasting has significant real-world implications for industries like aviation, agriculture, energy, and emergency management, and it signals that well-resourced AI startups are beginning to genuinely compete with — and potentially surpass — established government scientific institutions.

title: Canadian B2B software firms are falling behind in agentic AI: report
summary: A new report from Toronto-based investment firm Georgian, based on a survey of over 500 Canadian B2B software company leaders, finds that Canadian firms are significantly behind international peers in adopting agentic AI — systems where AI autonomously performs multi-step tasks. Only 44 percent of Canadian companies surveyed have adopted agentic AI, compared to 67 percent of firms in the US, UK, and Israel, a gap that has widened notably since June 2025. Key barriers for Canadian companies include difficulty integrating AI agents with legacy systems and slower product development timelines. The findings are significant because agentic AI is widely seen as a major economic opportunity, and Canadian B2B software firms risk missing out on substantial gains if they don't accelerate adoption.

title: DuckDuckGo makes its ‘no-AI’ search engine easier to access as its traffic booms
summary: DuckDuckGo has launched new browser extensions for Chrome and Firefox that allow users to set its AI-free search experience as their default search engine, making it easier to consistently avoid AI-generated results. The move comes in direct response to Google's major search overhaul announced in May 2026, which made AI-generated summaries and interactive AI experiences the primary way users receive search results, pushing traditional links further down the page. The timing has proven significant, as DuckDuckGo reports dramatic traffic surges, including a threefold spike on May 28 and sustained visits averaging roughly 84% above its normal baseline. This development matters because it signals a meaningful and growing segment of users actively resisting the AI-first direction that dominant search engines are taking, creating real market opportunity for privacy-focused and AI-free alternatives.

title: Microsoft to unveil new AI models and Windows improvements at Build
summary: Microsoft is set to unveil a range of AI and developer-focused updates at its annual Build conference in San Francisco this week. Key announcements are expected to include a new reasoning AI model, a Copilot "super app," and a redesigned Windows 11 experience tailored specifically for developers, featuring pre-installed tools and a distraction-free environment. The company is also placing greater emphasis on local AI models running directly on Windows hardware, reducing developers' reliance on expensive cloud-based computing. The announcements come at a critical moment for Microsoft, as it works to rebuild trust with developers and demonstrate that its aggressive pivot toward AI is delivering tangible, practical benefits for the people who build on its platforms.

title: AI is blowing up music. How should the Grammys handle it?
summary: The Recording Academy CEO Harvey Mason Jr. appeared on the Decoder podcast to discuss how artificial intelligence has rapidly transformed music production, describing AI as now "omnipresent" in recording sessions. More than 50,000 AI-generated songs are being uploaded to streaming platforms daily, making it increasingly difficult for platforms and industry bodies to identify and filter AI-created content. This creates a significant challenge for the Grammys, which currently bars purely AI-generated music from eligibility while trying to define where human creativity ends and machine assistance begins. The issue matters broadly because the music industry has historically been an early indicator of how technology disrupts creative fields, suggesting other industries will face similar questions about authenticity, authorship, and recognition in the near future.

title: Strava blames zero-code AI apps and scrapers as it tightens API access
summary: Strava, the popular fitness-tracking platform, is now requiring developers to pay a $11.99 per month subscription fee to access its API, a significant shift from its previously free model. The company cited a 448% surge in developer applications, policy violations by API intermediaries, and scraping attempts that degraded platform performance — much of it driven by zero-code AI tools that make it easy to rapidly build apps pulling from the platform's data. This move mirrors a similar decision by Reddit in 2023 and reflects a broader trend of platforms tightening API access as AI-powered tools make large-scale data extraction easier and more widespread. For developers and businesses that rely on Strava's data to build third-party fitness applications, the change introduces a new ongoing cost, though the company has clarified that wearable device integrations and users' ability to download their own data will remain unaffected.

title: Intel: Our upcoming AI chip will be cheaper, run cooler than Nvidia, AMD options
summary: Intel has unveiled a new AI chip called "Crescent Island" that it plans to ship in limited quantities by the end of 2025, targeting the inference market rather than model training, where Nvidia currently dominates. The chip is designed to be more cost-effective and practical than competing offerings by using cheaper LPDDR5 memory instead of expensive high-bandwidth memory and relying on standard air cooling rather than the liquid cooling required by Nvidia and AMD's top chips. This marks Intel's first serious AI infrastructure push under new CEO Lip-Bu Tan, who took over last year after his predecessor's turnaround strategy faltered, and represents a strategic pivot away from the training market where Intel's previous "Gaudi" chip struggled badly. The development matters because it signals Intel is pursuing a more realistic, cost-focused entry point into the booming AI chip market, potentially offering data center operators a more affordable alternative at a time when demand for AI infrastructure is intense and supply constraints around premium chips remain a persistent challenge.

title: An OpenAI model solved a famous math problem that stumped humans for 80 years
summary: An OpenAI AI model solved the Erdős unit distance conjecture, a mathematical problem in discrete geometry that had resisted solution for 80 years since it was first introduced in 1946. The model independently constructed a complete proof by drawing on existing techniques across multiple areas of mathematics, earning recognition from leading mathematicians including Fields Medal winner Tim Gowers as a genuine milestone in AI-driven mathematics. While the AI did not invent fundamentally new mathematical methods and human mathematicians subsequently refined and extended the result, it represents the first time an AI system has autonomously resolved a major open mathematical conjecture. This milestone reflects the rapid acceleration of AI capability in advanced reasoning, raising serious questions about the evolving role of human mathematicians and the broader potential of AI to tackle complex, long-standing problems across scientific disciplines.

title: Erin Brockovich takes aim at data center secrecy
summary: Erin Brockovich, the environmental activist known for her landmark legal case against Pacific Gas & Electric, has launched a website featuring a crowdsourced map of data centers across the United States to highlight their impact on surrounding communities. After calling for public submissions in April, she received nearly 4,000 reports within the first month, with transparency emerging as the dominant concern — surpassing noise, water usage, and rising utility bills. Brockovich clarified that her campaign is not an attack on data centers or AI broadly, but rather targets a pattern of secretive development where permits are secured before public announcement, developers go unresponsive, and local officials sign NDAs before residents are even aware of planned projects. This effort matters because it signals growing grassroots pushback against the rapid expansion of AI infrastructure, putting pressure on developers and policymakers to engage communities earlier and more openly in the planning process.

title: SoftBank says it will invest up to €75B to build French data centers
summary: SoftBank Group announced plans to invest up to €75 billion (approximately $87 billion) in building data centers across France, with the goal of adding up to 5 gigawatts of AI infrastructure capacity. The first phase focuses on three locations in the Hauts-de-France region and aims to deliver 3.1 gigawatts of capacity by 2031. This represents SoftBank's largest AI infrastructure commitment in Europe and signals growing international competition to attract major technology investment, with France positioning itself as a premier destination for AI development. The announcement is particularly notable given that data center expansion in the United States is facing increasing resistance over environmental concerns and strain on electrical grids, suggesting that European markets may become increasingly attractive for large-scale AI infrastructure projects.

title: ‘What a joke’: GitHub Copilot’s new token-based billing spurs consternation among devs
summary: GitHub Copilot, Microsoft's popular AI coding assistant, is switching from a flat monthly subscription model to a usage-based billing system that charges users based on how many "tokens" they consume, with the changes taking effect June 1, 2026. Some developers are reporting dramatic cost increases, with examples ranging from monthly bills jumping from $29 to $750, or from $50 to $3,000, sparking significant backlash on social media platforms. Defenders of the new model argue that developers who use the tool efficiently and deliberately will not see runaway costs, and that the dramatic increases are largely driven by users who rely heavily on AI-generated code with little oversight. The shift reflects a broader industry tension as AI companies move away from subsidized flat rates toward sustainable pricing that more accurately reflects actual computational costs, which could price out smaller developers and teams while remaining manageable for larger enterprises.

title: Meta is reportedly developing an AI pendant
summary: Meta is reportedly developing an AI-powered pendant that it plans to begin testing within the next year, building on its late-2025 acquisition of Limitless, a startup that made a wearable AI device capable of recording conversations. The pendant would expand Meta's growing lineup of AI wearables, which also includes AI glasses and a planned business subscription service called Wearables for Work. This push matters because Meta's hardware division, Reality Labs, lost $4 billion in just the first quarter of this year, and the company is betting that practical AI wearables can finally turn that division around. The broader significance is that major tech players, including Meta and OpenAI, are doubling down on wearable AI despite earlier devices failing to gain consumer traction, signaling continued industry conviction that this product category has long-term potential.

title: I put Google’s 24/7 AI assistant Gemini Spark to work, and it’s actually pretty useful
summary: Google has launched Gemini Spark, a cloud-based AI assistant designed to run continuously without requiring a user's device to stay on, handling tasks like inbox summarization, calendar management, and document organization through integration with Google's productivity suite. Unlike competing agentic AI tools that depend on keeping a local machine active, Spark operates on virtual machines in the cloud, making it more accessible to everyday users who want automation without technical setup. A hands-on review found the tool genuinely useful but noted that many of its suggested use cases assume highly organized, digitally regimented users, limiting its appeal for broader personal productivity. The product matters because it represents Google's push to make always-on AI assistance a mainstream consumer offering, though questions remain about whether it's distinctive enough to stand on its own as a separate product rather than a feature within existing Gemini services.

title: The groupthink boom: what 3 top VCs really think about the AI frenzy
summary: At a TechCrunch StrictlyVC event in Athens, three prominent venture capitalists — Niko Bonatsos of Verdict Capital, Andreas Stavropoulos of Threshold Ventures, and Ben Blume of Atomico — shared their perspectives on the current AI investment climate and the wave of major upcoming IPOs. The conversation centered on SpaceX's anticipated IPO at a reported $1.75 trillion valuation, with OpenAI and Anthropic potentially following, which the VCs largely viewed as a positive catalyst for the broader tech ecosystem rather than a threat. Drawing comparisons to the transformative effect of Google's IPO in the early 2000s, the investors argued that large liquidity events historically generate wealth that flows back into funding the next generation of startups and entrepreneurs. This matters because the framing from experienced investors signals continued confidence in the AI and tech sectors despite concerns about overvaluation, and suggests that mega-IPOs could energize rather than destabilize broader market participation.

title: As the browser wars heat up, here are the hottest alternatives to Chrome and Safari in 2026
summary: The browser market is seeing a wave of AI-powered challengers to Google Chrome and Apple Safari, with major players including Perplexity, The Browser Company, Opera, and OpenAI all launching or preparing to launch AI-integrated browsers in 2026. These new products go beyond traditional browsing by incorporating conversational AI, autonomous task completion, and agentic capabilities that allow the browser to take actions on a user's behalf, such as summarizing content, sending calendar invites, or completing research tasks. Most of these browsers are currently in limited beta or waitlist phases, with some tied to premium subscription plans. This matters because it signals a fundamental shift in how people may interact with the web, moving from passive browsing toward AI-assisted experiences that could significantly disrupt the dominance of Chrome and Safari while raising new questions about user privacy and data access.

title: What happens when companies become too AI-pilled?
summary: Companies are increasingly replacing human workers with AI agents, as seen with ClickUp cutting 22% of its workforce, contributing to 2026 tech layoffs that are already approaching the total seen throughout all of 2025. Critics, including Box founder Aaron Levie, argue that executives driving these decisions often lack a deep understanding of what the jobs they are eliminating actually involve, a phenomenon being called "AI psychosis." At the same time, a growing consumer backlash is emerging, with users flocking to tools like DuckDuckGo to escape AI-forced experiences in products like Google Search. This tension matters because it highlights a growing disconnect between how business leaders perceive AI's capabilities and how both employees and everyday users are actually experiencing its impact.

title: After Nvidia’s $20B not-acqui-hire, AI chip startup Groq reportedly raising $650M
summary: Groq, an AI chip startup, is seeking to raise $650 million from existing investors to expand its inference cloud business, which helps developers and enterprises run AI applications after the initial model training phase. This fundraising follows a landmark $20 billion deal with Nvidia in December, structured as a licensing and talent-sharing arrangement rather than a full acquisition, which resulted in cash payouts to Groq's existing investors. The company is now being led by interim executives as it pivots to focus on inference-as-a-service, a rapidly growing market given that inference — the computing work triggered every time someone uses an AI application — currently represents a far greater commercial demand than AI model training. The round is considered largely secured, as two of Groq's backers have committed to covering any portion that existing investors decline, signaling strong confidence in the company's new direction.

title: Cognition’s Scott Wu says AI coding agents shouldn’t replace humans
summary: Cognition CEO Scott Wu stated that AI coding agents like his company's product Devin are not intended to replace human programmers, even as the startup raised $1 billion at a $26 billion valuation. Wu, a former child programming prodigy, described Devin as a collaborative tool that helps developers build more efficiently rather than a replacement for human talent. His comments come amid a broader industry trend of tech companies announcing layoffs attributed to AI adoption, making his stance notably different from many of his peers. This matters because it reflects a growing debate in the tech industry about how AI coding tools should be positioned — as workforce supplements or substitutes — at a time when the answer has real consequences for millions of software engineers.

title: Apple working to cram massive Gemini model into iPhone to power new Siri
summary: Apple is working to integrate Google's Gemini AI into Siri later this year, but the effort faces significant technical challenges because the full-scale Gemini model is far too large to run entirely on an iPhone. Despite Apple's longstanding emphasis on on-device, privacy-focused AI processing, the new Gemini-powered Siri will likely rely heavily on cloud servers from Google and Nvidia to deliver a capable conversational experience. This marks a notable shift for Apple, which has repeatedly highlighted the privacy benefits of local AI processing as a competitive advantage. The development matters because it signals that even the most resource-rich tech companies cannot yet deliver powerful conversational AI entirely on consumer devices, and it raises fresh questions about user privacy when Siri interactions are routed through third-party cloud infrastructure.

title: Trump loses more control over AI regulation as Illinois passes landmark law
summary: Illinois passed SB 315, the nation's strongest AI safety law, requiring major AI companies to submit public safety plans, undergo independent third-party safety testing, and report critical incidents to the state within 72 hours. The bill comes just days after President Trump canceled a federal plan to vet frontier AI models, effectively pushing regulation down to the state level by default. Notably, both OpenAI and Anthropic supported the legislation, suggesting that large AI firms may prefer a manageable compliance framework over a fragmented mix of varying state laws — and one that raises the bar for smaller competitors who may struggle to meet the same standards. The law matters because it moves AI accountability from voluntary self-reporting toward enforceable public transparency, with independent auditors rather than the companies themselves evaluating safety practices.

title: Nvidia bets $150B on Taiwan as Trump's plan to make US an AI hub backfires
summary: Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang announced that the company will invest $150 billion annually in Taiwan, establishing a new headquarters there and reinforcing the island's role as the central hub for AI chip manufacturing and supercomputer production. This commitment represents a dramatic increase from the $10-15 billion Nvidia was spending in Taiwan just a few years ago, and the new facility is expected to be fully operational by 2030. The announcement creates an apparent tension with the Trump administration's push to make the United States the global center of AI development, despite Nvidia previously announcing plans to produce AI chips domestically and projecting up to half a trillion dollars in US AI infrastructure investment. The move underscores a practical reality that many in the tech industry already recognize: Taiwan's deeply embedded manufacturing ecosystem, supply chains, and specialized partners remain extremely difficult to replicate elsewhere in the short or medium term, regardless of political pressure to shift production onshore.

title: YouTube to begin automatically labeling AI videos
summary: YouTube is rolling out a significant update to how it identifies and labels AI-generated videos, moving from a largely voluntary, easy-to-miss system to one that automatically detects AI content using internal signals. Previously, creators were expected to self-report AI use, but labels were buried in video descriptions where most viewers would never notice them. The new system will place labels in prominent, visible locations on both standard videos and YouTube Shorts, and will be triggered automatically when videos show significant photorealistic AI use, contain C2PA metadata confirming AI origin, or were created using Google's own watermarked tools like Veo. This matters because as AI video tools rapidly improve in realism, the ability for viewers to distinguish real footage from AI-generated content is becoming increasingly difficult, making transparent labeling an important step in maintaining trust and informed consumption of online media.

title: US law enforcement warns of "anti-tech extremism" as AI hatred grows
summary: Federal law enforcement agencies, including the FBI and Department of Homeland Security, are now actively monitoring and categorizing opposition to artificial intelligence and technology as a potential domestic extremism threat, according to over 1,000 pages of internal reports obtained by WIRED. The effort introduces a new designation — "anti-tech violent extremism" — that broadly groups together a wide range of critics, protesters, and activists concerned about AI job displacement, data center expansion, and corporate power. This surveillance push aligns with Trump administration directives instructing law enforcement to target individuals holding "anti-capitalism" and "anti-American" beliefs, effectively placing legitimate political dissent under a counterterrorism umbrella. The development raises significant civil liberties concerns, as the government appears to be using national security infrastructure to monitor and potentially criminalize speech and peaceful assembly that challenges the administration's strong financial and political ties to the AI industry.

title: FBI agent explains how easy it is to ID people posting AI porn without consent
summary: The FBI arrested two men under the Take It Down Act, a new law targeting nonconsensual AI-generated sexual imagery, after investigators identified them through relatively straightforward digital detective work. Arturo Hernandez, 20, allegedly created and posted sexualized deepfakes of roughly 50 women — including people he knew personally — and was linked to the content through matching IP addresses, PayPal records, and a saved Instagram photo he had used to generate explicit material. Cornelius Shannon, 51, allegedly published even more content targeting approximately 90 women, and was identified in part because he used his own photo as his profile picture on the porn site. The cases highlight both the serious harm posed by AI-generated nonconsensual imagery and how easily perpetrators can be traced through basic digital footprints like account links, geolocation data, and reused usernames.

title: A reality check on the AI jobs hysteria
summary: Fears of an AI jobpocalypse aren't yet borne out by the data: US Bureau of Labor Statistics figures show unemployment is actually lower in the occupations most exposed to AI than in less-exposed ones, with no sign of workers fleeing white-collar roles for manual labor. MIT Technology Review's David Rotman notes real strain for 22-to-25-year-olds in software and other AI-exposed entry-level jobs, but cautions this may reflect a broader low-fire, low-hire market rather than AI specifically. The bigger problem, economists say, is that existing data tools can't yet show how AI is reshaping work, leaving forecasters, in Harvard economist David Deming's words, largely in the dark.
quote: "We're sort of flying blind." — David Deming, professor of economics, Harvard University

title: It’s time to address the looming crisis in entry-level work
summary: AI hasn't caused mass unemployment, but it may be quietly eroding the first rung of the career ladder: entry-level roles in software, customer support, and other AI-exposed fields are declining, and a cited Stanford study found sharp employment drops for young workers in such occupations after generative AI spread. Writing for MIT Technology Review, USC economist Georgios Petropoulos argues that because these jobs are where new workers build skills and judgment, their disappearance poses a long-term risk. He calls for paid co-ops and apprenticeships in schools, plus targeted tax credits, wage subsidies, and training grants to push employers to hire early-career workers into structured, AI-augmented roles.
